schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Multiple readings of abandoned places: governing, living and researching in foreclosed futures
schedule_event.description:
Abandonment has increasingly emerged as a key concept for understanding contemporary geographies of inequality, marginalisation and urban restructuring. While geographical scholarship has long addressed abandonment through its material manifestations—such as depopulation, housing vacancy, infrastructural decay and territorial decline—these processes have often been framed as residual outcomes of economic restructuring or demographic change. This session instead approaches abandonment as an active, selective and temporally uneven mode of governance, through which institutions govern surplus lives and disposable spaces.

Building on Agamben’s conceptualisation of abandonment as a relational condition of being simultaneously excluded from and subjected to the law (Agamben, 1998, 2017), and engaging with critical geographical debates (Pratt, 2005; Gilmore, 2007, 2022; Selmeczi, 2012; Leshem, 2017), abandonment is understood here not as absence or failure, but as an organised and normalised technique of power. This perspective foregrounds the intersection of institutional withdrawal, symbolic violence and material neglect, unfolding across long temporalities and embedding violence within everyday life (Bourdieu, 1998; Berlant, 2007; Povinelli, 2011).

​As a second analytical layer, the session examines the lived realities of abandonment through the concept of endurance. Endurance, according to the Cambridge dictionary, is “the ability to keep doing something difficult, unpleasant, or painful for a long time” and has been used by Povinelli (2011) and feminist scholars to focus on the mundane, everyday violence without transformation (Wilkinson & Ortega-Alcázar, 2019). Rather than reading endurance as hope, resistance or resilience, this session understands it as an ethical and political condition of living through abandonment—one that makes visible both survival practices and the structural cruelty that necessitates them.

​Crucially, the session also invites reflection on how abandonment and endurance are researched. Conducting research in abandoned spaces requires a certain degree of ambiguity and “staying with the trouble” (Haraway, 2016) to do justice to the injustices without resorting to romanticising, solutions or redemption (Dawney & Jellis, 2024). The session thus invites engagement with alternative narratives, pluralities and reading for difference (Gibson-Graham, 2008) that address these themes and critically reflect on how to conduct and present research on “chronic and cruddy” (Povinelli, 2011) everyday forms of violence that persist in abandoned places.

​We welcome papers that engage (broadly) with the following questions:

  • How does abandonment operate as a multidimensional and temporally uneven mode of governance across different contexts?
  • What everyday practices sustain life within conditions of abandonment, and at what costs?
  • How do institutional, symbolic and material dimensions of abandonment intersect with experiences of endurance?
  • What methodological and ethical challenges arise when researching and writing about spaces of abandonment and foreclosed futures?
  • How do scholars write about spaces where there are no clear solutions, no redemptive futures, and no easily narratable forms of resistance? How can critical scholarship avoid reproducing territorial stigma or, conversely, romanticising suffering as innovation or resilience?

paper.title: Enduring State Abandonment: Occupation and (Self)Care among Destitute Migrants
Decrepitude and Excess in the Realtor Colony: towards a political economy of urban abandonment

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Map Room Conversations (1): Vietnam and the Visualisations of War
schedule_event.description: Maps establish conversations between spaces, bringing into dialogue locations, symbols, meaning, and people. The relation between mapping as practice and maps as objects has long been the subject of theoretical and methodological debate within and beyond the discipline of geography. In the sessions below we use the RGS-IBG map collection to continue this debate about maps and mapping through two types of conversation (which build on the first sessions of this type at the 2024 conference, see https://rgs-ibg.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/toc/10.1111/(ISSN)1475-4762.map-room-conv). The first is between two researchers who use maps from the collection to explore a shared interest from different perspectives. The second conversation will be between the researchers, who will present some of the maps they work with, and the participants in these interactive sessions. We invite you to take this opportunity for close encounters and conversations with the Society’s map collection.

paper.title: Vietnam Map (Room) Conversations
Mapping the Social Relations of the War Economy

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - 'Transport Governance for a Future' with Professor Kate Pangbourne
schedule_event.description: In this session Professor Kate Pangbourne will give a short presentation to introduce her innovative and interdisciplinary book tackling one of the 21st century’s most urgent challenges: steering transport systems toward a socially just, low-carbon future. Transport Governance for a Future is published by Edward Elgar in the TGRG series Transport, Mobilities and Spatial Change. She will introduce her CATI Transition Momentum Framework, designed to identify leverage points for accelerating change through systematic analysis of continuities, alignments, technologies and implementations. She will explain how her empirical work on transport governance structures and processes has informed its design. She will call academics and practitioners to action that consciously and collaboratively integrates diverse perspectives and knowledges across a range of fields not currently central to transport, so that modernising transport governance is fit for a world in polycrisis. Transport Governance for a Future is an essential resource for students and academics in transport studies and human geography, and has important insights for those in economics, politics and public policy, public administration and management and environmental studies. It is also a useful guide for practitioners and policymakers involved in global transport governance. Professor Karen Lucas will then chair an invited panel of 2/3 academics to critically engage with the book (who will have had an advanced electronic pre-print) and with Kate. Karen and each panellist will give a 5 minute response to the book before more general discussion and Q&A.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Hormonal Geographies: Tracing the Endocrine Through the Anthropocene (1)
schedule_event.description:
Session Aim: This session seeks to bring together research on hormones and endocrine-disrupting chemicals to explore how understanding the endocrine system as co-modulated by both internal and environmental factors can inform responses to contemporary forms of hormonal governance and (dis)regulation.

In recent years, hormones have emerged as an important theme in the social sciences and humanities for their role in shaping health, reproduction, and gender, with particular attention to the ways hormones are sensed and negotiated in everyday life. Work on contraceptives, fertility, menopause, pregnancy, the uterus, polycystic ovary syndrome, and low testosterone in cismen, alongside critiques of hormonal gatekeeping in trans* communities and non-consensual hormonal interventions on people with intersex conditions, has shown how hormones function as tools of biopolitical governance, enforcing heterosexual reproductive futurity while pathologizing deviations from binary sex and gender norms (Appleton, 2022; Berg and Linander, 2024; Erikainen et al., 2025; Joanes, 2023; Wang, 2024).

Meanwhile, attention across multiple research strands has been growing to the consequences of exposure to 'endocrine-disrupting chemicals' - substances largely originating from industrial sources that interfere with the body's hormonal system. As many of these compounds are xenoestrogens (synthetic estrogens), queer and trans* scholars have explored how such exposures might 'queer' sexed and gendered embodiments (Ah-King and Hayward, 2014; Di Chiro, 2010; Zabalegui, 2024), while environmental justice perspectives have highlighted the unequal health burdens of toxicity among those communities living near industrial sites (Shadaan and Murphy, 2020).

This session seeks to bring research on hormones and endocrine disruption into conversation to highlight how they co-modulate the endocrine system, both through biochemical processes and as biopolitical regimes that manage exposure, regulate populations, and produce uneven conditions of harm and resistance. This convergence underscores urgent political stakes: in the Anthropocene, industrial, ecological, and socio-political transformations have created conditions where bodies are increasingly disciplined through biomedical intervention, access to gender-affirming care is restricted, while atmospheric exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals remains largely mitigated. At this critical moment, this session brings together diverse perspectives on hormones and endocrine disrupting chemicals to explore pathways towards more just and sustainable endocrine futures.

We invite theoretical, empirical, methodological, and ethical contributions focusing on hormones, endocrine disruption, and the endocrine system from feminist, queer, and trans perspectives, as well as from health geography and environmental justice approaches.

Potential topics may include, but are not limited to:

  • The medicalisation and regulation of hormones
  • Gendered and life-course experiences of hormones
  • Hormonal health inequalities and endocrine health
  • Queer and Trans* hormonal experiences and practices
  • Fertility, reproduction, and contraception
  • Embodied and sensory experiences of hormones
  • Sexuality and sexual practices
  • Endocrine-disrupting chemicals
  • Environmental justice approaches to hormonally active pollution

paper.title: Endocrine Disruption in Teesside, UK: Towards an Endocrine Ecologies Framework
Toxic Geographies of Reproduction: Endocrine Disrupting Pesticides and Reproductive Futures in Chile
Infrastructures of access: tracing hormonal pharmaceutical geographies
The market for (the crisis in) masculinity: testosterone production and markets in the United States and Canada

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Grounding nuclear imaginaries: Placing geographies of nuclear ‘progress’, protest, and care (1)
schedule_event.description:
The nuclear continues to preoccupy our minds. As energy, weapon, mineral, medicine, and waste, nuclear issues and the social imaginaries that underpin them continue to exert a shaping force on global and local affairs. This session invites scholars and practitioners to attend to these contemporary imaginaries: their modes of production, internal logics and inconsistencies, the possibilities they enable or foreclose, and the forms of power that they underpin. 

Two questions are of particular interest. The first is the extent to which contemporary nuclear imaginaries are tied to or inherit from ideas, knowledges, and aesthetics of the past. Are these novel formations, or do they bear the traces and traumas (Schwab, 2020) of bygone periods like the Cold War (Faux, 2024)? To what extent are efforts to renew the nuclear (for example the efforts to brand civil nuclear power as ‘green’) an effective strategy in re-ontologising it? At the heart of this is a consideration of the nature of nuclear imaginaries as a potent mode of reproducing historical patterns (and violences) as well as a new set of tools to grasp the specificity of the present. The second centres the relationship between nuclear imaginaries and place. In the last two decades, important ethnographic and historical research have sought to articulate various ‘local’ nuclear imaginaries tied to particular lands, nation-states, governments, and scientific organisations: for example, an American post-Cold War nuclear imaginary (Masco, 2008), British nuclear cultures (Hogg, 2016; Hogg and Brown, 2019; Hughes, 2012), and civic epistemologies of the Indian elite classes (Anderson, 2010; Haines, 2019). Yet, outside of this corpus, scholars note a tendency in popular imaginaries of the nuclear to untether from place. The historian Lisa Yoneyama (1999, 2023) calls this tendency nuclear universalism, where the abstraction of nuclear imaginaries – especially into global imaginaries by way of an aesthetics of apocalypse – obscures the structural violence inflicted upon specific communities and places erased by these imaginaries (Hecht, 2012; Nixon, 2011; Voyles 2015). The disembedding of imaginaries from place has an obscuring function; it is only by concealing the long-term material and ecological burdens of nuclear mining, processing, and waste storage that its inherent promises (e.g. of cheap and endless energy) can be upheld (Pottin, 2024). These are nuclear imaginaries that ironically inhibit imagination itself, making it hard to think otherwise (Deckard, 2022) and posing a challenge to forms of anti-nuclear resistance (Faison and Fields, 2024; Urwin 2025). 

Taken together, these two questions guide what promises to be a lively exchange on the forms and functions of nuclear imaginaries in yet another supposed ‘nuclear renaissance’. The session turns on the central provocation that it ought to matter where thought takes place and how thought makes place. Only by attending to the worlding capacities of imaginaries might we be able to consider ways to shift them (Carpenter, 2016). We invite papers that consider the recursive relationships between nuclear imaginaries and nuclear places.

paper.title: Uneven Geographies of the Nuclear Dream: National Imaginaries and Place-Based Transformation in Akkuyu, Türkiye
Between Nature, Nurture and Nuclear War: Risk Rationalities in the Scandinavian Women for Peace Movement (1980-1989)
Progress, Transition, Generation: Grand Narratives and Imaginaries of the Hinkley Point C Nuclear Power Plant
Care Contested: Living (well) with Energy Transitions at Sellafield

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Re-thinking (critical) digital geopolitics (1)
schedule_event.description:
Whereas connections between the ‘digital’ and the ‘geopolitical’ appear self-evident today, the connections between ‘critical geopolitics’ and ‘digital geographies’ are perhaps less evident. Conceptual and empirical studies of ‘digital geopolitics’ have largely taken place beyond the confines of an (admittedly porous) body of work named ‘critical geopolitics’ and more readily found in fields like urban geography and ‘digital geographies’ (see for, example: Amoore, 2024; Amoore & de Goede, 2021; Jackman, 2024; O’Hara & Hall 2018; Maalsen, 2025). Re-visiting early writing in critical geopolitics (see O Tuathail and Dalby, 1998: 2 on ‘techno-territorial formations’) highlight that questions about the relationship between technology and geopolitics were very much a core concern. But what might a critical engagement between the fields of critical geopolitics and digital geopolitics look like in the contemporary moment? We are interested in both: the ways ‘digital geopolitics’ shows up across the disciplinary landscape; and: how ‘the digital’ and ‘the geopolitical’ (both broadly conceived) intersect and are entangled. Reflecting on the four theses on geopolitics proposed by Agnew and OTuathail (1992), the session addresses the gamut of ways digital geopolitics is being 're-thought', but especially those which speak to (some of) the following:

  1. The proliferation, but also fracturing, of the performative discourses which construct the foundations of the geopolitical stage, especially through digital cultures;
  2. The changing scope and participation of 'practical' geopolitical reasoning via digital media;
  3. The broadening of geographical knowledge production and shifts from ‘traditional’ arbiters to technology corporations and digital tools such as AI;
  4. The redefining and rescripting of ‘rule making’ and ‘rule following’ through digital media, and military and strategic forms of geo-power aided by developing technologies.

Amoore, L. (2024) The Deep Border, Political Geography 109: 102547 

Amoore, L. & de Goede, M. (2023) “Open Access: Datawars: reflections twenty years after 9/11”, in: Jackson et al. eds. 9/11 Twenty Years On, Routledge, London: pp. 29-33. 

Jackman, A. (2024) AI urbanism and feminist geopolitics: making space for diverse practices, actors and agencies. Urban Geography 45(7): 1292–1296. 

O’Hara K. & Hall, W. (2018) Four Internets. The Geopolitics of Digital Governance. CIGI Papers No. 206 — December 2018. 

Maalsen, S. (2025) Digital geographies II: All about AI. What’s old? What’s new? Where to?, Progress in Human Geography OnlineFirst.

paper.title: Popular geopolitics in the (un)making of diasporas online
Urban AI and deep borders: reimagining Hebron
Post-truth and digital geopolitics
“Horizontal Connections”: Housing Platforms as Sites of Everyday Geopolitics

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Inclusive Field Teaching
schedule_event.description: Fieldtrips, and specifically residential fieldtrips, are a fundamental component of geographical teaching and learning. The Royal Geographical Society describe fieldwork as the ‘jewel in the crown of geography’. Fieldtrips at undergraduate level are core learning spaces developing analytical research skills, real-world experiential learning, observation-based knowledge generation, as well as teamwork and cohort building opportunities (Herrick, 2010). Market driven trends have seen the rise in seemingly ‘exotic’ long-haul destinations (McGuinness and Simm, 2005), yet this has been tempered within Geography departments by discussions about the value (both in learning and financial terms) of such trips, and concerns around risk and risk-taking for students and staff (Herrick, 2010). More recently broader debates around decolonising both higher education, and geography specifically, have permeated discussions around the geography fieldtrip, particularly where fieldtrips involve travel to the Global South (Staddon et al., 2021; Noxolo, 2017). It is therefore imperative that both the teaching and educational experience provided by fieldtrips is diverse and inclusive for both students and staff.

paper.title: Flattening Scale in the Field: Rethinking Hierarchy Through Undergraduate Field Teaching
The geographical memory/-ies of a Geography Education fieldtrip, southern Drakensberg, c. 2008: teaching and learning opportunities and challenges.
Rethinking teaching in the field: inclusion and building resilience through experience

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Uneven Geographies of Tourism: The Parkification, Landscape Political Geology/ Landscape Political Ecology and Culture Nexus
schedule_event.description: 1. This session seeks contributions that explore how contemporary processes of parkification— including the designation and management of UNESCO Global Geoparks, National Parks, and other geological heritage landscapes—reconfigure regional societies through intersecting regimes of nature, governance, tourism, and cosmology (Jung, 2025). Building on emerging work in Landscape Political Geology and Landscape Political Ecology and critical geotourism studies, the session invites contributions that examine how geological or ecological formations become politicised, commodified, or sacralised through park designation, and how these transformations articulate with local livelihoods, spiritual ecologies, sustainable tourism, and regional governance. We welcome interdisciplinary, comparative, and international case studies from diverse geological heritage regions—including but not limited to Southeast Asia, East Asia, Europe, Africa, and the Americas.

paper.title: When Heritage Becomes Political Infrastructure: Post-Disaster Parkification at Mount Rinjani, Indonesia
National parks: Local, national, or international legitimacy?
Correction factors’ impact on physical and social carrying capacity: A longitudinal study of Mt. Kinabalu 2015-2025

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Just parks? Making urban green spaces more accessible and inclusive (1)
schedule_event.description: Urban green spaces are widely acknowledged as critically important amenities that provide a range of social and ecological benefits. But the provision, scale and quality of these spaces tends to vary considerably between and within different cities. Even in cities where there is adequate provision, green spaces are not necessarily welcoming, safe or usable for many disadvantaged or marginalised groups. The Landscape Geography Working Group and the Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group invite papers that address the accessibility and inclusivity of urban green spaces. This co-sponsored session aims to bring together academics working in disparate fields, including leisure studies, urban planning, landscape architecture, urban sociology, political ecology, and health studies, and various strands of geography. The title of the session ‘just parks?’ emphasises that provision tends to be unjust but also suggests that part of the solution may lie in directing attention beyond conventional green spaces. Papers might address the obstacles that restrict access to urban green spaces including physical, financial, administrative and symbolic barriers. However, the session is framed positively, and we also want to include papers that suggest how progressive change may be achieved. We have used the term ‘making’ in the subtitle because we want to explore how urban green spaces might be designed, managed or programmed differently to make them more accessible/inclusive. The co-convenors have worked together after presenting at previous RGS-IBG Conferences and we aim to provide a forum through which future collaborations might emerge.

paper.title: Designing Tower Hamlets’ Green Grid: reshaping urban green spaces through gender inclusive design
From playing fields to a sporting hub – a template for inclusive greenspace?
“I personally feel unhappy about it, because ** was a happy place”. Children’s visual reflections on a redesigned urban park
‘They just want to have a safe space to express themselves’: a park design typology to promote adolescent inclusion
Gendered leisure in Iran and the politics of segregation: Justice or state power? The case of women-only parks

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Urban Heat: Thermopolitics in More-Than-Human Cities (1)
schedule_event.description:
Extreme heat is rapidly emerging as one of the twenty-first century’s most pressing urban challenges, with profound implications for health, livelihoods, infrastructures, and everyday life. Far from affecting all urban inhabitants equally, urban heat intersects with entrenched inequalities in housing quality, energy access, infrastructural maintenance, and environmental conditions. As temperatures rise, the capacities of different urban populations to cope with, survive, and recover from heat differ starkly. Many policy and scholarly responses frame urban heat as a technical, environmental, or public health problem, while tending to overlook the highly political relations through which thermal vulnerability is produced, or indeed how power and governance operate through thermal relations.

This panel seeks to develop a relational, more-than-human approach to urban thermopolitics. Where an emerging body of geographical work on urban heat has begun to acknowledge the importance of infrastructures and urban form in generating and distributing thermal discomfort, there has been only limited engagement with the more-than-human politics of heat. Connecting insights from more-than-human geographies, urban political ecology, and STS, the panel seeks to extend human-centred accounts of thermal inequalities by including not only infrastructures, technologies, and other forms of inanimate matter, but also sentient, living other-than-human beings. From energy grids and building materials to plants, animals, and microbes, we seek to understand the more-than-human relations that shape and are shaped by urban heat and its attendant inequalities.

The panel invites contributions that examine how uneven thermal experiences and vulnerabilities emerge through interactions among people, infrastructures, technologies, animals, plants, and other-than-human actants, and how these relations shape who and what is rendered vulnerable, protected, or expendable in overheated cities. Empirically, we welcome papers that engage with a range of thematic concerns across diverse urban contexts; these could include addressing, for instance, one or more of the following questions:

  • How are thermal inequalities produced through more-than-human relations in urban environments? How do interactions between non-human elements—such as buildings, materials, energy systems, vegetation, animals, and atmospheric conditions—and human bodies shape urban thermal environments, and how do these interactions unevenly distribute heat exposure, vulnerability, and comfort?
  • What everyday more-than-human caring practices emerge in overheated cities? How do residents, community organizations, and informal networks negotiate heat through situated practices of care, repair, and adaptation, and how do these practices mobilize non-human actors such as materials, plants, and technologies?
  • How is extreme heat framed in urban planning and policy? How do dominant approaches conceptualise heat as a risk, resource, or technical problem, and what human or other-than-human forms of agency, knowledge, and responsibility are foregrounded (or conversely, obscured) in these framings?
  • Which alternative methodologies enable the study and advancement of thermal justice? Which innovative methods can be used to study extreme heat as a relational, more-than-human urban process, and how do these approaches contest dominant ways of knowing heat while enabling alternative imaginaries and practices of thermal justice?

paper.title: The Thermopolitics of Infrastructural Precarity: A Methodological Proposition Toward Thermal Justice
How an Urban Poor Community Faces the Heat: Insights from India
Housing as an infrasture of care under extreme heat

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Heat-stress in and Beyond Humans: Critical Planetary Health Perspectives (1)
schedule_event.description:
Over 2.4 billion workers worldwide are exposed to excessive heat each year, resulting in nearly 19,000 deaths and tens of millions of occupational injuries and illnesses (ILO, 2025). Heat also affects more-than-human life, including the deaths of over 650,000 farm animals during the 2021 Pacific Northwest heatwave and thousands of chickens in India’s 2015 extreme heat events (Huber, 2022). These patterns position heat not simply as environmental exposure but as a lived health condition produced through intertwined climatic, ecological, and socio-economic transformations that unevenly distribute vulnerability across human and other-than-human life.

This panel foregrounds heat as an embodied and relational health experience. Rather than treating climate change as a distant environmental driver, we approach heat as something encountered through working bodies, homes, infrastructures, care practices, and everyday survival strategies. Heat reshapes hydration, fatigue, sleep, cognition, pregnancy outcomes, and chronic illness, while also altering patterns of disease transmission, microbial activity, and multispecies cohabitation. Health, in this sense, emerges not from isolated bodies but from entangled environments.

We place these dynamics in conversation with EcoHealth, One Health, and Planetary Health frameworks to examine how well-being and care are (re)produced across human and more-than-human relations (Cole & Deshpande, 2019; Hoogeveen et al., 2023). While these frameworks emphasize interconnected systems, lived experiences of heat reveal uneven burdens shaped by labour precarity, housing conditions, access to care, and social marginalisation. Occupational exposure often restricts possibilities for rest and recovery, making heat a workplace health and safety issue as well as a broader well-being concern (Kjellstrom et al., 2019).

At the same time, responses to the climate–health nexus frequently remain technocratic and depoliticised, privileging metrics and thresholds over lived experience (Sim & Parsons, 2025). We instead consider heat harms as ethical and relational conditions shaping care, responsibility, and vulnerability across species (Celermajer et al., 2025). This perspective foregrounds everyday coping practices, cultural understandings of heat, and community-based adaptations as central to health.

This panel invites papers that ask questions such as (but not limited to):

  • How is heat experienced as an embodied condition across human and more-than-human bodies in specific places and environments?
  • In what ways does heat and its related indicators reorganise labouring conditions and
    occupational health for working bodies, including vulnerable human labour groups and more-than-human workers such as livestock and working animals, particularly where exposure cannot easily be avoided?
  • What critical, methodological, or interdisciplinary interventions are needed to move beyond technocratic or Eurocentric framings of heat and health, thus moving towards multispecies just futures?

As an extended and exploratory conversation, this panel aims to bring together scholars working across environmental, health, labour, and more-than-human geographies to think collectively about heat and its related forms as an embodied, relational, and multispecies condition, and to explore how more just planetary futures might be imagined under conditions of climate change.

paper.title: From Pleasant Weather to Planetary Risk Rethinking Heat in Temperate Contexts
Heat-stress in more-than-human labouring bodies: A case study from Cambodia
Technologies for and beyond
Affective Thermographies: Visualizing Embodied Heat Beyond Thermal Imaging in the Brazilian Amazon

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Contested depths: Subsurface geographies of the energy transition (1)
schedule_event.description:
The subsurface is an increasingly contested terrain. From geothermal energy extraction to carbon storage, heat networks to critical mineral mining, competing claims over what lies beneath our feet and impacts on the landscapes, heritage sites and natural environments above raise fundamental questions of access, ownership, and environmental justice.

Subsurface governance structures can reflect and reproduce existing power asymmetries. Property rights frameworks may privilege surface landowners or corporate extractors whilst marginalising communities who bear environmental risks. Regulatory regimes designed for fossil capitalism struggle to accommodate renewable subsurface uses like geothermal energy or seasonal thermal storage. Routine planning and regulatory decisions can foreclose future subsurface options before they are ever considered, locking in incompatible surface development and uneven transition pathways. Indigenous communities worldwide contest colonial appropriation of subsurface resources and assert sovereignty over traditional territories extending below ground.

Whilst energy transitions extend across horizontal and vertical space, subsurface resources are often rendered invisible within planning systems, regulatory frameworks and public debate, even as they become central to decarbonisation strategies. This mismatch between an emerging net-zero subterranean energy regime and largely surface-oriented governance arrangements creates new sites of conflict, exclusion and lock-in.

This session examines how subsurface allocation, regulation and contestation intersect with spatial inequalities across multiple dimensions: distributional (who benefits and who bears costs), procedural (who participates in governance), epistemic (whose knowledge counts) and scalar (coordination across regulatory jurisdictions). We're particularly interested in cases where governance fragmentation between surface and subsurface systems creates barriers to just energy transitions.

We welcome theoretical, empirical and practitioner perspectives which explore how subsurface geographies connect to questions of spatial inequality, environmental justice and pathways toward more just and equitable energy and resource transitions, with particular attention to how planning, policy and governance arrangements shape what subsurface energy transition futures become possible in practice.

paper.title: Deeper underground: On the speculative politics of critical mineral production from geothermal resources
More-than-human geographies of (sub-)terranean transformation: Contested hydropolitical spaces of contamination in and around Bitterfeld-Wolfen, Eastern Germany

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Voluminous Futures (1) Conceptualising futurity through geographies of volume
schedule_event.description:
Amidst intensifying climate volatility, geopolitical contestation, and rapid technological acceleration, volume––across air, ice, water, and underground strata––have become critical terrains through which actors imagine, contest, and enact futures. Far from empty or inert, these environments are materially complex, unevenly known, and often only partially visible. Their indeterminacy not only complicates existing regimes of knowledge and power, but also makes them particularly potent for imagining and governing uncertain futures that exceed conventional planning or forecasting. Uncertainty in voluminous environments is, therefore, more than something to be pre-emptively managed and contained: it is a generative condition that enables multiple modes of future-making.

Foregrounding the imagined futures of volume provides an opportunity to analyse how these spatialities open up particular forms of futurity whilst foreclosing others, and to interrogate the vocabularies––anticipation, speculation, pre-emption, projection––through which they are described and enacted. From the opening of Arctic passages, the remaking of oceanic territories through subterranean and undersea energy, data, and telecommunications infrastructures, to the expansion of aerial logistics and drone corridors, imaginaries of volume are reorganising territorial claims, political authority, resource frontiers, and forms of governance. These projected futures also shape everyday subjectivities, affective orientations, multi-species and non-human worlds.

This Voluminous Futures session aims to initiate a dialogue between literature on volume and future(s) through two time slots. In this 100-minute session, the first hour will see four paper presentations, followed by a more in-depth discussion about the ideas raised in these papers amongst the presenters and audience, led by the convenors.

Focusing on underground and aerial volumes, this session has gathered fascinating and cutting-edge contributions, from discussions of airspace futures in DJI drone discourse and the politics of breathing in high altitudes of the Tibetan plateau, to the mapping of the underground in historical Northeast India and Upper Burma and the extraction of rare earths in contemporary Laos.

paper.title: Engineering the Future Vertically: DJI Drones and the Urban Atmosphere of Speed and Terror
Mapping the Underground: Volumetric Geology in Northeast India and Upper Burma, 1850–1900
Indeterminate Strata: Rare Earths Extraction and Politics of Future in Lao Uplands
Thin Air: Exploring the Altitudinal Politics of Breathing

The selected contributions are indeed presenting empirical cases as varied as the tracing of the recent evolution of future-making in the depth of the Pacific Ocean, to the disruption of Euro-modern sovereignty by the employment of modern low-orbit satellites sensing, measuring and assessing states territories. Others are discussing the futures of disaster capitalism and their imaginations of varied volumes, both on and off-Earth.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Concealment: Temporary Urban Barriers of Masking, Covering, and Hiding
schedule_event.description:
To conceal: not allow to be seen; hide.

This panel focuses on barriers as affective infrastructural tools (Bosworth, 2023; Henderson, 2008) that physically, visually, and behaviorally establish a temporary relation of masking, covering, or hiding space in the city. Through varying materialities, opacities, and scales, barriers impose a vertical limit that redirects movement, interrupts sightlines, and reshapes everyday spatial politics.

Temporary barriers have their own social functions (Brighenti 2019). Some are heavy and overtly securitised, such as Iraq’s concrete post-blast barriers (Murrani, 2016), Beirut’s Walls of Shame (Ghazal, 2024), or Turkey’s protest barricades (Ertür, 2016). Others are lighter, yet operate through visual forms of concealment and display, such as construction hoardings (Székely, 2019), disaster fencing, or scaffold nets. Across these forms, such structures generate what this panel terms a “geography of concealment”. They obscure political tensions, suspend access, and conceal future uncertainties while simultaneously regulating perception: determining what may be seen, remembered, and contested. These same barriers are also appropriated to create counter-visualities (Mirzoeff 2011), thereby subverting power dynamics in everyday urban spaces.

Across contexts shaped by crisis, securitisation, and speculative development (Jones, Robinson & Turner, 2012), temporary barriers increasingly operate as management devices of physical and visual concealment, or architectures of hiding (Abughannam et al., 2024). Concealment as such is not a neutral act but a political one that mediates power dynamics and contributes to the production of urban affects of fear, anticipation, and secrecy, as well as branding and aspirational images of urban development. This panel asks:

  • In what ways do barriers function as tools for concealment, protection, or spatial control?
  • How are barriers appropriated, contested, or reconfigured by residents, activists, or artists?
  • How do hoardings and similar devices contribute to shaping urban imaginaries and projected futures?
  • What is the meaning of “temporary” in these contexts — as a short-term condition, an ongoing state, or a strategic designation?

The panel invites contributions from researchers, artists, architects, and policymakers to examine the role of concealment in shaping urban visual cultures, atmospheres, and politics. We welcome contributions from disciplines such as urban and legal geography; cultural policy; architecture; sociology; urban planning and design; and communication, media, and semiotics studies.

Possible themes for 15-20min presentations include:

  • Concealment as a form of spatial and visual governance
  • Barricading, fencing, and boarding up as strategies of securitisation
  • Materials of concealment: plywood, railing, fencing, netting, vinyl, and concrete
  • Construction screens, building wraps, and scaffolding nets as media surfaces
  • Protest, occupation, and the tactical reworking of barriers
  • Hoardings, branding, and speculative urban futures
  • Concealment and heritage destruction

paper.title: Criminal Spaces and the ‘apparatus of filth’; lewd acts in the Alpha City, seen through Bataille’s Eye
Settler Colonial Barriers: Access, “Protection”, and the Politics of Control at The Al Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem
Beirut’s Security Architecture,  Stories of Control and Resistance Inscribed in Concrete
Business As Usual - Defensive Architectures during Hong Kong’s social unrest

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Wasteful geographies of (un-)building: tracing hidden earthly flows
schedule_event.description:
The built environment produces enormous volumes of waste amid an intensifying climate crisis, extractive urbanisation, and contested sustainability transitions. Building on work that foregrounds waste as a materially and politically charged condition of contemporary capitalism (Armiero, 2020; Lepawsky, 2018; Liboiron and Lepawsky, 2022; Moore, 2023; Pyyhtinen et al., 2023; Shapiro & McNeish, 2021;), this session turns to the earthly and mineral materialities that flow through construction and demolition terrains: excavated soils, buried rubble, concrete, sand, gravel, and other matter often considered waste or surplus. While there are extensive reflections on the role of waste for society in general, the mineral materialities of construction are mostly absent in these debates. Moreover, governing entities and (de-)construction companies treat these materialities as stable and inert, enabling economic exploitation through discourses of efficiency and utility.

In this session, we break with this misconception: to the contrary, these materialities move, leak, and persist (Hird, 2013). Surplus and waste soils and rocks travel across sites, borders, classifications, and value regimes, at times framed as resources, at others as hazardous contamination (Meulemans, 2022; Wildeboer & Savini, 2023). In doing so, they trouble tidy narratives of circularity, efficiency, and environmental protection. In this context, what is considered waste, where it is sent, and who must live with its consequences are all deeply political questions. Construction waste management does more than simply manage debris; it redistributes harm, reconfigures land use, and entrenches unequal geographies of exposure and exclusion, often in ways that are difficult to see, map, or measure. And still, much of this process happens out of view, underground, or buried in bureaucratic and scientific classifications (Balayannis, 2020; Greeson et al., 2020).

Our interest in the residual materials of current building practices sits alongside a growing body of work that calls for a radical rethinking of construction itself, from proposals for moratoria on new building and value shifts toward existing stocks (Malterre-Barthes, 2025) to architectural practices rooted in sufficiency, care and repair, and experiment with less extractive urban futures (Hertweck et al., 2023). These interventions challenge dominant types of governance, which are often still associated with the idea of endless building and economic growth, and acknowledge the inherited and ongoing damage of these governance practices.

We invite papers that critically examine how flows of earthly wastes of the built environment contribute to the making of unjust places, for example, through the displacement of environmental risks, the marginalisation of already vulnerable communities, or the erasure of more-than-human worlds. We are especially interested in research that attends to what is made invisible and unmeasurable: forms of harm that resist quantification, or that are actively obscured in dominant planning, policy, and governance frameworks.

Format: Oral presentations (15 minutes each + discussion)

Convenors:

Simona Popova, University of Luxembourg

Delphine Rumo, Aalto University, Finland

Michael Wittmann, University of Freiburg, Germany

paper.title: Inherited Special Deals. Extended Networks of Material Deposition as Architectures of Cross- System Infiltration in Divided Germany

paper_authors.identity_string: Ecole Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne - EPFL

paper.title: Hidden Debris: EndofLife Ships as Demolition Waste
Garbic Soils, Gases and EPA Audits: The Human-Made, Mobile Geologies of the Sunshine Landfill
Wasteful (de-)construction geographies and hidden earthly flows

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Uneven and Contested Geographies of Data Centers (1)
schedule_event.description:
In recent years, data centres have become an increasingly common subject of public and political debate (Edwards, 2024). The focus and criticism in these debates are usually directed at the massive consumption of natural resources (Brodie, 2024; Hogan, 2015), their effect on local communities (Bridges, 2024), and the concentration of geopolitical power – especially with regards to large data centers that are currently being planned or built in the context of the hype surrounding artificial intelligence. As essential infrastructure for living in our increasingly datafied worlds and for organising digital capitalism, data centres also raise a variety of geographical questions and problems. These range from the conflict-ridden embedding of data centres in local environments (Rone, 2024), to their role in larger infrastructure constellations (Atkins, 2021; Velkova, 2016), urban settings, and geopolitical issues such as digital sovereignty, digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019) and data localisation (Rosa, 2022). This session brings together conceptual and empirical contributions to critical data centre studies and research on the geographies of digital infrastructures. Against this backdrop, our session brings together contributions from researchers in digital geography, political geography, urban studies, political ecology and science and technology studies, examining the socio-technical imaginations, material effects and geopolitical and infrastructural embeddings of data centres. The papers engage with topics such as data centers in the Global South / Global Majority regions, contested infrastructuralization of data centers in rural and urban areas, the embedding of data centers in geopolitical strategies, programs, and conflicts, sociotechnical imaginations of AI data centers and social movements mobilizing against the data centers.

Literature
Atkins, E. (2021). Tracing the ‘cloud’: Emergent political geographies of global data centres. Political Geography, 86.

Brodie, P. (2024). Smarter, greener extractivism: Digital infrastructures and the harnessing of new resources. Information, Communication & Society, 1–20.

Bridges, L. E. (2024). Competing digital capacities: Between state-led digital governance and local data center tradeoffs. Information, Communication & Society, 27(10).

Edwards, D., Cooper, Z. G. T., & Hogan, M. (2024). The making of critical data center studies. Convergence. 

Hogan, M. (2015). Data flows and water woes: The Utah Data Center. Big Data & Society, 2(2).

Kwet, M. (2022). Digital colonialism and Infrastructure as dept. Frontiers in African Digital Research, 65–77.Rone, J. (2024). The shape of the cloud: Contesting date centre construction in North Holland. New Media & Society, 26(10)

Rosa, F. R. (2022). Code Ethnography and the Materiality of Power in Internet Governance. Qualitative Sociology, 45(3), 433–455.

Velkova, J. (2016). Data that warms: Waste heat, infrastructural convergence and the computation trafficcommodity. Big Data & Society, 3(2).

paper.title: Geographies of Anticipation: AI Growth Zones, Data Centres and Post-Industrial Futures in North East England
Misaligned Imaginaries: Industry, Government, and Community Visions of Australia’s Data Centre Futures

Institute for Sustainable Futures, University of Technology Sydney

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Uneven and Contested Geographies of Data Centers (3)
schedule_event.description:
In recent years, data centres have become an increasingly common subject of public and political debate (Edwards, 2024). The focus and criticism in these debates are usually directed at the massive consumption of natural resources (Brodie, 2024; Hogan, 2015), their effect on local communities (Bridges, 2024), and the concentration of geopolitical power – especially with regards to large data centers that are currently being planned or built in the context of the hype surrounding artificial intelligence. As essential infrastructure for living in our increasingly datafied worlds and for organising digital capitalism, data centres also raise a variety of geographical questions and problems. These range from the conflict-ridden embedding of data centres in local environments (Rone, 2024), to their role in larger infrastructure constellations (Atkins, 2021; Velkova, 2016), urban settings, and geopolitical issues such as digital sovereignty, digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019) and data localisation (Rosa, 2022). This session brings together conceptual and empirical contributions to critical data centre studies and research on the geographies of digital infrastructures. Against this backdrop, our session brings together contributions from researchers in digital geography, political geography, urban studies, political ecology and science and technology studies, examining the socio-technical imaginations, material effects and geopolitical and infrastructural embeddings of data centres. The papers engage with topics such as data centers in the Global South / Global Majority regions, contested infrastructuralization of data centers in rural and urban areas, the embedding of data centers in geopolitical strategies, programs, and conflicts, sociotechnical imaginations of AI data centers and social movements mobilizing against the data centers.

Literature

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Energy Transition and Social Justice: Geographies in the Climate Emergency
schedule_event.description: Amid the global drive toward low-carbon energy transition under the climate emergency, the spatial unevenness of transition processes and their intrinsic entanglement with energy transition justice have emerged as critical concerns. As the inescapable impacts of climate change and energy challenges continue to intensify, it is imperative to infuse the disciplinary positioning of energy geography with new connotations centered on justice and spatiality. Existing studies often treat energy transition and climate justice as disjointed domains, neglecting their inherent geographical interconnections, including but not limited to how spatial disparities in energy infrastructure deployment shape the distribution of transition costs and benefits, and how local geographical contexts mediate the implementation and outcomes of just transition policies under the climate emergency contexts. This session attempts to address this gap by adopting a geographical lens to unpack the spatial dynamics underpinning energy transition justice, offering a nuanced understanding of uneven transition pathways and their justice implications. Such an inquiry is vital for formulating context-specific low-carbon policies and advancing equitable climate governance in an era of climate emergency.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Critical Climate Geographies: Unsettling the Discipline (1): Empirics
schedule_event.description:
Parsons et al. (2025) recently reminded us that “climate change is everywhere within geographical scholarship. Yet, it is not equally everywhere.” This tension sits at the heart of what it means to engage in critical climate geographies today. Climate change permeates the discipline, yet the power relations, epistemic architectures, and political imaginaries that shape it often remain unexamined or unevenly articulated. In line with the inequalities theme of this years’ RGS-IBG Annual Conference, this session takes seriously the provocation that to understand climate change and its related inequalities, we must also unsettle the discipline itself - its assumptions, its boundaries, and its inherited ways of knowing.

Building on catalytic interventions across human geography (e.g. Parsons et al. 2025; Bulkeley & McFarlane, 2024; Liverman, 2024; Sultana 2022; Mahony & Hulme 2018; Braun 2015; Anderson 2010), we foreground the diverse trajectories that have pushed climate scholarship into new conceptual and political terrain. The aforementioned articles collectively map a field in which climate epistemologies, decolonial critique, multi‑scalar governance, and speculative climate futures have become central to understanding how climate change is made and remade through power, knowledge, and lived experience.

Within this expanding geographical landscape, scholarship on climate epistemologies has interrogated the politics of knowledge production, revealing how climate expertise is constructed, circulated, and contested (Mahony and Hulme 2018; Liverman, 2024; Parsons et al. 2025). Decolonial and anti‑racist climate geographies have exposed how colonial legacies, racial capitalism, and structural inequalities underpin climate vulnerability and shape global policy imaginaries (Liverman, 2024; Parsons, 2024; Sultana 2022; Yusoff 2018; Baldwin 2016). Meanwhile, work on climate governance, security, and multi‑scalar politics has traced the entanglements of climate change with human security, adaptation, and state power (Rothe et al., 2025; Kythreotis et al., 2024; Westman, 2024; O’Brien 2012), while analyses of anticipatory and speculative climate futures highlight how imaginaries of socio‑ecological transformation shape political action and the horizons of possibility (Bulkeley & McFarlane, 2024; Cox, 2024; Braun 2015; Anderson 2010).

We invite papers that support, extend, challenge, or disrupt these trajectories. Contributions may address climate epistemologies and co‑production; decolonial, feminist, and anti‑racist approaches; digital and algorithmic climate governance; climate justice, migration, and security; embodied and affective climate geographies; urban and multi‑scalar governance; or speculative and transformative climate futures. We particularly welcome interdisciplinary, reflexive, and methodologically experimental work – ethnographic, creative, digital, speculative, or collaborative – that pushes the boundaries of what climate geography can be.

If critical climate geographies are to meet the political and epistemic demands of the present moment, they must continue to unsettle the discipline: its categories, its hierarchies, and its claims to authority. This session aims to convene that unsettling.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Intensity (1)
schedule_event.description:
Intensity appears to be a problem today. The crisis prone present seems to be characterised by too much of the wrong sort of intensity. Claims are made that subjects are too outraged or too disaffected. Whilst some are baited to outrage amid today’s digital economy, others lie flat as they feel a pernicious combination of expectation and economic downturn. Extended periods of stretched out waiting for fragile promises to materialise (Pettit 2023), coexist with hustle cultures of constant activity (Thieme 2025). Calls to foster passionate attachment to work coexist with quiet quitting (Hong 2022). Politics amid the extended end of neoliberalism and as mediated by digital capitalism is characterised as being full of uneasy mixtures of outrage and boredom. Maladies of intensity – from burnout (Han 2015) to overstimulation – appear to serve as the signature affects for a present characterised as at once too much and too little, both too intense and not intense enough. 

But in addition to being key to diagnosing the present conjuncture in all its overdetermined complexity, intensity can be found across multiple geographies. Value is created through generating intensity, whether in new digital worlds, through branding and hype, or via entertainment forms (e.g. the Blockbuster film). Capitalism has frequently been understood to involve a logic of intensification as value is extracted from life, labour and land. Political forms offer and promise intensity, whether the quietness and disaffection of liberalism or the rage infused enjoyment of right-wing populism (Anderson & Secor 2025). Forms of power work through intensity. Foucault (1977), for example, writes of how sovereignty works through ‘visible scenes of intensity’ as he details the spectacle of the scaffold. Events that are apprehended as crises are mediated through intensities that can change how they become present in everyday life. Mediated in terms of the catastrophic, climate change might be met with boredom and other disaffected feelings, for some subjects. The ‘immigration crisis’ gathers intensities around it, as authoritarian intensities are performed through mediated spectacle of violent raids targeting racialised subjects. At the same time, intensity is central to the lived experience of place. Whether the buzz of a city, the density of a crowd (McFarlane 2016) or stillness of a landscape, particular kinds of good intensity are governed, desired, valued, and experienced. This set of sessions explores the cultures, politics and economies of intensity.

paper.title: Official hope – the politics of feeling climate action
Climate change and the politics of disaffection
Enveloping Intensities
Geo-intensities: Living with flooding in Morpeth, UK
'Upvoting the end of the world': Enjoying Climate Change on Reddit

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Integrating Economic and Place-Based Perspectives on Peripheral Development
schedule_event.description:
Contemporary non-metropolitan and peripheral regions are navigating a complex landscape of globalisation-driven pressures, rapid technological restructuring, and demographic shifts. While traditional models emphasise the primacy of urban agglomeration (Duranton & Puga, 2004), recent evidence (e.g. Pike et al., 2011; Rodríguez-Pose, 2013) indicates that many peripheral territories exhibit significant structural resilience and distinct evolutionary trajectories that challenge standard growth paradigms. An increasing number of studies and publications are focusing on innovation beyond agglomerations, and the focus on exploiting growth potential is being contested, and other types of goals as well-being (Randolph & Currid-Halkett, 2022), social and cultural values (Leick & Lang, 2018), are highlighted as important in a sustainable and resilient policy perspective (Syssner & Erlingsson, 2023).

Current research, however, remains characterised by a degree of theoretical fragmentation. Research often bifurcates into siloed analyses: focusing either on economic drivers (e.g., innovation systems, human capital, labour market dynamics) or on more place-based dimensions (e.g., institutional governance, quality of life, and social cohesion).

However, regional development processes are not just purely economistic processes, but rather a conflictual process in which different opinions or even divergent views emerge and compete through different language relationships (Calignano & Nilsen, 2024). This session, therefore, seeks to synthesise these perspectives by examining the multi-dimensional interactions between social, environmental, and economic processes. We specifically aim to explore how structural conditions intersect with localised agency, place-based identity, and spatially anchored capital to influence regional outcomes.

By moving beyond deficit-based frameworks, this session invites contributions that investigate how non-metropolitan territories leverage territorial rationality and endogenous agency to secure their long-term economic trajectories. We welcome theoretical, empirical, and methodological papers that bridge the divide between economic and place-based scholarship to provide a more holistic understanding of development in the periphery.

We welcome papers on topics such as:

- endogenous agency in regional development

- well-being as a policy goal in regional development

- the geography of 'left-behind' places

-  competing narratives between "growth-oriented" and "well-being-oriented" in regional policy

- holistic understanding of sustanable regional development

paper.title: Putting Agents in Agency: The Role of Patriotic Capital in Local and Regional Development
Beyond Growth-Led Development: The Semiconductor Industry and the Social Cost of Regionalism in Taiwan's Industrial Periphery
Firm Strategies and Regional Development in Norway
The twin transition: informed policy advice for a peripheral case

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Infrastructures of Social Reproduction and Political Subjectivation: Housing, Class, and Urban Inequalities (1)
schedule_event.description:
In the context of intersecting housing, climate, and cost-of-living crises, access to basic socio-technical infrastructures—water, energy, mobility, and telecommunication as well as housing itself—has become increasingly precarious for many. Yet these infrastructures are central for maintaining social reproduction understood as the everyday work of sustaining life and labour power (see, e.g., Gillespie and Hardy 2021; Madden 2025; Meehan 2025). In recent years, scholars have increasingly focused on the crises of social reproduction arising from precarious access to such structures – entailing exhaustion, health issues, and limited participation in wage labor as well as intensifying urban inequalities (see, e.g., Madden 2025; Protschky and Formella forthcoming; Rao 2021). Research shows that alienation and depletion of social reproduction often inhibit solidarity and political action by those affected - due to limited time, power-imbalances between tenants, landlords, unhoused people and service providers, shame for one’s own situation or fragmentation of groups affected by infrastructural precarity (Latocha 2024; Protschky 2023; Reichle and Bescherer 2021; Rinn et al. 2022). While precarious access to infrastructures of social reproduction thus effectively impedes engagement against this precarity, scholars also point to venues for enabling political action, including practical support in reproductive activities, raising class consciousness, and bridging divides between class fractions (Gehriger 2025; Uhlmann 2023).

This curated session invites contributions that take seriously the material and political consequences of infrastructural precarity for reproductive life and political subjectivation. Bringing Social Reproduction Theory into dialogue with critical housing and infrastructure studies, we ask how housing and infrastructure regimes shape the embodied, affective, and relational capacities through which people endure, comply with and resist precarious conditions while organizing everyday life.

We are particularly interested in contributions that address questions such as:

  • How do precarious housing and infrastructural arrangements reorganise the conditions under which people can sustain everyday life and reproduce labour power?
  • In what ways do exhaustion, alienation, shame, time poverty, and fragmentation inhibit solidarity and political organising—and how might they also become grounds for renewed political agency?
  • How do finance-dominated housing regimes and state regulation govern social reproduction, and with what classed, gendered, and racialised effects?
  • How can we theorise housing as an infrastructure and terrain of social reproduction—linking uneven materiality, lived experience, political governance, and struggle?
  • What practices (mutual aid, reproductive support, tenant organising, coalition-building) enable political action and bridge divides between class fractions, such as tenants and unhoused groups?

We welcome creative, conceptual, and empirical contributions, including but not limited to:

  • Housing and socio-technical infrastructures (e.g., water, energy, mobility, telecommunications) as material conditions of social reproduction
  • Financialisation, commodification, and the governance of social reproduction
  • Tenancy, eviction, and the infrastructural politics of (un)housing
  • ‘Lived’ housing crises: depletion, alienation, and everyday survival strategies
  • Political subjectivation under infrastructural precarity: demobilisation and politicisation
  • Class (re)formation through housing relations; reproductive relations and urban inequality
  • Feminist political economy perspectives on dwelling, labour power, and urban class experience
  • Organising amid crisis: care infrastructures, mutual aid, and bridging fragmented publics
  • Comparative and relational approaches across cities/regions; methodological reflections

paper.title: More than Nothing, less than Housing. Homelessness, Shelter Designs, and the Ambivalences of Minimalism
Affective Infrastructures of Housing: Navigating Economic Displacement Pressures in Vienna

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Mapping human-microbial relations (1): Complicating probiotic cultures: Imaginaries, governance, and exclusions
schedule_event.description:
Human-microbial relations have undergone a profound transformation over the past century. In the Global North, the negative consequences of Pasteurian paradigms of eradication (such as drug resistance, the rise of allergies, autoimmune diseases, or the loss of soil fertility) have prompted a ‘probiotic turn’ in cultural imaginaries that have so far been dominated by ideas of hygiene and surveillance. This trend reframes microorganisms as ‘Old Friends’ essential to human health and ecosystem function, and proposes approaching more positively and harnessing a diverse range of microbial functions and their multispecies entanglements. Processes from industrial agriculture to urban wastewater treatment highlight how our worlds intimately depend on the invisible and free labour of regulated microbial ecologies. Yet, convivial or flourishing socio-microbiomes centred on individual health tend to unfold in affluent contexts supported by antibiotic legacies and risk reproducing exclusionary logics.

Building upon the understanding of microorganisms as active world building agents that co-create, enable, and constrain different forms of social life, this panel aims to differentiate, pluralise, and expand the changing attitudes, practices, and socio-bio-ecologies that constitute the ‘microbial turn’ in their geographically and historically specific contexts. We ask what happens when (unjust, inequitable) social formations encounter recalcitrant and indeterminate microbial agencies. How are microbes enrolled into human worlds (and vice versa), what kinds of transformations unfold and what possibilities come to light? In particular, we aim to investigate the (unintended, unpredictable) political, social, and ecological implications of dis/encouraging microbial growth, and to map how situated practices with microbes create patchy geographies that variously reflect, reinforce, and resist power imbalances.


As the first of two linked sessions, 'Complicating Probiotic Cultures' interrogates 'post-Pasteurian' imaginaries across diverse techno-infrastructural and agro-ecological environments. The papers in this session demonstrate how probiotic practices facilitate novel forms of governance in which microbial lives are enrolled to produce value. By centering geographies of exclusion and accumulation, contributors challenge the convivial promise of the 'post-antibiotic' era, revealing the uneven power dynamics that underpin contemporary microbial management.

paper.title: The uneven geographies of probiotic agriculture: the political economies of plant protection
The ‘probiotic turn’ as microbial frontiers of accumulation

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Mapping human-microbial relations (2): Human-microbial encounters at the ‘margins’: Bodies, exposures, and infrastructures
schedule_event.description:
Human-microbial relations have undergone a profound transformation over the past century. In the Global North, the negative consequences of Pasteurian paradigms of eradication (such as drug resistance, the rise of allergies, autoimmune diseases, or the loss of soil fertility) have prompted a ‘probiotic turn’ in cultural imaginaries that have so far been dominated by ideas of hygiene and surveillance. This trend reframes microorganisms as ‘Old Friends’ essential to human health and ecosystem function, and proposes approaching more positively and harnessing a diverse range of microbial functions and their multispecies entanglements. Processes from industrial agriculture to urban wastewater treatment highlight how our worlds intimately depend on the invisible and free labour of regulated microbial ecologies. Yet, convivial or flourishing socio-microbiomes centred on individual health tend to unfold in affluent contexts supported by antibiotic legacies and risk reproducing exclusionary logics.

Building upon the understanding of microorganisms as active world building agents that co-create, enable, and constrain different forms of social life, this panel aims to differentiate, pluralise, and expand the changing attitudes, practices, and socio-bio-ecologies that constitute the ‘microbial turn’ in their geographically and historically specific contexts. We ask what happens when (unjust, inequitable) social formations encounter recalcitrant and indeterminate microbial agencies. How are microbes enrolled into human worlds (and vice versa), what kinds of transformations unfold and what possibilities come to light? In particular, we aim to investigate the (unintended, unpredictable) political, social, and ecological implications of dis/encouraging microbial growth, and to map how situated practices with microbes create patchy geographies that variously reflect, reinforce, and resist power imbalances.

As the second of two linked sessions, ‘Bodies, exposures, and infrastructures’ investigates human-microbial encounters at the ‘margins’. Presentations in this session focus on how uneven relations of gender, class, and race produce geographically and historically specific forms of human-microbial bio-sociality. This session frontstages intersections between microbial lives, technological infrastructures, and medical epistemologies to chart situated disturbances of the socio-microbial nexus. Here, metabolic disequilibrium and dysbiotic shifts emerge as precarious modulations of bodies and environments that defy binary categorisation.

paper.title: Avoidance or tolerance regimes of exposure: Contested environmental prescriptions to urban pollen allergies

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Engaging Everyday Spatialities: Mixed Methods for Visual, Narrative, and Designerly Approaches to Spatial Knowledge
schedule_event.description: This roundtable addresses the need for mixed methods to expand our understanding of mapping practices across disciplines. Moving beyond conventional cartographic approaches, it brings together perspectives that integrate visual techniques (such as GIS and critical cartography) with non-cartographic forms of mapping, including ethnographic and narrative practices, recognising that maps are never neutral but actively shape what—and whose—realities become visible. Our discussion will examine how these various approaches to mapping can and cannot be combined to produce rich spatial knowledge that represents everyday spatialities. How can these distinct approaches to mapping be combined to represent the positionalities of different stakeholders, hiding, revealing, and confronting what are, in fact, dynamic social and ecological relations? In what ways does mixing methods have the capacity to resolve the issue of particular communities, features, and territorialities being excluded? The roundtable will query how mixing methods can both enable and challenge researchers to combine quantitative and qualitative data to capture lived, embodied, and relational dimensions of space that are typically neglected in traditional cartography, yet are essential to just representations of everyday spatialities. Building on the collaborative work of the Leiden-Delft-Erasmus PortCityFutures Thematic Research Group “Mixed Methods and Design Strategies", the roundtable aims to foster an interdisciplinary dialogue among human geographers, anthropologists, and spatial scientists on innovative ways of multi-dimensional mapping, foregrounding questions of representation, power, ethics and responsibility in how spatial knowledge is produced.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Mapping Intersecting Inequalities through Relief Maps: Scales, Processes and Lived Experience (1)
schedule_event.description: This session brings together a set of conceptual, empirical and methodological contributions that centre on mapping intersecting inequalities through the Relief Maps model. Drawing on findings from the ERC-funded INTERMAPS project, the session examines how inequalities are produced, lived, and negotiated across different places, scales, and everyday contexts. The central focus of the session is on mapping intersectional inequalities as lived experience. Relief Maps are mobilised across all papers as a shared feminist and intersectional framework that makes it possible to translate embodied experiences of inequality into spatial forms of analysis. Rather than treating mapping as a neutral act of representation, the session understands mapping as a situated and relational process through which inequalities can become visible. In this sense, the session draws on a multidimensional and relational understanding of place that goes beyond location alone. By engaging with place as the intersection of spatial settings, everyday practices, emotional attachments, and social positions, the papers show how intersecting power relations are materialised differently across homes, urban spaces, sites of activism, environmental contexts, and more-than-human relations. This approach allows for the analysis of inequalities across multiple scales, while remaining grounded in lived experience. By foregrounding intersectional mapping grounded in lived experience, the session contributes to geographical debates on spatial justice and shows how rethinking mapping practices can support the pursuit of more just places.

paper.title: Quantitative approaches to mapping intersecting lived experiences of inequality across space
New Developments in Mapping Intersectional Inequalities with Relief Maps
Intersectional class-based housing discomforts: Domestic experiences of inequality
Feminist Approaches to GIS Data Analysis and Visualisation from. More-Than-Human Perspectives

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Mapping Inequality Critically (1)
schedule_event.description:
Critical commentators have made visible how data science, quantitative data, and modelling processes can reinforce and produce inequalities. Indeed, the ways in which knowledge is extracted, processed, and repackaged through data science in various guises, to serve institutional and corporate interests, reinforces structural inequalities. Dominant forms of mapping also have a troubled history from dispossession to colonisation of lands. Given these critiques, a question mark lingers over the suitability of quantitative mapping techniques, that anchor data-driven analysis and modelling, to evidence and address the growing spatial inequalities arising in diverse contexts and domains globally.

In response, this session aims to showcase how data science and mapping (broadly defined) approaches can be used to challenge the structures of oppression that sustain inequality, but only when orientated towards critical principles underpinning collective social relationships (e.g. accountability, democracy, justice, reflexivity and participation). In doing so, we also hope to refute the idea that geographers engaging with data science and doing quantitative work cannot also be critical.

In the session, we invite contributions that counteract assumptions that quantitative practices are neutral or universally applicable, instead emphasising the deeply subjective and contextual elements of mapping inequalities. Our aim is to capture the whole mapping process, from the concepts, theories and ideas that underpin it, to the collection, analysis, and interpretation of data, and finally to its applications and consequences. This might include contributions that:

- Engage with critical social science theories to understand inequality;

- Embrace diverse forms of knowledge beyond Western epistemologies;

- Present empirical cases that are strongly rooted in local context;

- Map inequalities using approaches or datasets that are innovative, participatory, creative or unconventional;

- Assess gaps that arise in mapping inequality, socially and spatially;

- Apply methodologies that centre those most affected by data-driven decisions.

paper.title: Mapping Urban Quality of Life in Extremis: Lived Experiences in Times of War and Genocide
Equity vs sustainability in housing policies: A Swiss case study
Beyond the standardization trap:  A critical quantitative perspective on disabilities across transit and public spaces
