schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Mobilising care (1): exploring the imaginaries, expectations, politics and qualities of care in everyday travel
schedule_event.description:
Whilst time remains the dominant currency when thinking about what matters in everyday travel, it is increasingly clear that for many journeys outside labour relations, articulations of care sit at the heart of much travel decision-making (Ivins and Spinney 2024). As a result, time savings in particular, but also many other aspects of travel such as levels of comfort and safety are prioritized in the pursuit of care, rather than cost savings. Mobility and the ways we choose to travel are therefore central to the cultures and politics of care (Kent 2025). Pushing off from the premise that mobility contributes to the accomplishment of three broad forms caring relation (1) self-care: physical/ emotional work directed towards maintaining/ enhancing our personal wellbeing; (2) familial care: activities directed toward maintaining/ enhancing the wellbeing of others; and (3) environmental care: activities directed toward maintaining/ enhancing local and natural environments (Bowlby 2012:2101; Hobson 2006), this session seeks to expand and explore debates at the intersection of mobility and care. Against this backdrop the session seeks contributions engaging with (but not limited to) a range of themes and questions including:

  • How is care accomplished through different mobilities; which mobilities (digital and physical) are seen to possess qualities that contribute to care, and which are not?
  • To what extent does care underpin judgements and decisions about how we travel, what forms of care are evident, and where (self, others, environments) and when (towards immediate or future goals) is care directed?
  • Caring expectations – what standards of care do we come to expect in relation to different mobilities, and how are these expectations shaped and ratcheted up? What role do different forms of mobility play in shaping standards of care and the qualities that are deemed important?
  • How do different scales of mobility - encompassing daily travel and global mobility shape imaginaries and accomplishments of care?
  • How do standards of care desired in mobilities differ within and across different groups in society, how do standards change over time and across regions and countries?
  • How do particular embodiments arising from gender, age, labour, ethnicity, income, class, etc shape how we perceive and accomplish care, and the forms of mobility we choose to use?
  • How do attempts to change travel behaviour towards more sustainable alternatives affect people’s ability to enact and accomplish care? What happens when care not be accomplished in ways and to standards of our choosing?
  • What politics of care arise when considering the roles, contradictions, and potential ‘carelessness’ and harms of different mobilities in caring relations?
  • How do physical and virtual mobilities intersect in the accomplishments of care?

paper.title: Mobilities of care and the economy of qualities

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - More-than-Human Friendships (1)
schedule_event.description:
The more-than-human has swept geography, the social sciences, and humanities in recent years, having come to encompass life-affirming ways of living with and theorising relationships with and networks of others. In this more-than-human turn, there has been a seemingly endless fascination with all kinds of others and the spaces and places that they, and we, co-produce. From the city to the sea, the familiar to the otherworldly, the more-than-human world has perhaps never been more considered than in contemporary “human” geography. Beyond the boundaries of geography, our moral duties to the non-human world and their rights on and to the Earth have been the concern of politics and philosophy scholars, while creative practitioners have contemplated how best to represent this more-than-human world in art, poetry, and writing.

Yet, despite this wealth of literature, we believe that there remains considerable scope for exploration, experimentation, and indeed playfulness within this ever-expanding field. Specifically, we are interested in how friendship might be useful, or at least interesting, in understanding more-than-human worlds, lives, deaths, futures, and endings. Friendship itself, although shaping everyday lives, has been relatively under-considered in geographical scholarship and even less so in relation to the more-than-human world. It is our intention with this session to explore how, if at all, friendship might shape or be shaped by the more-than-human relations and worlds.

paper.title: Befriending the Otherworlds: exploring portals of consciousness for co-creating intuitive wisdom for harmonious Earth Futures
Bouzouki, my research companion
Imaging friendship: more-than-human intra-actions in new materialist video experiments
More-than-Human Friendships

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Nature, cities, and environmental inequalities: exploring the intersections of environment, human health, and social justice (1)
schedule_event.description:
The current debate on inequalities has well acknowledged the systemic disparities that cut across health, social, environmental, and economic domains in both the Global South and the Global North (Dorling, 2023). Over the recent decades, there has been growing recognition of the interconnected nature of these systems (Ellis et al., 2025), particularly through the lenses of sustainability and ecological health (Barrett, 2022). Social and economic inequalities frequently translate into unequal access to natural resources, environmental amenities, and essential services, factors that play a critical role in shaping both physical and mental health outcomes (Bratman et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2026).

Environmental inequalities in access to nature are closely related to broader social and economic inequalities, with clear implications for health and well-being (Fratesi et al., 2024; Roberts et al., 2021). Communities facing persistent socioeconomic disadvantage are more likely to experience limited availability and lower quality of green and blue spaces, alongside higher exposure to environmental degradation and risk. These patterns are shaped by structural factors, including siloed organisation of spatial planning, land-use, and infrastructure investment decisions, which systematically privilege and marginalise different communities (Mayen Huerta et al., 2025). An intersectional and place-based analytical approach is therefore essential to investigate the mechanisms through which environmental inequalities are produced and maintained, and to inform integrated policy responses that address environmental justice and health inequalities holistically (Cohen et al., 2024).

Meanwhile, with less than four years remaining to deliver the targets of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development under the Global Biodiversity Framework, the gap between commitments and lived practice is becoming increasingly concerning (Obura et al., 2026). The narrowing policy window heightens the need for evidence-based research to inform place-sensitive interventions, particularly those addressing inequalities rooted in socioeconomic and geographic conditions. In the UK, such disparities are recognised as having implications for long-term national security (Defra, 2026).

This session seeks to further continue an interdisciplinary dialogue on the intersections of nature, human health, and equality, exploring both the challenges and opportunities. We welcome theoretical, methodological, and empirical contributions that address, but are not limited to, the following themes:

  • The role of nature-based solutions, including green and blue spaces, in reducing health inequalities
  • Innovative and community-based approaches to urban development and biodiversity integration
  • Urban planning, environmental justice, and policies promoting equitable access to nature
  • The impacts of climate change and environmental degradation on health inequalities
  • Intersectional perspectives on environment, health, and social justice
  • Digital technologies, AI, and smart-city tools in mapping and addressing health inequalities
  • Nature, care, and social infrastructure in marginalised communities

paper.title: A Dose of Nature: Building a Natural Health Service in North Yorkshire's protected landscapes
Uneven Ecologies of Transition: More-than-Human Environmental Justice on a Post-Industrial Waterfront
Mapping inequalities in the urban tree canopy cover of the UK
Planning for inclusivity in decision-making about urban green and blue spaces: a realist review of the involvement of communities

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Place-based solidarities under strain? Contested local civic spheres in unequal times (1)
schedule_event.description: There has been a recent return to ‘place-based policy’ from the UK government, exemplified in policies such as ‘Levelling Up’ and ‘Pride in Place’. Such a discourse springs from a recognition of political and resourcing failures in centralised programmes. Alongside economic development, health policy is proposing moves towards ‘neighbourhood’ and ‘community’ health whilst ambitions for net zero are increasingly premised on localised programmes. Such initiatives seek to galvanise a sphere of everyday care and civic action in the service of welfare and sustainable development, whilst also responding to the ‘geographies of discontent’ (McCann 2020) shaped by profound inequalities between regions. This shift to a local focus may offer opportunities for neglected or ‘left behind’ communities. However, inequalities and ongoing conditions of austerity have hollowed out the local and shaped unstable and contested local civic spheres. Whilst there is everyday grassroots ‘experimentation’ happening in communities, this is a highly fragmented arena often involving community, faith-based, voluntary and private sector actors rather than public authorities (Thompson and Lorne 2023). Furthermore, the grievances of localised inequality and neglect are now playing a key role in the rise of far right movements in communities (Santamarina and Ince 2025) as well as populist backlash against other progressive policy such as local climate initiatives (Yazar 2024).  This panel will present research on topics including civic action and imaginaries in local contexts; critical accounts of health initiatives premised on a ‘place-based approach’; models of solidarity and social economic experiments combining social, and climate justice; analysis of far-right organising around the local as well as localised counter-movements

paper.title: Fragile infrastructures of care and solidarity in everyday welfare at the margins
Place-Based Solidarity: Crisis, Austerity and the Devolution of Responsibility
(In)visibility, poverty and “helping out”
Who repairs what and why? Community labour after the riots
Alleyway gardens, Street WhatApp groups and informal solidarity economies in post pandemic Manchester.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Place-based solidarities under strain? Contested local civic spheres in unequal times (2)
schedule_event.description:
There has been a recent return to ‘place-based policy’ from the UK government, exemplified in policies such as ‘Levelling Up’ and ‘Pride in Place’. Such a discourse springs from a recognition of political and resourcing failures in centralised programmes. Alongside economic development, health policy is proposing moves towards ‘neighbourhood’ and ‘community’ health whilst ambitions for net zero are increasingly premised on localised programmes. Such initiatives seek to galvanise a sphere of everyday care and civic action in the service of welfare and sustainable development, whilst also responding to the ‘geographies of discontent’ (McCann 2020) shaped by profound inequalities between regions. This shift to a local focus may offer opportunities for neglected or ‘left behind’ communities. However, inequalities and ongoing conditions of austerity have hollowed out the local and shaped unstable and contested local civic spheres. Whilst there is everyday grassroots ‘experimentation’ happening in communities, this is a highly fragmented arena often involving community, faith-based, voluntary and private sector actors rather than public authorities (Thompson and Lorne 2023). Furthermore, the grievances of localised inequality and neglect are now playing a key role in the rise of far right movements in communities (Santamarina and Ince 2025) as well as populist backlash against other progressive policy such as local climate initiatives (Yazar 2024).  In this session we wish to consider some of the tensions of the local and the community as a sphere of social, economic and political change, and the prospects for solidaristic action given conflicting agendas and the place of the local in regressive politics. We also wish to hear about hopeful and optimistic experiments in local support, care and provisioning which may be able to navigate some of these tensions in place. 

The panel will present research on topics including civic action and imaginaries in local contexts; critical accounts of health, climate or other local initiatives premised on a ‘place-based approach’; models of solidarity and social economic experiments combining social, and climate justice; analysis of far-right organising around the local as well as localised counter-movements

paper.title: The civic imaginaries of ‘two Fromes’: negotiating place, belonging and futures in an edgy English town
Neighbourhood solidarities against the far right: a view from Stobswell, Dundee
Where is the public in (community-centred) public health?
Entangled Solidarities: Navigating Mutuality, Charity and Crisis in a Tower Hamlets Food Cooperative
‘Deport Asylum Seekers’ or ‘Fuck the Tories’? Understanding the Kirkby Riot in Context.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Planning for Just Ocean Spaces
schedule_event.description:
Ocean space is a frontier for conservation and development in the context of expanding economic activities and increasing geopolitical dynamics. Over the last two decades or so, marine spatial planning (MSP) has gained traction as a key approach to managing these pressures. However, MSP is generally working with conventional, Euclidean notions of space that may not be well-suited to the task in hand (Jay, 2012). Contemporary spatial theorising provides new ways of conceiving of ocean space (Anderson & Peters, 2014) that may benefit MSP efforts by enabling it to move beyond simplified rational constructions of planning and deal with the complexities of ocean governance.

In this session we will discuss the ways in which marine planning has engaged with geographical and spatial theory, and debate the ways that MSP can be a response to the dynamic characteristics of the sea (Boucquey et al, 2016; Frazão Santos et al. 2020; Jay, 2018, 2019; Schlüter et al, 2020). In particular we will focus on how alternative conceptualisations can bring out new insights for MSP practice, such as on issues of justice, equity and fairness in the oceans, ocean development, use of public space, challenges such as displacement, and opportunities such as co-existence.

Panel Discussants Stephen Jay Honorary Reader in Marine Planning, University of Liverpool; Ben McAteer, Queens University Belfast, and; Tim Stojanovic, University of St Andrews will reflect on multiple research projects, invite audience debate, and discuss the engagement between geography and planning theory and practice.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Towards Just Blue Places: Blue Economies, Equitable Futures and Resilience in Times of Climate Change
schedule_event.description:
The rapid expansion of blue economies is widely framed as essential to sustainable development and economic growth by governments and industry (e.g. OECD, 2016). Oceans are increasingly constructed as frontiers of innovation, as sites for renewable energy, aquaculture, bioprospecting, seabed mining and marine conservation alongside traditional fisheries and harvesting of new species. Yet this raises urgent questions: Who benefits from these blue economies? Who is excluded? How are coastal landscapes and communities and ocean spaces being reassembled (e.g. Li, 2014), and with what consequences for justice, equity and place?

While policy narratives often promise resilience, inclusion and sustainability, growing evidence suggests that Blue Economy transitions may reproduce or deepen existing inequalities along lines of gender, indigeneity, class, migration status, age and geography (Bennett et al., 2021; Fusco et al. 2022; Cisneros-Montemayor, A. M., et al. 2025). Processes such as “ocean grabbing” (Bennett et al., 2015) and the marginalisation of small-scale and Indigenous fisheries raise concerns about access, rights and recognition. At the same time, climate change is intensifying pressures on coastal communities, reshaping governance, livelihoods and senses of belonging.

Geographical scholarship has begun to question how ocean-based industries are mapped as occurring at sea while remaining disconnected from onshore communities and dependent places (St. Martin & Hall-Arber, 2008), despite obvious overlaps in some industries, such as aquaculture (Martin et al.2021). Attending to the relationship between blue spaces (at sea) and blue places (onshore communities) opens up critical questions about spatial justice, governance and belonging. Emerging debates around Blue Justice (Jentoft et al., 2022) further highlight the need to rethink how ocean economies are assembled and for whom.

This session focus on geographical engagements with the social, spatial and political dimensions of Blue Economy transitions and transformations. By foregrounding equity, place and power, this session aims to rethink Blue Economies and how they might be reimagined to support more just, resilient and sustainable coastal futures.

References

Bennett NJ, Govan H and Satterfield T (2015) Ocean grabbing. Marine Policy 57 61–68.

Bennett NJ, Blythe J, Cisneros-Montemayor AM, et al. (2021) Just transformations to sustainability. Marine Policy 125 104387.

Jentoft S, Chuenpagdee R and Said A (eds) (2022) Blue Justice: Small-Scale Fisheries in a Sustainable Ocean Economy. Cham. Springer.

Li TM (2014) What is land? Assembling a resource for global investment. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers 39(4) 589–602.

Martin, Sarah J., et al. "‘Landing’ salmon aquaculture: Ecologies, infrastructures and the promise of sustainability." Geoforum 123 (2021): 47-55.

St. Martin K and Hall-Arber M (2008) The missing layer: Geo-technologies, communities, and implications for marine spatial planning. Marine Policy 32(5) 779–786.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - New Demographic Trends and Regional Development: Dynamics, Consequences, and Policy Responses
schedule_event.description:
Declining fertility, rising life expectancy, and the resulting population decline and ageing are defining mega-trends of our time. However, scholarship on the regional impacts of these trends remains relatively limited. The paper session aims to initiate a ‘regional turn’, shifting the focus toward regional transformations – across multiple dimensions and across various contexts – that are driven or shaped by the ongoing demographic changes. By providing a collection of regional analyses situated within this context, it highlights the importance of the regional scale for understanding the impacts of the demographic mega-trends shaping our world.

The central premise is that the global slowdown in population growth has contributed to increasing regional demographic divergence, that is, regions are becoming increasingly different from each other over time in their demographic structures and trajectories. This divergence poses substantial challenges for balanced regional development, given the mutually constraining relationship between demographic dynamics and regional development trajectories. Regional economic performance, for example, is shaped by population size and structure, as reflected in concepts like the demographic dividend, human capital, and consumer city. Likewise, public service and infrastructure provision, often managed at the regional level, has been reported to face growing demand-supply imbalance in ageing regions, potentially contributing to rising social discontent. The profound economic and social impacts of demographic change, in turn, raise the importance of populations and their characteristics in the regional governance agenda, where ‘regional biopolitics’ is widely practised through discourses and policies such as ‘healthy ageing’ and the ‘learning regions’ . Ultimately, the mechanisms of uneven demographic transitions, selective out-migration, and population ageing, among others, intensify regional disparities, compounding the challenges faced by left-behind regions.

To address the complexities of demographic divergence and regional disparities, we invite theoretically guided empirical research articles that address the following (non-exhaustive) questions and topics:

  • How do demographic trajectories manifest differently at the regional scale? and through what specific mechanisms, such as selective out-migration, varying population ageing, or uneven demographic transitions? does this divergence occur?
  • How, and through what channels, does increasing demographic divergence contribute to escalating regional economic disparities?
  • How do new demographic trends reshape the regional provision of public services and infrastructure, and to what extent do these shifts influence regional inequalities in welfare and well-being?
  • How do, or should, regional governance agendas and policy instruments adapt to the socio-economic consequences of new demographic trends?

paper.title: Governing Silent Ageing in the Paris Metropolitan Region

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Participatory Ecological Urban Futures by Design
schedule_event.description:
This session explores participatory design approaches towards urban spatial justice and access to and with the natural world by bringing together a geographical and environmental psychological lens.

Urban geographies reflect inequalities that shape unequal access to nature. Our collective futures for healthy, just and democratic spaces rely on sensory engagement with the ‘more than human’ worlds and sense(s) of belonging.

We imagine how our bodies in connection with ‘nature’ (eco-bodyfulness) might inform understanding of citizenship now, and in the future, at a place-based (micro) and global (macro) level. Through guided movement, sensory reflection, case examples, and discussion, we will collectively rethink spatial justice through embodied relationships as humans who are a part of nature.

We will explore:

  • What is a felt experience of spatial justice in cities?
  • To what extent is ‘sense of belonging’ a planetary health issue?
  • How does movement and connection with ‘nature’ influence our experience of place (micro v macro)?
  • How might we imagine human-nature led experiences to inform urban design, and what is the role of researchers to facilitate this?

From this workshop, participants will take away a felt experience of urban landscapes to inform co-defined understandings and solutions for integrating human-nature insights into urban design.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of design
schedule_event.description:
Design plays a fundamental role in shaping spatial relations and ordering environments. Despite underpinning several strands of geographical thought and research, however, the term ‘design’ has remained strangely peripheral, rarely itself becoming the central object of investigation. This appears to be changing. Geographers have recently explored the incorporation of the more-than-human realm into design (Japser, 2018; Oliver, 2025); the emergence of neo-organicist approaches to design (Krivý and Gandy, 2023); the aesthetics of contemporary ecological design (Brady and Prior, 2020; James, 2025); and the postcolonial and decolonial politics of design (Dang, 2021; Jazeel, 2017). 

This session reflects upon these emerging though often discrete currents. It aims to explore the relationship between design and geographical inquiry as well as the possibilities for new lines of interdisciplinary connection. In line with the conference focus on the geographies of inequalities and just places, the session is principally concerned with papers that explore design as always-already political. Who designs? Through what modalities and for whom? What are the possibilities for counter-hegemonic forms of design that challenge rather than reinforce systemic inequalities? 

Contributors might speak to one or more of the following themes:

1. Historical and contemporary geographies of design: How has design historically ordered, staged, and transformed nature, space, and social relations? What continuities and ruptures exist between historical and contemporary design practices?

2. Sites and scales: How does design differ across diverse scales and geographical contexts? What is lost and gained as practices of design travel? 

3. Interdisciplinary engagements: What connections might be made with diverse literatures on design encompassing architectural criticism, urban studies, urban political ecology, the environmental humanities, aesthetic theory, and critical landscape studies? Is there a distinctive geographical contribution to be made within contemporary debates concerning design?

4. Politics and power: How do design practices reflect and reproduce particular power relations? Conversely, how has design been mobilized towards counter-hegemonic ends?

5. Aesthetics and atmospheres: Explorations of how design curates particular aesthetic experiences, affective atmospheres, and sensory encounters. 

6. More-than/non-human design: How does design mediate human/non-human relations? How might contemporary approaches center non-human agencies, multispecies relations, and ecological processes?

References:

Brady, E. and Prior, J., 2020. Environmental aesthetics: A synthetic review. People and Nature, 2(2), pp.254-266.

Dang, T.K., 2021. Decolonizing landscape. Landscape Research, 46(7), pp.1004-1016.

James, Z., 2025. Staged ecologies: Aesthetics, nature and infrastructure in the late‐modern metropolis. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, 50(4), p.e70004.

Jasper, S., 2018. Sonic refugia: Nature, noise abatement and landscape design in West Berlin. The Journal of Architecture, 23(6), pp.936-960.

Jazeel, T., 2017. Tropical modernism/environmental nationalism: The politics of built space in postcolonial Sri Lanka. Fabrications, 27(2), pp.134-152.

Krivý, M. and Gandy, M., 2023. Zany beetroot: Architecture, autopoiesis, and the spatial formations of late capital. Environment and Planning D: Society and Space, 41(6), pp.1058-1074.

Oliver, C., 2025. Dancing with foxes: More‐than‐human design in ‘the chicken city’. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, 50(2), p.e12695.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The mobility of children and youth in times of socio-ecological transformation: From school streets to cities for children (1)
schedule_event.description:
The active, independent mobility of children and adolescents has been declining for decades. Journeys primarily made on foot or by bike are increasingly being made by car, characterizing nowadays young people as the “backseat generation”. This has a negative impact on their physical health and cognitive development and limits their use of public space. Overall, environmental burdens and safety issues are increasing with higher car-use among parents and children. To tackle this, recent projects have emphasized young people’s needs in urban and transport planning by initiating, e.g., school or play streets, low-traffic neighbourhoods or other means of street transformation for youth. Taking children and adolescents into account as a planning principle for equitable urban areas and just transport systems is central to a socio-ecological transformation.

Therefore, the session aims at combining research on children and youth in urban and transport geography, especially emphasizing the influence of urban street transformation on young people’s mobility and use of urban space as well as participation of children and youth towards creating a just and sustainable city.

The session comprises empirical contributions that draw on sound theoretical reflections from inter- and transdisciplinary perspectives on the mobility of youth, children and their families, addressing e.g.:

  • children and youth in urban planning and street transformation projects (e.g. school streets)
  • participation formats and methods for involving children and youth in urban and mobility planning
  • promoting active mobility and designing streets or public spaces for children and youth

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Active Mobility to School: Geographies of Inequality, Justice, and Everyday Urban Movement (1)
schedule_event.description: Children’s journeys to and from school sit at the intersection of three global crises affecting youth: preventable road collisions, rising physical inactivity and worsening well-being. Yet research and policy remain fragmented across transport planning, public health, education and governance, with limited cumulative evidence on how to deliver andscale interventions in real cities. This session positions Active Mobility to School (AMTS), including walking, cycling andwheeling, as a developmental nexus that shapes autonomy, social inclusion and long-term travel habits, while influencing health and safety outcomes. We aim to convene an international forum that integrates social sciences, urban policy, health, and transport engineering. Through evidence from the Global North and South, this session brings transport geography into direct dialogue with debates on inequality and justice, focusing on AMTS, by examining how transport systems, policies, and street environments unevenly shape children’s mobility opportunities across different urban and regional contexts and identify practical pathways for institutionalisation and equitable scale-up.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Environment and development futures - living productively with climate, extraction and biodiversity in Latin America
schedule_event.description: This panel brings together human geographers who work with scientists (including physical geographers) and community partners in Latin America on interdisciplinary questions around environment and development futures. It picks up on recent interests in ‘hopeful geographies’ to foreground some of the creative ways in which communities have made livelihoods productively in the past in the face of diverse environmental and development challenges and continue to do so in the contemporary period despite the shifting nature of long standing geopolitical, climatic and extractive threats. The panellists will spotlight scenarios and places where this often-invisible heritage of living productively with climate, extraction and biodiversity is being mobilised to imagine productive environmental and development futures at different sites and scales. They will discuss how to envisage a research agenda that can capture these processes and reflect on the challenges of embedding co-production into innovative methodological processes in socially and environmentally just ways.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Reversing the Gaze on Technology as a Dominant Tool for Informing Global Targets for Nature Conservation and Recovery: Building an international collaborative network and research agenda
schedule_event.description:
Digital technologies – from remote sensing to satellite telecommunications to AI – have been increasingly employed as a ‘global gaze’ to measure progress towards quantitative global targets[1] for nature conservation and recovery[2] This global gaze, defined by the remote decision-making spaces where global targets are set, risks increasing inequalities if it overlooks the knowledge, priorities and realities of the people and places where nature conservation and recovery is planned or practiced. In response, there is rising demand - even as articulated in the targets themselves - to reverse the global gaze of nature conservation and foreground equitable, place-based human-nature relations. Yet there is an inherent tension between the global target-centrism of international decision-making processes, the digital measurement technologies they employ and the recognition of diverse place-based knowledges and priorities, which can be harder to measure and scale. This tension raises questions - from the technical to the ethical and ontological – about whether place-based knowledge and priorities can or should be ‘counted’ towards global targets and priorities, and the role of digital conservation technologies* in making this possible. This panel will be organized as a series of paper presentations that address this tension between the use of digital technologies to serve global priorities and its repurposing for place-based empowerment. This will be followed by a discussion of opportunities for further collaboration and research on this theme.

*We define ‘digital conservation technologies’ very broadly, ranging from remote sensing to produce visual maps or soundscapes, to eDNA, to the use of social media, AI, etc.

[1] E.g. one prominent example among many of such targets, is the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF) “30x30” target to protect 30% of global land and waters by 2030. See also McDermott et al. 2022.

[2] We use ‘nature recovery’ in a general sense to refer to efforts to restore and recover natural processes.

paper.title: Reversing the gaze on nature in an era of technological innovation

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Rethinking Privilege in Transnational Migration from the Perspective of the Global South
schedule_event.description:
Privilege is about power, (in)equality and (in)justice (McIntosh, 2012). In transnational migration studies, privilege is commonly defined by the capacity to move across borders, migrants’ everyday embodied experiences, changes in structural positioning and meso- and macro-level migration infrastructures (Cranston & Duplan, 2023; Duplan, 2023; Hannam et al., 2006). Building upon existing debates, privilege is shaped through intersections of class, ethnicity, gender and the stratified power of states often rooted in colonial and postcolonial histories. As such, privilege is neither fixed nor universal, but geographically and temporally contingent, produced through specific socio-political contexts and spatial hierarchies (Chattoraj, 2025; Duplan & Cranston, 2023; Kunz, 2016).

While insightful, existing scholarship remains anchored in a Global North-centric teleology, leaving the nuanced power dynamics emerging within the Global South comparatively underexplored (Bommes & Morawska, 2017; Fiddian-Qasmiyeh, 2020; Pisarevskaya et al., 2020). This analytical bias also risks obscuring shifting global power and migration landscapes in which certain Global South countries, including BRICS states, are increasingly positioned as regional or global hubs. Consequently, privilege in these contexts may be reconfigured through domestic socio-political transformations within Global South countries as well as through evolving geopolitical relationships among them.

This session invites geographers to rethink privilege in transnational migration from the perspective of the Global South, foregrounding its relational and context-sensitive character. Rather than treating the “Global South” as a fixed category, the session approaches it as a theoretical intervention and epistemic standpoint that helps challenging Western-centric assumptions in existing debates on privilege in transnational migration. This session welcomes theoretical, empirical and methodological contributions regarding, but not limited to, the following themes:

  • Unequal capacities for mobility in the Global South
  • Passports, visas and citizenship as privilege-making regimes
  • Racialised and nationalised hierarchies of privilege within Global South destinations
  • Privileged and marginalised migrant categories in Southern contexts
  • Gendered privilege in Global South migration
  • Migrant agency and the strategic negotiation of privilege
  • Postcolonial legacies of privilege in global mobility
  • Privilege in Southern migration amidst globalisation or anti-globalisation

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - “Species and Spaces of Learning”: more-than-human dimensions of learning and children's geographies
schedule_event.description:
This work-in-progress session invites early career researchers to share research exploring more-than-human dimensions of learning and children's geographies. We are particularly interested in research examining how children, young people, families, and educators relate to plant and animal species and spaces across formal schooling, informal learning environments, alternative education settings, and community-based pedagogies.

Situated within the conference theme "Geographies of Inequalities," this session provides a space for querying the boundaries of educational research. We invite submissions that grapple with the methodological, ethical, and theoretical frictions of working with more-than-human worlds that resist easy categorisation.

For researchers exploring transdisciplinary approaches to the more-than-human entanglements shaping spaces of learning, this session offers space and opportunity to think alongside others engaged in similar work. We welcome contributions across transversal themes, including: spatializing agency (how non-human actors shape educational geographies); inequalities of access (who or what is included or excluded from "legitimate" learning spaces); methodological frictions (doing research with rather than on participants); decentering the human (reimagining curriculum as ecological relation); and affective geographies (messy, embodied encounters in forming environmental subjectivities).

The session seeks to build community among early career researchers through collaborative discussion of works-in-progress. Presenters will be able to share abstracts, research projects, or thesis excerpts and receive focused peer feedback to advance their work. We also aim to extend this support beyond the conference through an ongoing early career researcher network, fostering future collaborations and collective projects.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Rethinking Educational Pedagogies and Practices in Unequal Contexts
schedule_event.description:
Education is frequently positioned as a vehicle for social mobility and justice, yet institutions are deeply uneven places, shaped by widening social, economic and spatial inequalities. This session invites contributions that critically examine student and/or staff experiences of teaching, learning and assessment within these unequal contexts. It invites contributions that explore how educational experiences, institutional resources and academic labour are geographically differentiated and unevenly valued.

Contributions may focus on geographical disparities in education provision, for example widening participation, inequalities in academic careers and working conditions, in addition to broader spatial politics of internationalisation, and unequal impacts of neoliberal austerity, marketisation and institutional restructuring.

The session also welcomes work on campus geographies, such as inclusive pedagogies, assessment practices and curriculum design, and how these are inflected by inequalities related to class, race, gender, disability, citizenship and place. Papers might also discuss digital divides, accessibility, caring responsibilities, precarity and institutional policies and practices shaping everyday educational experiences in classrooms, online spaces and the field.

Crucially, the session seeks to move beyond diagnosis to consider resistance, reform and reimagining of power, privilege and exclusion. This includes reflections on policy interventions, collective action and alternative educational practices. Contributions may be empirical, conceptual or practice-based, and may engage with students, staff or institutional policy contexts. The session aims to create space for critical discussions about how educational spaces might become more just places of learning and work.

Potential themes:

  • Teaching, learning and pedagogy in unequal institutional contexts
  • Assessment practices, fairness and equity
  • Student experience, belonging and exclusion
  • Staff experience, workload, wellbeing and precarity
  • Widening participation, access and retention
  • AI, digital and hybrid learning practices and environments
  • Disability, accessibility and inclusive practices
  • Institutional policies, metrics and governance
  • Marketisation, austerity and institutional change
  • Resistance, reform and reimagining educational practices

Pedagogies and Place: Spatial Inequalities and Blended Doctoral Education
Theorizing Self-Accommodation as Spatial Practice
From Margins to Methods: Women of Colour Navigating Higher Education
Making Virtual Field Trips Inclusive in Geography Education: A Case Study of NE London

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Towards Just Futures: Negotiating Emotional Geographies of Safety and Place (1)
schedule_event.description:
For Children and Young People (CYP), justice is not only a legal or abstract concept, but an everyday, emotional and spatial experience; shaping how they move through space, relate to others, and imagine their futures. CYP are often overlooked as active participants in the making of justice (Blaisdell et al., 2021; Kraftl, 2013). Instead, justice is often encountered through adult-designed systems, rules and institutions that regulate their bodies, emotions and possibilities.

At a time when uncertainty, violence, displacement, conflict, and insecurity are intensifying, CYP’s contributions to geographical debates are overlooked by adult-centric discourses and narratives. Yet, CYP are increasingly expected to live with the consequences of injustice while having limited power to shape responses to it (Khan, 2021; Pain et al., 2010). Within these conditions, justice is deeply entangled with violence and trauma (Pain and Cahill, 2022). Some CYP live with the impacts of violence inflicted upon them, while others are positioned as causing trauma through acts of violence within constrained, unequal and emotionally charged environments (Fraser, 2015). These experiences are not separate, but circulate through the same places, relationships and institutions, shaping how safety, responsibility and accountability are understood and enacted. In schools, streets, community spaces, the home and digital environments, CYP negotiate justice through emotions, as they navigate harm, its consequences and possibilities for repair (Andal, 2023; Marrun and Rodriguez-Campo, 2023).

CYP occupy a contested role in the future imaginaries of a just world. As a beacon of hope and promise, CYP play a vital role in acting towards social, environmental, political, and economic justice (Paterson-Young et al., 2024). On the other end of the dualism, CYP are often underscored as vulnerable, inexperienced and naïve to the ‘real world’ or even seen as troublemakers and agitators (Schliehe, 2021).

Session I & II seek to explore how justice is felt through practices of navigating harm, seeking safety, supporting peers, and challenging exclusion. Attending justice as a youth centred emotional geography foregrounds how CYP actively interpret, perform and produce justice in ways that are deeply relational, situated and tied to the places they move through.

paper.title: A Political Economy of Being: Youth, Justice and the Making of Viable Futures in Bodoland

paper_authors.identity_string: Tokyo College, The University of Tokyo

paper.title: Relational security and insecurity in public space: Experiences and practices of young asylum seekers and refugees in Leipzig

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geospatial Artificial intelligence methods and applications (1)
schedule_event.description:
Over the past decade, significant advancements in deep learning have launched a new “AI spring,” reigniting research interest in artificial intelligence within GIScience and quantitative geography. While large language models have been making headlines in newspapers worldwide, a broader range of foundation models and architectures (e.g., CNN, GNN GAN, LSTM, Transformer) have sparked new work in geospatial artificial intelligence (Janowicz et al., 2020; De Sabbata et al., 2023, Mai et al., 2025) and representation learning (Liu et al., 2025; Klemmer et al, 2025). For example, vision models have been applied to street-view imagery to explore urban perceptions and infer socio-economic outcomes (Biljecki and Ito 2021; Law et al 2019). The transformer architecture has been used for natural language processing (Berragan et al., 2023), image analysis and machine vision (Li et al, 2022). Graph neural networks have been employed for geodemographic classifications and question-answering with geographic knowledge graphs (De Sabbata and Liu, 2023; Mai et al, 2020). More recently, geospatial foundation models (Janowicz et al., 2025) have emerged, leveraging large-scale geographic data to support multi-modal geographic analysis.

This session aims to be a forum to discuss advances, opportunities, and challenges of the use of GeoAI in quantitative geography and geographic information science, showcasing the latest advancements in GeoAI theories, methods and applications within the realm of quantitative geographic studies.

References

  • Berragan, C., Singleton, A., Calafiore, A., & Morley, J. (2023). Transformer based named entity recognition for place name extraction from unstructured text. International Journal of Geographical Information Science, 37(4), 747-766.
  • Biljecki, F., & Ito, K. (2021). Street view imagery in urban analytics and GIS: A review. Landscape and Urban Planning, 215, 104217.
  • De Sabbata, S., et al. (2023). GeoAI in urban analytics.* International Journal of Geographical Information Science*, 37(12), pp.2455-2463.
  • De Sabbata, S., & Liu, P. (2023). A graph neural network framework for spatial geodemographic classification. International Journal of Geographical Information Science, 37(12), 2464-2486.
  • Janowicz, K., at al. (2020). GeoAI: spatially explicit artificial intelligence techniques for geographic knowledge discovery and beyond. International Journal of Geographical Information Science, 34(4), pp.625-636.
  • Janowicz, et al. (2025). GeoFM: how will geo-foundation models reshape spatial data science and GeoAI? International Journal of Geographical Information Science, 39(9), 1849–1865.
  • Klemmer, K., et al (2025). Earth Embeddings: Towards AI-centric Representations of our Planet.
  • Law, S., Paige, B., & Russell, C. (2019). Take a look around: using street view and satellite images to estimate house prices. ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (TIST), 10(5), 1-19.
  • Li, W., & Hsu, C. Y. (2022). GeoAI for large-scale image analysis and machine vision: Recent progress of artificial intelligence in geography. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 11(7).
  • Liu, Y., et al. (2025). Representation learning for geospatial data. Annals of GIS, 31(4), 557–583. 
  • Mai, G., et al. (2020). SE‐KGE: A location‐aware knowledge graph embedding model for geographic question answering and spatial semantic lifting. Transactions in GIS, 24(3), pp.623-655.
  • Mai, G., et al. (2025). Towards the next generation of Geospatial Artificial Intelligence. International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation, 136, 104368.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Exhibiting Nations
schedule_event.description:
With 2026 marking the 75th anniversary of the staging of the Festival of Britain (and the 175th anniversary of the Great Exhibition), the papers in this session examine the interface of two long-standing and occasionally overlapping interests within cultural and historical geography and cognate fields: exhibitions; and nations. On the one hand, geographers have had a long-standing interest in both studying and organising exhibitions: analysing exhibitions and practices of exhibiting; generating exhibition and display content; and curating exhibitions. On the other hand, geographers have also held a long-standing concern with the narration and performance of nations and national identities. At times, the interface of these two themes has been explored, particularly in academic discussions of national museums, exhibitions, festivals, commemorations, and anniversary celebrations. However, practices of exhibiting extend into and show up in many different spheres, becoming incorporated into a vast array of ‘national’ performances, from the production of television history documentaries to the staging of historical pageants and world expositions.

This session is open to scholars wishing to present on how nations are ‘exhibited’, including but not limited to the following:

  • Critical studies of exhibitions that have been tied into discourses of the nation, from multinational world fairs/universal exhibitions, to nationally-focused museum and art exhibitions.
  • Studies of different exhibition themes in their entanglement with discourses of nationhood and national identity, including exhibitions relating to geographic, anthropological, historical, artistic and commercial themes.
  • Exhibitions composed in different media and formats, from living museums, to exhibitions of photography, maps, art, and anthropological objects.
  • Studies of how nations are exhibited in a range of different media-spaces, from books and television programmes, to more performative modes such as operas, pageants, and plays.
  • Studies focussing on the public reception as well as the staging and curation of exhibitions.
  • Studies of seemingly banal and everyday performances of nations and national identities through exhibitions of different kinds, as well as traditionally ‘hot’ celebrations and commemorations.
  • Studies of different kinds of spaces of exhibition, from village shows and local museums, to national museums and World fairs.
  • Studies exploring questions of justice and injustice, examining the inclusion and exclusion of particular subjects or themes from exhibitions, and the role of minorities, colonies and empires (and their ‘subjects’) in national narratives.

paper.title: Exhibiting Britain, or Exhibiting Wales? The Dolhendre Hill Farm Scheme and the 1951 Festival of Britain
Exhibiting Anticolonialism: Fleeting Visions of a Future Nation at the Annual Conferences of the Indian National Congress, 1924-47
Stepping through the national geobody: the case of nation-themed miniature parks
Exhibiting at Home: Curating Nation in the Everyday
Exhibiting Exploration: The Museum of The Royal Geographical Society

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Fear and Liking in the Fonds: Radical Archival Praxis
schedule_event.description:
In recent years, there has been a burgeoning attention paid to ‘community’ (Caswell 2014), ‘grassroots’ (Rawson 2009), ‘autonomous’ (Moore and Pell 2010), ‘interference’ (Sellie et al. 2015), or ‘radical’ (Schwenk 2011) archives, celebrated as spaces capable of accentuating the contingency of archival authority while preserving and mobilising marginalised histories and alternative urban politics (Burgum 2020, Beswick et. al. 2016). Work highlighting the social and collaborative nature of working with/in archives (Ashmore et. al. 2012) further develops the inherently political recognition that archives create histories as much as they contain them: an attention to what is presented and elided in archival collections demonstrates a ‘methodological commitment to how history’s exclusions are secured and made’ (Stoler 2009, 17). 

We position the radical archive as a space of embodied and potentially prefigurative politics (Routon 2026), one which ‘is change-oriented and future-minded, insofar as it helps us imagine both a different way of archiving and a different world to be archived’ (Ghaddar and Caswell 2019, 72). That said, archival work is nuanced and depends upon one’s ‘angle of arrival’ (Ahmed 2014), capable of provoking affections, aversions, anxieties, and frustrations that resonate in researchers’ bodies as they encounter materials (Dwyer and Davies 2010, DeSilvey 2007, Steedman 2001). Methodological work on this point can itself be radical; several scholars have recently explored how marginalised bodies—Black, Trans, or disabled, for example (see Longley 2021, Rawson 2009, Brilmyer 2022)—experience and transform archival spaces. Meanwhile, other scholarship engages the difficulties of working with ‘unsavoury’ radical collections, such as those of racist or fascist movements (Ashe 2021). Working with hate in this way presents its own ethical challenges around positionality and researcher safety, drawing the political responsibilities of collections and archivists into sharp relief (see Jones 2024). At the same time, archival practice presents promising avenues to oppose resurgent radical nationalisms through forms of counter-history and ‘archival resistance’ (Orich 2020). 

For this session, we welcome papers which engage with radical archival institutions and/or collections, encompassing empirical work and/or methodological reflections. Submissions could explore themes such as: 

  • The politics of access to radical, political, and/or atypical collections 
  • Considerations of how different kinds of radical materials should be labelled, stored, and organised 
  • Embodied experiences with/in archives, including questions around access and representation 
  • Encounters with hateful, extreme, or other ‘unsavoury’ materials in the archive 
  • Navigating contradictory commitments in archival work 
  • Connections between radical archival materials and other research methodologies 
  • Radical archives as sites of resistance to hegemonic political formations

paper.title: A Radical Archive of Radicals
Collecting a White Power Archive: The Three Klans
Archival co-curation as method
The fascist intimate: Encountering poems, postcards, and expense receipts in the archives of the west German radical right

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Rural Spatial Justice and Place-Making from a Planetary Perspective (1)
schedule_event.description:
Processes of rural place-making are closely connected to questions of inequalities and injustice. Disparities in the distribution of wealth, resources and services between rural and urban areas, as well as between rural places, shape trajectories of place development and the identities attached to places. Processes of place-making in turn shape perceptions of spatial injustice, or the unfair treatment, informing a ‘rural consciousness’ through which rural residents interpret politics (Cramer, 2016). Questions of rural spatial justice are also evident in the variegated capacity of individuals to participate in making rural places – a ‘right to the rural’ paralleling the ‘right to the city’. Moreover, approached as a normative concept, rural spatial justice can inspire proactive and progressive place-making (Woods, 2023).

This session specifically calls for papers that address these dynamics between rural place-making and spatial justice from a planetary perspective. Following principles outlined in Wang et at’s (2025) call for planetary rural geographies, a planetary perspective highlights the making and remaking of rural places within unequal global relations and associated questions of economic and social justice; the more-than-human composition of place and the questions of multi-species justice embedded in rural places; the justice or injustice of knowledge claims for describing, knowing and representing rural places, from colonial scientific legacies to indigenous thinking; and rural place-making with respect to planetary crises, including issues of environmental justice in the remaking of rural places by climate change and in conflicts over the repurposing of rural land for renewable energy, rewilding or carbon forestry, for example.

As such, we understand place-making as a relational, contested, and uneven process shaped by multi-scalar forces, including climate change, agro-ecological transitions, infrastructure expansion, extractivism, conservation, migration, and food sovereignty struggles. We are particularly interested in how justice claims emerge across scales, and how rural places are made, unmade, and remade through interactions among human and more-than-human actors.

The session aims to foster dialogue between rural studies, critical agrarian studies, political ecology, and more-than-human geographies, advancing a relational and planetary understanding of rural spatial justice.

paper.title: Broiler farm planetary geographies: maps, food-feed regimes, metabolic justice
When Fruit Trees Uproot the Grain: Agrarian Transition, Power, and More-Than-Human Place-Making in Rural China
Rewilding rural justice: negotiating planetary and local claims in the Dartmoor landscape
Conservation–extractivism nexus and the more-than-human co-production of planetary rural landscapes in the Llanos, Colombia

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Rural Politics of the Far Right
schedule_event.description:
In a multitude of regions across the globe, far-right political parties have demonstrated a notable degree of success, particularly in rural areas. This phenomenon is especially pronounced in areas that are economically disadvantaged and have experienced marginalization as well as stigmatization. However, the manner in which the far right intends to confront the challenges faced by these rural regions remains to be elucidated. Furthermore, it is imperative to assess whether far-right parties effectively address the specific challenges confronting these regions and communities.

The session invites contributions to an analysis of the political activities of the far right in, for, or through rural areas. We posit that a more profound comprehension of the rural dimensions of far-right politics, their absence, contradictions, past development, and national differences can enhance the extant discourse on the political geographies of the far right. To that end, we invite submissions of papers from diverse empirical and geographical backgrounds on the following (or other related) issues:

  • How does the far right think about "the rural"? What are their ideas about the past and future of rural areas?
  • What problems are the far right identifying in their rural policies, and who do they blame for them?
  • What are far-right suggestions for regional policy to deal with problems in rural areas, such as economic and demographic decline or infrastructure decay? What are the far-right positions in other rural-related fields, such as agricultural politics?
  • What is the far right organized like in rural areas? Who is part of their network? What do the national parties of the far right have planned for rural areas?
  • What methodological tools could be useful for studying the political activities of the far right in rural areas?
  • What theoretical tools can help us understand the connection between the far right and rural places?

paper.title: Interstitial Radicalisation: the Far Right and Farmer Discontent in Europe
“You can’t eat electricity”: A new reactionary ‘politics of the rural’
Rural metapolitics – German far-right agricultural policy in the 21st century
Bureaucracy in agriculture as a subject of far-right mobilisation
Rural-Urban Social Antagonisms and Political Polarity: A Marxist Perspective

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Landing offshore wind: uneven coastal geographies of marine extractivism (1)
schedule_event.description:
The sea has become a resource frontier for the expansion of new energy technologies, especially offshore wind farms. Offshore wind is considered an important part of the solution to the climate crisis, energy security and as a catalyst for economic growth. Moreover, the oftentimes deceptive notion of offshore wind being “out of sight, out of mind” suggests that placing turbines at the sea would be less controversial and that their deployment does not create as severe conflicts on land as their counterparts onshore (Haggett 2008; 2011). However, even when these extractive activities take place under water or beyond the horizon, they still have multi-faceted impacts on land. Offshore wind farms, as a new form of marine extractivism, require the construction of e.g. onshore substations, pylons with high voltage cables, underground cables, harbour facilities for turbine construction, operation and maintenance activities, and auxiliary services which can all affect and reshape environmental and socio-economic conditions, cultural values ​​and the lived experiences of coastal communities (e.g. Glasson et al. 2022, Schneider & O’Neill 2025, Sornn-Friese et al. 2022). The expansion of offshore wind infrastructures and the emergence of the offshore wind industry have manifested in local and regional, yet geographically uneven, industrial development (e.g. Dawley et al. 2010, 2015; Gibbs & Jensen 2022; MacKinnon et al. 2022) ), thus engendering environmental, distributional and democratic disparities and inequalities. Since impacted communities and beneficiaries rarely overlap, the expansion of offshore wind raises critical questions of spatial and distributional justice and of democratic governance. Increasing protests from coastal communities along with the rapid expansion offshore wind farms vis-a-vis efforts to capture economic opportunities actualizes the need for knowledge about how land-based impacts manifest, where and why economic opportunities emerge, and to what extent industrial activities can be anchored locally.

This session seeks to shed critical light on the various manifestations of uneven geographies of expanding offshore wind developments, especially opportunities, challenges and conflicts emerging from land-based activities. We call for papers on this topic, exploring for example:

  • Stakeholder and/or public responses to offshore extractive projects
  • Spatial implications of the offshore wind value chain
  • Environmental and cultural implications and needs for coordinated spatial governance
  • Regional economic competition (e.g. between different harbours and industries) and collaboration on attracting new initiatives
  • Imaginaries and narratives of offshore wind developments
  • The role of institutions in steering industrial development of offshore wind energy
  • The role and fate of local and community identity (e.g. resisting, subverting, co-evolving)
  • The role and fate of existing infrastructures (assets, legacies..)
  • Interplay between industry, government, third sector, residents in shaping more locally appropriate and just offshore wind developments
  • Spatial implications of labour activities of the offshore wind industry
  • Benefit capture, skills development, distribution of job impacts (positive or negative)
  • Marine and/vs terrestrial spatial planning; multi-use and multi-functional landscapes

paper.title: Scalar Regimes and Governance Mismatches in Norwegian Offshore Wind Development
Living the process: Local experiences of Norwegian offshore wind development

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Landing offshore wind: uneven coastal geographies of marine extractivism (2)
schedule_event.description:
The sea has become a resource frontier for the expansion of new energy technologies, especially offshore wind farms. Offshore wind is considered an important part of the solution to the climate crisis, energy security and as a catalyst for economic growth. Moreover, the oftentimes deceptive notion of offshore wind being “out of sight, out of mind” suggests that placing turbines at the sea would be less controversial and that their deployment does not create as severe conflicts on land as their counterparts onshore (Haggett 2008; 2011). However, even when these extractive activities take place under water or beyond the horizon, they still have multi-faceted impacts on land. Offshore wind farms, as a new form of marine extractivism, require the construction of e.g. onshore substations, pylons with high voltage cables, underground cables, harbour facilities for turbine construction, operation and maintenance activities, and auxiliary services which can all affect and reshape environmental and socio-economic conditions, cultural values ​​and the lived experiences of coastal communities (e.g. Glasson et al. 2022, Schneider & O’Neill 2025, Sornn-Friese et al. 2022). The expansion of offshore wind infrastructures and the emergence of the offshore wind industry have manifested in local and regional, yet geographically uneven, industrial development (e.g. Dawley et al. 2010, 2015; Gibbs & Jensen 2022; MacKinnon et al. 2022) ), thus engendering environmental, distributional and democratic disparities and inequalities. Since impacted communities and beneficiaries rarely overlap, the expansion of offshore wind raises critical questions of spatial and distributional justice and of democratic governance. Increasing protests from coastal communities along with the rapid expansion offshore wind farms vis-a-vis efforts to capture economic opportunities actualizes the need for knowledge about how land-based impacts manifest, where and why economic opportunities emerge, and to what extent industrial activities can be anchored locally.

This session seeks to shed critical light on the various manifestations of uneven geographies of expanding offshore wind developments, especially opportunities, challenges and conflicts emerging from land-based activities. We call for papers on this topic, exploring for example:

  • Stakeholder and/or public responses to offshore extractive projects
  • Spatial implications of the offshore wind value chain
  • Environmental and cultural implications and needs for coordinated spatial governance
  • Regional economic competition (e.g. between different harbours and industries) and collaboration on attracting new initiatives
  • Imaginaries and narratives of offshore wind developments
  • The role of institutions in steering industrial development of offshore wind energy
  • The role and fate of local and community identity (e.g. resisting, subverting, co-evolving)
  • The role and fate of existing infrastructures (assets, legacies..)
  • Interplay between industry, government, third sector, residents in shaping more locally appropriate and just offshore wind developments
  • Spatial implications of labour activities of the offshore wind industry
  • Benefit capture, skills development, distribution of job impacts (positive or negative)
  • Marine and/vs terrestrial spatial planning; multi-use and multi-functional landscapes

Just and Resilient Offshore Wind Transitions? The Evolving Narratives of Energy Futures in Germany and Poland
Conflicting imaginaries in the renewable energy transition around the Danish island of Bornholm
Matchsticks or Not? The Power of Metaphors in Shaping Imaginaries of the Offshore Wind Landscape

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Authors meet critics: Philippa Williams and Lipika Kamra's Privacy Techtonics: Feminist geopolitics of privacy, WhatsApp and democracy in India
schedule_event.description:
This panel will include reviews of Privacy Techtonics with responses from the authors. 

About the book: Today, privacy is more than a personal choice, it is a terrain shaped by power, politics and everyday life. The global dominance of Meta’s WhatsApp and Big Tech’s recent turn towards ‘digital private spaces’ raises important questions about the balance between public and private interests in the digital age.

Through a feminist geopolitics of privacy, Privacy Techtonics draws connections between Meta’s imagined private digital future and the everyday reality of surveillance, marginality and resistance. Focusing on India, WhatsApp’s largest market, it shifts attention away from western experiences of digital privacy to centre the ‘digital peripheries’ that have sustained WhatsApp’s expansion and corporate power.

Privacy Techtonics shows how WhatsApp’s encrypted privacy architectures intersect with data colonialism, state politics and surveillance and social inequalities. It illustrates how privacy – as ideology, design, law and experience – is infused with power, from intimate life and everyday politics to the policies of state governments and the financial strategies of technology companies.

By challenging western, technocratic and patriarchal notions of privacy as a universal good, Privacy Techtonicsargues that privacy is socio-technical and geopolitical process shaped by the contradictory practices and struggles of Big Tech, the state, civil society and citizens. The book concludes that privacy is being used both to support and to erode democracy in today’s digital age.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - A Change in the Climate
schedule_event.description:
The climate crisis is affecting everyday lives worldwide. It is asking many of us to cope with more frequent extreme weather events, disrupted food and ecological systems, and a growing incidence of diseases that are food, water and vector borne. The urgency of these risks has led to a rise in climate ‘emergency’ declarations. While such declarations are intended as a call to action, they can evoke panic, compromising the time and attention required to prioritise responses rooted in justice, care and solidarity. In this session, we reflect on what it means to centre justice in responses to the climate crisis and to make space for alternative ways of knowing, being and living in a climate-changed world.

In the first part of the session, Dr Sarah Bell will be in conversation with Dr Liz Crow of Roaring Girl Productions. Liz has been leading ‘A Change in the Climate’; a multi-disciplinary visual arts project exploring the interweave of climate change and disability, echoing the nuanced fragility and strength within disabled bodies and non-human nature. The project has created a series of striking still and moving images, which speak to existential questions at the heart of social injustice and the climate crisis; who gets framed out and whose needs are centred, who gets to live and who gets discarded, and how do we make space for joy and remember why the world is worth fighting for?

Liz will introduce and discuss these pieces in conversation with Sarah, who is leading the Sensing Climate project; a five-year study, examining opportunities for responses to the climate crisis to build fairer societies, building on the priorities and insights of people who are disabled, d/Deaf, neurodivergent and people with long-term health conditions.

In the second part of the session, Liz and Sarah will be joined by three panellists who will draw on their work to reflect on the questions raised by ‘A Change in the Climate’ in the context of wider social and climate justice issues:

  • Dr Harrie Larrington-Spencer, Research Fellow at the University of Westminster, whose work is rooted at the intersection of inclusive environmentalism, feminist theory and critical disability studies.
  • Dr Richard Scriven, Geographer at University College Cork, who will draw on his work in advocacy and human rights law to discuss the environmental concerns of disabled people and the need to strengthen the intersectional dimension of environmental justice.
  • Dr Danielle Johnson, Postdoctoral Researcher at Durham University, whose work spans experiences of marginalisation from climate action amongst Māori communities in Aotearoa New Zealand and, more recently, the risks of power outages in extreme weather amongst disabled and neurodivergent communities in northeast England.

Each panel member will have ten minutes to respond to the questions raised by ‘A Change in the Climate’ before opening the floor to questions from, and discussion with, the audience.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - “Zeitenwende”: Political–Economic Geographies of Militarization and Rearmament in Germany and Finland
schedule_event.description:
In light of Russia’s war against Ukraine and the dramatically shifting role of the United States in the global configuration of geopolitical alliances, Germany—similar to its EU partners—faces a new geopolitical security environment. In both EU-level foreign and security policy debates and domestic political discussions within Germany, calls for autonomous defense capabilities have become increasingly prominent.

These emerging geopolitical discourses of (in)security intersect in Germany with a context in which the military has traditionally been viewed with skepticism since the Second World War and in which, particularly since the end of the Cold War, the reduction of arms production (“peace dividend”) has been especially pronounced. Against this background, the globally observable trend toward rearmament and the associated processes of militarization assume a particularly significant form in Germany and are characterized above all by extensive investments in the defense industry.

The session analyzes these developments through three interrelated contributions. It examines both the discursive processes underpinning the current (re-)militarization of Germany and political-geographical as well as labor-geographical perspectives on their manifestations within the German defense industry.

paper.title: Political-Geographical Perspectives on the Scaling Up of the German Defense Industry
“Troubling Worlds of Work”: Workers’ Experiences of Conversion from Civilian to Military Production
Emotional Geopolitics of Militarization: A Critical Geopolitical Analysis of Current Debates in the German Bundestag
Military geographies in the Sámi homeland in Finland

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Food system transformations: mediating or generating new inequalities? (1)
schedule_event.description:
This session explores food system transformations as uneven and contested processes that may mediate, rework or produce inequalities across different actors, places and scales. While such transformations are widely promoted as necessary responses to environmental, climatic and food security challenges, less attention has been paid to how transformation pathways reshape power relations, access to resources and participation in decision-making, often with unequal consequences.

The session invites critical engagement with whether, how and for whom food system transformations generate exclusions, dependencies and new forms of inequality, or alternatively mitigate and redistribute existing ones. Rather than treating inequality as an unintended side-effect, the session foregrounds inequality as a constitutive feature of transformation processes. It also seeks to advance discussion on the tools and approaches used to identify, understand and make visible these unequal dynamics, including methods that foster more inclusive research and engagement.

We welcome contributions that examine how power relations are reconfigured through specific food system transformation pathways. This includes pathways shaped by technological change, policy interventions, governance arrangements, market restructuring and sustainability standards, and how particular visions of “successful” transformation become dominant while alternative pathways and knowledges are marginalised.

Themes include, but are not limited to:

  • Tools and approaches for mapping, analysing and making visible inequalities in food system transformation processes, including participatory and inclusive methods;
  • Equity-sensitive approaches to assessing food system outcomes across environmental, economic, social and health dimensions;
  • Food system transformation pathways and the production or mediation of inequalities;
  • Power, governance and decision-making in shaping and legitimising transformation pathways;
  • Uneven access to resources, knowledge and participation across food system actors and places;
  • Contestation, resistance and alternative transformation pathways.

paper.title: Food system transFARMations: how can we bring the field closer to the school fork?
Neighbourhood food stories: a qualitative secondary analysis across 5 years of FoodSEqual data identifying food practices in a community facing financial constraints
Making Inequality Visible: Applying a Food System Sustainability Compass across Scenario- and Place-based Assessment
How is knowledge produced in food systems transformation? Perspectives from operationalising a food systems literacy framework in EU Knowledge and Innovation governance
