schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Investigating Inclusivity: Qualitative Approaches to Understanding Inclusion in Space, Place, and Practice
schedule_event.description: Inclusivity has become a central ambition in contemporary urban policy and planning, yet the ways in which inclusion and exclusion are experienced in everyday environments remain unevenly understood. This panel includes contributions that examine how qualitative research methods can illuminate lived experiences of inclusivity within urban spaces, with particular attention to public environments such as green spaces, streets, and neighbourhoods.Drawing on emerging research on inclusive green space access and use, alongside projects exploring age-friendly urban design and environments for older adults and climate justice approaches to nature-based urban heat adaptation, the session will foreground how qualitative approaches—including interviews, participatory methods, ethnography, and co-design—can reveal the complex social, spatial, and cultural dynamics that shape who feels welcome, safe, and able to participate in urban life.While policy frameworks often promote inclusivity as a universal goal, lived experiences of urban environments are shaped by factors such as age, mobility, gender, health, and socio-economic context. Qualitative methodologies provide crucial tools for capturing these situated experiences and for understanding how design, governance, and everyday practices influence access to urban amenities and public life.The panel seeks contributions that critically explore inclusive design, public space, and urban accessibility, particularly those that foreground underrepresented perspectives or methodological innovation. By bringing together research on green infrastructure, climate adaptation, age-friendly environments, and inclusive urbanism, the session aims to open interdisciplinary dialogue on how cities can be designed and governed to support more inclusive and equitable urban futures.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Investigating the geopolitics of private satellite infrastructure: new pathways for connectivity and conflict
schedule_event.description:
Corporate satellite companies, such as Starlink and Amazon Leo, have promised to rewire global connectivity and communication through their new generation of low-earth orbit (LEO) constellations. The launch of thousands of these satellites into orbital space will have significant consequences on earth, as digital infrastructure begins to shift from terrestrial to space-based systems. The political, economic, and security impact of such a shift remains untold and untested. For example Starlink’s potential connectivity is creating new connectivities between and among scam economies, conflict zones and smuggling networks. At the same time, increased accessibility of satellite communication is challenging state-based monopolies and creating new opportunities for resilience and improvisation in the face of infrastructural failure. As power circulates from legacy and state-owned telecommunications giants to a club of private enterprises, new questions around state-sovereignty, technological independence, and illicit economies arise. This call for papers seeks submissions rooted in political geography that identify emergent patterns, themes, and issues related to the emergence of privately-owned planetary infrastructures.While not limited to these issues, panellists will discuss one or more of the following questions:

  • How are privately-owned satellite companies, such as Starlink, reshaping geopolitical relations?

  • How do we trace the impacts of these innovations, are there particular geographical methods or approaches that need to be brought to the fore?

  • To what extent are such innovations addressing or exacerbating existing global inequalities?

paper.title: Materiality in motion: tracing the illicit deployment of space-based connectivity
The view from above: extra-terrestrial bordering and new scales of seeing

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Repoliticising Inequality: Organised Abandonment, Carceral Violence and Actively Working Towards Abolition (1): Mind: knowing domination
schedule_event.description:
This experimental 1-day session at this year's RGS conference, invites creative and conventional contributions that centre around abolitionist understandings of “organised abandonment” (Gilmore 2022, Arefin and Khan 2024, Mei-Singh 2024). Mei-Singh (2024) (drawing on Gilmore 2008 and Bhandar 2018) offers a brief overview of the state of the concept:

Existing scholarship on organized abandonment conveys how neoliberal state reorganization sacrifices particular places. This results in the flight of economic activity and jobs, environmental degradation, underemployment, the diminishment and privatization of public programs, and the disappearance of entire ways of life, all part and parcel of the disregard of those subjugated by a racial capitalist social order.

The session(s) invite activists, scholars, artists, community workers and others (at all career stages and from diverse locations) to come together - in ways that are life-giving, creative and transcend academic convention - to continue ‘sharpening our focus of how such abandonment results from plans, as well as abdications of responsibility’ (Gilmore 2023). Arefin and Khan (2024) maintain that organised abandonment is ‘not a passive withdrawal, but an active plot’. They (Ibid:) go on to add that this ‘invites the same precision in our counterplot’.

Therefore, the session(s) also invite us to continue sharpening our focus on how we counter and unmake organised abandonment. We see this countering and unmaking as ‘actively working towards abolition’ (Kaba nd) and finding, recovering and restoring lifeways that have been forcibly abdicated (Mei-Singh 2024). Sessions include papers and micro-workshop activities that help deepen how we understand organised abandonment and how we resist and restore in the wake of the ‘rupturing of life-giving relations’ (Mei-Singh 2024). This matters because grassroots activists continue to theorise abolition as an ethics, practice, struggle and way of being, that requires the development of “dual power” (Kaba and Ritchie 2021) where emergent and oppositional forms of resistance coexist and interdepend (Brown 2017, Ritchie 2023, Chhibber 2025, see also Hughes 2021). That is:

understanding, opposing, fighting and unmaking carceral systems, organised abandonment and related ways of being

coexist and interdepend with

restoring, building and practicing life-affirming systems and ways of being that refuse to use organised abandonment and carcerality as solutions to entrenched social problems (Davis 2003, Kaba and Ritchie 2022, Ritchie 2023, Kass 2025).

✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀ ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀

We see this session not as a way of coming to a definitive disciplinary conclusion or resolution on what organised abandonment is but rather as a way of coming together with an openness for whatever emerges, be this contradictions, contingencies or interrelations. We encourage ‘acting and speaking in ways that are not about mastery, but rather about audacity or incompleteness, because [we] don’t think any of us can be complete in our understanding or in our pursuit of freedom on our own’ (Bhattachrya np 2025).

✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀

Organised abandonment and organised violence in the context of the Italian border regime: the criminalisation of care.

'Abolition is a group improvisation’: A Sound Workshop

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Pracademic positionality in feminist research.
schedule_event.description: Positionality has been acknowledged as a fundamental element of feminist approaches within research (Devault, 1990; Parker, 2016; Peak et al, 2024; Bjorkdahl and Mannergren, 2021) and practice (Koleth and Temenos, 2022; Bassam, 2023). As pracademics (practitioners and academics) we have a unique positionality within our research, consistently navigating between the researched and the researcher, to drive the “difference” towards the “action” (Peake and Sharpe, 2025, Pg 122). The power imbalances we have identified within our research are present because of our involvement in other gender equity networks across the planning and architecture professions. This panel explores these imbalances and how we mediate them as feminist pracademics so that participants within our research are not influenced by a power imbalance in the same way that they may have been within traditional patriarchal practice (Didaskalou, 1996; Dutton, 2024; Dupius et al, 2022). Being situated within the communities that we are researching relies on meaningful connection through vulnerability with our participants (Kamarauskaite, 2025) and a consistent challenging of our positionality within the research we are doing.. The panel will explore how, within our pracademia, we can mitigate or acknowledge our positionality and the power imbalance present to ensure a feminist approach to research lin which “lived experiences then are not just things we write about but are what we write from and for.” (Choi, 2021)

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Uneven waters: Exploring meanings and approaches to just climate adaptation in urban blue spaces across the Global South and North (1)
schedule_event.description:
Water risks and their uneven geographies have become a central concern for the planning and development of cities in the context of a global climate emergency and other intersecting crises. As cities confront the challenges associated with managing water extremes (both floods and droughts), climate adaptation efforts increasingly focus on controlling and redefining urban water's changing dynamics and flows. Such efforts are especially visible in ‘urban blue spaces’ —i.e., areas adjacent to salt, fresh, or brackish surface waters– where water-related vulnerabilities, value, and governance intersect to reshape infrastructure and everyday life. Yet the production of urban blue spaces is far from neutral. They result instead from layered histories of investment and neglect, formal and informal practices, and uneven distributions of risks, benefits, and responsibilities. Climate impacts and adaptation interventions can entrench or unsettle these longstanding inequalities, through individual and collective responses enacted by state agencies, private actors, community practices, and ecological processes. Critical urban and adaptation scholarship has begun to expose such uneven outcomes, but more work is needed to further unpack how water is managed in urban blue spaces and its unjust implications, and to reveal alternative, justice-oriented approaches.

This session will present conceptual, methodological, and empirical contributions that interrogate how justice materializes or fails to materialize in urban blue spaces across the Global South and North. The presentations include comparative, interdisciplinary, and reflexive work that is attentive to power, knowledge asymmetries, and diverse ways of understanding water through the lens or justice.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Uneven waters: Exploring meanings and approaches to just climate adaptation in urban blue spaces across the Global South and North (2)
schedule_event.description:
Water risks and their uneven geographies have become a central concern for the planning and development of cities in the context of a global climate emergency and other intersecting crises. As cities confront the challenges associated with managing water extremes (both floods and droughts), climate adaptation efforts increasingly focus on controlling and redefining urban water's changing dynamics and flows. Such efforts are especially visible in ‘urban blue spaces’ —i.e., areas adjacent to salt, fresh, or brackish surface waters– where water-related vulnerabilities, value, and governance intersect to reshape infrastructure and everyday life. Yet the production of urban blue spaces is far from neutral. They result instead from layered histories of investment and neglect, formal and informal practices, and uneven distributions of risks, benefits, and responsibilities. Climate impacts and adaptation interventions can entrench or unsettle these longstanding inequalities, through individual and collective responses enacted by state agencies, private actors, community practices, and ecological processes. Critical urban and adaptation scholarship has begun to expose such uneven outcomes, but more work is needed to further unpack how water is managed in urban blue spaces and its unjust implications, and to reveal alternative, justice-oriented approaches.

This session will present conceptual, methodological, and empirical contributions that interrogate how justice materializes or fails to materialize in urban blue spaces across the Global South and North. The presentations include comparative, interdisciplinary, and reflexive work that is attentive to power, knowledge asymmetries, and diverse ways of understanding water through the lens or justice.

paper.title: Hydro-hazardscape of Seoul's Banjiha Housing: Assembling Techno-Politics and Compressed Modernity of Seoul
Decentring flood adaptation approaches towards climate justice
Ecology of drainage: Land use planning, stormwater and the production of environmental risk in Mumbai

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Unfuturisms: Unsettling the Rhythms of Planetary Social Life (1)
schedule_event.description:
Progressive, modern, technological, alternative, utopian, socialist, planetary, radical, ecological, revolutionary, catastrophic, subaltern, sustainable, resilient, more-than-human, speculative, pluriversal, dystopian, in the ruins, from below: we’re awash in futures to come —always yet come— futures that are neither quite delivered upon nor ever quite given up on.

Across human geography and the humanities and social sciences more broadly, extraordinary amounts of energy have been devoted to critically attending to the future’s unjust and unevenly distributed nature, and to the search for alternative futures in the Anthropocene. Defying pronouncements of the future’s imminent yet slow “cancellation,” the attempt has often been that of reprising and expanding the archive of “futurisms” (across geographies, political traditions, literary genres and forms of activism) to re-open possible futures for a planetary age.

And yet, the fundamental problem is that “the future” is not what it used to be (Savransky, 2024, 2025). It was modernity’s faith in progress that enabled one to envisage the future as something one could bring unto oneself, unto others, unto the earth . But in an age of irreversible planetary instability and climate chaos, what was once a source of hope and aspiration, and a problem of anticipation, imagination and redemption, has become precarious when not an outright extortion, a source of fear and dread, a problem of finitude and catastrophe, managed retreat and adaptation (Savransky & Lundy, 2022).

This panel is an invitation to gather around a provocation: not to envisage alternative futures but to envisage unfuturisms— alternatives to “the future” as the organising grammar and rhythm of planetary social life. What becomes of the idea of “the future” on an earth rendered forever unstable? What would it take to slip out of the future’s grip and stray out of the uneven and unjust striations through which it continues to dig and groove the terrain? What other rhythms of planetary life —human and more— might be rendered perceptible in the act of giving “the future” a pass?

paper.title: For Now For Ever: Notes on Unfuturism

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - United Cities, Divided Workers
schedule_event.description:
Across the globe, cities are being remade through infrastructures of speed, circulation, and digital coordination. Transport megaprojects, platform-mediated services, sensor-based management systems, digital payment infrastructures, and algorithmic governance promise integration, efficiency and urban ‘smartness’. These interventions are frequently framed as progressive - enhancing sustainability, connectivity, and economic opportunity. Yet the apparent unification of cities through infrastructure often produces new forms of labour fragmentation, control, and uneven exposure to risk. As cities become more interconnected, the workers who sustain them – construction workers, delivery drivers, domestic workers, waste workers, and others - face regimes of surveillance, precarity, subcontracting, and algorithmic management – impacting workers’ livelihoods and bargaining power. This is deepening the existing gendered, classed and racialised inequalities, and creating new socio-economic uncertainties.

In this panel, we ask: how do projects that promise urban integration to generate new divisions in labour? (How) do infrastructural and digital transformations reshape who bears risk, who captures value and whose mobility is enabled or constrained?

We invite critical contributions that question the political economy of contemporary urban transformation and foreground the labour that makes urban circulation possible. We are particularly interested in work that engages with critical infrastructure studies and labour geography on themes including, but not limited to:

  • Gender, racialisation, and the platformisation of domestic and care work
  • Mobile labour, subcontracting chains, and fragmented accountability
  • Urban logistics, just-in-time delivery, and the intensification of logistics labour
  • Algorithmic management, data extraction, and surveillance in urban work
  • Climate transition policies and the uneven redistribution of labour risks
  • Critical infrastructure studies and labour governance
  • Informal, migrant, and marginalised workers in “smart” cities

paper.title: Sharing Economy, Digital Platforms and Community of Digital Nomads - Studying the Workers in Gig Economy, Delhi

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Uneven geographies of decent work
schedule_event.description:
Renewed global attention to decent work as a policy goal (ILO, SDG 8) has positioned it as a pathway towards inclusive growth, reduced inequality, and social justice. Despite this, research consistently demonstrates that access to decent work is deeply uneven, contingent on place, scale, and intersecting social differences. Across diverse sectors and regions, work that is secure, dignified, and fairly remunerated remains inaccessible for many, while precarious, informal, and undervalued forms of labour are increasingly normalized. Access to decent work, under what conditions, and in which places, is still shaped by gendered, racialized, and classed hierarchies; migration regimes and bordering practises; platformisation and technological change; and environmental and political crises. These dynamics not only structure labour outcomes, but also actively produce unjust places through uneven distribution of risk, care, opportunity and recognition.

This session invites contributions that critically engage with decent work both as a normative ideal and a lived, spatially situated experience. We are particularly interested in research that examines how (decent) work is defined, negotiated, denied and contested across different geographical contexts, and how workers, communities, and institutions attempt to make work and places more just. We also welcome papers that move beyond policy framings to foreground everyday practices, collective action, and alternative imaginaries of work, livelihood, and value.

We welcome research grounded in diverse occupational and geographical contexts, including but not limited to service industries, care work, manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, platform labour, and informal economies. Areas to consider could include (this is an indicative rather than exhaustive list):

  • Uneven geographies of decent work across regions, sectors, and scales.
  • Precarious, informal, and insecure labour and their implications for spatial justice
  • Gendered, racialized, and migrant labour inequalities
  • Care, social production, and the undervaluation of essential work
  • Platform, gig, and algorithmic labour and new forms of control and exclusion
  • Workers’ organising, solidarity economies, and place-based struggles for labour justice
  • Crisis, uncertainty, and the reconfiguration of decent work
  • Alternative visions of work, livelihood, and value beyond dominant economic models.
  • Critiques of policy definitions of decent work and/or framing around inclusive and sustained economic growth

paper.title: Navigating Precariousness: Spatial Practices in Hospitality Work
Rhythm Analysis and Mobility in Hospitality Work

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - World Café: At a Distance: Researching urban elsewheres
schedule_event.description:
With the world becoming increasingly urban, UK Higher Education Institutions have hired academics from diverse global contexts, keen to ensure leadership at the forefront of urban knowledge creation, theory building and pedagogy. Despite these intentions, however, Global North-based academics with knowledge and experience of research and home ‘elsewheres’ find their ability to contribute to curriculum development, theory-building and grant funding limited.

This Roundtable is part of a suite of work on Urban Elsewheres, funded by a GW4 grant and aims to establish a community of international researchers who are passionate about using their global expertise to strengthen scholarship on urban challenges. We wish to establish a community with the objectives to uncover the experiences of living in one context and researching elsewhere and collectively address the difficulties of integrating such work, particularly work undertaken in the Global Majority, into research in their institutions. The World Café would revolve around 3 questions:

  • What are the unique opportunities presented by living in one place and researching another?
  • What challenges have you experienced as an ‘in-between’ researcher?
  • In what ways would having an urban ‘elsewheres’ research network be useful in supporting your work?

We propose to use a World Café style of engagement to set up 3 “stations” of discussion through which people rotate. This is followed by a plenary reporting back on the discussions and adding further thoughts.

The World Café would have three core objectives:

  • To connect and network international urban researchers who are often isolated within their departments or institutions, creating a foundation for future collaborations and potential joint funding applications.
  • To identify the challenges these scholars face in leveraging, developing, and applying their global expertise within their institutions.
  • To explore collaborative initiatives that could be developed to address these barriers.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Utility natures: the financial lives of water and energy
schedule_event.description:
Amid tumbling wages and the spiralling cost-of-living crisis, utility poverty has emerged as a defining feature of contemporary life. New logics of accumulation and financialisation in both the energy and water sectors have deepened household indebtedness and are reorganising the relations of social reproduction in ways that overlap and compound precarity (Meehan 2025; Koutlou 2025; Purcell et al. 2020, 2025; Furlong 2021; Desvallées 2020; Loftus et al. 2019; Petrova 2018). Twinned affordability crises in energy and water are, at their heart, a question of politics and power. Yet while geographers have advanced critical understandings of financialisation and its pernicious effects on water and energy provision, attention to the ‘lived experiences’ of utility natures are often amiss and are seldom considered in tandem. What can we learn from the lived experiences of water and energy struggles? What shapes peoples’ experiences of utility poverty? What lines of commonalities and difference can we draw across these two sectors? And in an era of increasing inequality, wracked by crises in household social reproduction and (un)affordable living, what forms of struggle and political insurgency have emerged in response? This session seeks to centre the ‘lived experiences’ of utility natures by bringing together perspectives that reveal the interwoven relations of water, energy, and finance. We welcome presentations that advance critical theoretical and/or empirical knowledge about the experience, logics, governance, politics, and/or struggles of utility natures. At a minimum, we encourage papers that start to draw connections among different utility natures and consider the benefits and/or challenges of bringing these areas of scholarship together to understand, contest, and potentially transform the financial lives of water and energy. Topics of interest may include:

  • Utility poverty (energy and/or water) and its lived experiences, especially within the cost-of-living crisis
  • Financial subjectivities and the multi-scalar role of fiscal infrastructures in the production of utility poverty
  • Techniques of financial discipline — such as water, gas, or electricity shutoffs — and their impacts and repercussions on households
  • Utility shutoff moratoriums and their legal and critical geographies
  • State and private utility governing logics; how they address, produce, or reproduce new inequalities and exclusion
  • Logics of value grabbing in utility natures and their impacts on households
  • Racial capitalism and utility natures; legacies of racialised and class-based urban planning and struggles in energy and/or water poverty
  • Platform capitalism and the role of digital technologies that mediate and govern utility natures
  • Financialisation of life’s essential services and infrastructures, and the cascading effects on unaffordability and exclusion, profit, and power
  • Critical approaches to assistance programs and experiences of radical alternatives
  • Utility natures and household adaptation to climate change and extreme weather (heat, flooding, drought, cold)

‘Utility Natures’ is modelled after a session we are running on the same topic at the POLLEN conference in Barcelona, July 2026. Subject to engagement and interest, we plan to develop key insights and contributions from both sessions into a special issue.

This panel is intended to be in person only.

Financing Inclusion Through Exclusion:  Debt, Utilities, and the Political Economy of Medellín’s Urban Transformation

schedule_event.title: ONLINE - Unequal City: Rethinking Representation for a Just Urban Future
schedule_event.description:
Urban representations, unbuilt spaces, walkability, imaginaries, and idealized images fundamentally shape spatial justice, yet circulate unequally. This panel examines Beirut as a laboratory of Middle East and North Africa (MENA) geographies of inequalities, where these regimes collide with space and power, coloniality /modernity, and infrastructure politics, revealing the politics of city imaging toward just places.

Practices of unbuilt urban space expose how demolition as violence (Fijalkow) erases collective memory in informal settlements' "lived spaces" (Fremont), replacing them with sanitized redevelopment amid resource politics and territoriality. Building on Gehl's public space practices and Whyte's "Social Life of Small Urban Places," we trace how visual disappearance rewrites urban history, displacing marginalized narratives and knowledge production.

Beirut's car-centric urban design, rooted in coloniality/modernity legacies, prioritizes connectivity over local accessibility, spawning pedestrian reclamation initiatives. Yet mobility policies, key infrastructure policies, optimize for cars, not commuters, exacerbating citizenship divides. The urban imaginary (A. Bourdin) becomes a counter-discourse where pedestrians voice hopes and needs, challenging macro-scale planning with micro-scale (Secchi, Vigano) lived experience.

Social media amplifies utopian city images where inequalities vanish; a "perfect just world" accessible to all. These circulating representations promise spatial justice but elide lived disparities; genocide echoes, war scars, and power asymmetries are functioning as ideological screens (Debord) that naturalize exclusion while claiming universality.

Anchored in representation theory (Debord), spatial justice (Soja), and visual culture studies, this panel bridges architecture, urban sociology, and media analysis. Beirut's crises—informality, mobility inequity, digital utopianism—crystallize global MENA challenges of equitable city representation.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Quantum Geographies / Geographies of Quantum
schedule_event.description:
Quantum mechanics revolutionised physics and is at the heart of many contemporary technological advancements. Despite occasional proposals to bring quantum mechanics into geographical theorisation (e.g., Peterman, 1994; Smith, 2016), and despite the radical possibilities for reconceptualising geographic concepts including space, time, and objectivity, most quantum social science has emerged from other disciplines. Wendt (2015) proposed a quantum mechanical concept of consciousness that has been applied in international relations (Der Derian and Wendt, 2022), whilst Barad (2007) developed agential realism through interdisciplinary engagement with the philosophy-physics of Niels Bohr. Drawing upon this agential realism, along with Black studies and Caribbean thought, Noxolo’s Black Quantum Creative Geographies (2025) is a welcome new beginning for research on quantum geographies.

Alongside these disciplinary debates, the Second Quantum Revolution has generated a significant but understudied industry around quantum science and technology, which in the United Kingdom is entangling various spatial dynamics and geographies as well as social, ecological, and economic processes. In Autumn 2025, Kier Starmer and Donald Trump signed a £31 billion ‘Technology Prosperity Deal’ to promote US-UK cooperation in Artificial Intelligence, civil nuclear energy, and quantum technologies. In November of the same year, the Tony Blair Institute for Global Change, released a ‘New National Purpose’ paper stressing the centrality of the UK quantum science and technology industry to national sovereignty and economic growth, to be secured through ‘venture capital statecraft’ (Schwarz 2025). The UK National Quantum Strategy lays out a network of ‘Quantum Hubs’, anticipates the infrastructure for a ‘quantum city’, and implicates global resource and labour supply chains. The ways in which this quantum science and technology industry is being shaped by and altering existing political geographies across a variety of scales deserves attention and requires novel theoretical and methodological approaches to the study of geographies of science and technology (Mahony 2021).

In this session, we want to explore the role that quantum mechanics can play in geographical theorisation, geographical research into scientific research and technological development based on quantum mechanics, and the entanglement between the two.

paper.title: Between Body and Elsewhere: Spatializing Non-Physical Place with Quantum Ontology
Barad’s brittlestars and the question of life
Quantum Black cultural geographies and the rise of Generative AI
Geographies of Quantum

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geography and Area Studies in the current conjuncture
schedule_event.description:
Geography and area studies share a long and troubled history. Both have been deeply entangled with colonial and imperial projects, in which the production of regional knowledge was mobilised to govern, extract, and dominate. The imperative to “know the world” has too often been aligned with the reproduction of violent global orders. Yet this history has never been uncontested. Over recent decades—and with renewed urgency today—geographers and area studies scholars have sought to critically rework these traditions, developing approaches that foreground epistemic plurality, linguistic difference, and situated knowledges, and that challenge Eurocentric hierarchies of theory and method.

In parallel, activists and scholars in regions such as Latin America/Abya Yala have long attempted to re-imagine themselves as anti-imperial projects grounded in solidarity, mutual aid, and alternative political imaginaries. However, these ambitions are increasingly constrained by the current global conjuncture: the resurgence of overt imperial practices, authoritarian nationalism, and geopolitical realignment. Across the Americas, the Middle East and North Africa, and beyond, external intervention, internal fragmentation, and the weaponisation of knowledge have reshaped the conditions under which area-based research is produced and circulated.

This session asks: what is the value of area studies within geography today? What strategic possibilities does it offer for articulating anti-colonial, transnational, and dialogical research agendas? How might area studies contribute to the co-production of knowledge across epistemic, linguistic, and political differences—without reproducing extractive or exceptionalist logics? Equally, what could a critical geographical approach bring to area studies? and, finally, what challenges does the current conjuncture pose for such projects, and how might geographers respond?

By revisiting area studies through a critical geographical lens, the session seeks to open a collective discussion on the futures of geographically grounded, politically engaged scholarship in an increasingly polarised world.

The session will take the form of a roundtable dialogue between geographers working on Latin American and MENA and encourages active participation of all present.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Building food justice through participatory and innovative methods for community food research (1)
schedule_event.description:
Persistent injustices such as food insecurity, unequal food access, land-use pressures, and diet-related disease continue to shape our food system. Community food can be a space for addressing inequalities and imagining alternative food systems. Whether in the form of urban agriculture, community supported agriculture, food banks, allotments, community meals or other forms, spaces of community food are often sites where inequalities are contested, making them sites of sustained interest from a research perspective.

Community food organisations are frequently asked to participate in research in various roles. Without clear goals agreed from the start, expectations can be misaligned, leading to uneven outcomes, duplication of research (often driven by funding availability) and research fatigue. Short-term and often niche funding opportunities (and other factors including academic workload, university bureaucracy, third sector reliance on volunteers and their own funding cycles) can also make long-term relationships between academics and external partners difficult to maintain, despite increasing emphasis on the importance of ‘impact’ from the university side.

Participatory research approaches produce knowledge, but they also redistribute power, centre lived experience, and challenge extractive research relations. This session aims to explore how participatory methods, and other methodological innovations, can make research with community food organisations more just and more democratically grounded. It explores tensions and opportunities in co-producing knowledge with community actors; how scholar-activists navigate power, positionality and ethics; and innovations, particularly in participatory methodologies, for addressing food insecurity, urban food governance; or other related topics.

paper.title: Home Gardening: Co-designing sensory gardening diaries with elderly migrants
Women’s knowledge, collective action, and food security in rural Zambia

paper_authors.identity_string: Royal Holloway University of London and CABI (k.constantine@cabi.org)

paper.title: From stories of change to collective actions: A participatory, creative and decolonial methodology about/for food system transformations
Grow, Learn, Share: Community-engaged survey

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Sanctuary in a Time of Crisis: towards more just futures (1)
schedule_event.description:
The last fifteen years have seen the development of a rich literature relating to sanctuary. These studies often include sanctuary politics as part of politics of place, opening up progressive, relational forms of politics that frame responsibility, connectivity, inclusion, solidarity, and welcome as civic virtues for just places. However, the last few years have posed questions over the future of sanctuary politics and practices. In the USA, sanctuary cities have been the target of hostility, targeted for confrontation by President Trump’s second administration through threats to cut funding and sending in the para-military ICE. In the UK, protests over housing people seeking sanctuary have become common-place in recent years. The UK Government, in its attempt to create ‘a fairer’ UK asylum system, has also recently sought to emulate Denmark’s restrictive asylum model, which prioritises revoking residencies and ‘repatriation’, placing restrictions on family reunion and access to public funds, and seizing assets of individuals to contribute towards asylum support costs. Against the back-drop of ever-increasing hostility, we invite contributions that consider the future of sanctuary movements and solidarity practices around the world. Topics may include, but not be limited to:

  • Sanctuary and resistance;
  • Sanctuary and just places;
  • Sanctuary and just futures;
  • Sanctuary and solidarity;
  • Sanctuary and justice;
  • Alternative manifestations of sanctuary;
  • Methodological and ethical reflections in sanctuary research.

paper.title: For the Birds (and Animals): A Reimagining of 19th Century Sanctuaries in the Age of Climate Catastrophe
Unruly infrastructures: civic unrest and competing claims to sanctuary and solidarity
Critical pedagogy and the geographies of sanctuary between a university and city of sanctuary
Sanctuary + University = ?
“It’s more welcoming than other cities, but still we have problems here”: Everyday sanctuary and structural exclusion in Sheffield

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Social Movements, Protests and Anti-tourism Activism
schedule_event.description:
Abstract

This proposed session addresses the conference theme, Geographies of Inequality, by foregrounding the political sociology/geography of tourism and examining how social movements, resident protests and anti-tourism activism are reshaping debates on sustainability, liveability and justice in tourist destinations. We invite contributions that connect political economy drivers with contentious politics, situating anti-tourism mobilisation within broader struggles over redistribution, recognition and decarbonisation. Conceptually, we encourage work that engages with the right to the city, tourism degrowth, mobility justice, platform-mediated short-term rentals, and environmental/climate justice, among other related exigencies. We particularly welcome papers that theorise mechanisms of diffusion and spillover (how claims, tactics and repertoires travel between islands, heritage cities and rural/Indigenous territories), coalition formation (tenants’ unions, neighbourhood associations, precarious workers, environmental NGOs, decolonial and feminist groups), and multi-scalar governance (street–court–ballot–platform arenas; municipal, regional, national and transnational policy venues). We also welcome studies that evaluate policy trajectories (from tourist taxes and cruise caps to short-let moratoria) and assess their distributional and ecological consequences toward anti-tourism sentiment. We hope that the session will move the needle beyond managerial “stakeholder” framings towards politically informed analyses of conflict, agency and institutional change in visitor economies. It will clarify when and how anti-tourism contention transforms governance and markets, and when it is co-opted or contained.

Keywords

Social movements, sustainable tourism, anti-tourism, mobility justice, tourism degrowth


References

  • Gómez-Bruna, D., & Martín-Duque, C. (2025). Understanding tourism as a problem on the public agenda in tourism destinations: consequences, intensity and urgency. International Journal of Tourism Cities, 1–16. https://doi.org/10.1080/20565607.2025.2545240
  • McGehee, N. G., Kline, C., & Knollenberg, W. (2014). Social movements and tourism-related local action. Annals of Tourism Research, 48, 140-155. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.annals.2014.06.004
  • Milano, C., Novelli, M., & Cheer, J. M. (2019). Overtourism and degrowth: A social movements perspective. Journal of sustainable tourism, 27(12), 1857-1875. https://doi.org/10.1080/09669582.2019.1650054

Link to Special Issue

Papers included in this session will be invited to submit papers for consideration in a Special Issue of the International Journal of Tourism Cities - https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/rjtc20.

paper.title: Rethinking Touristification
DarkSky Place certification as place-based politics in Aotearoa New Zealand
Portraying it as incontestable? State-led touristification and the struggle for justice in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia
Overtourism and the fragility of public space: An Arendtian reading of anti-tourism mobilization in Málaga

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Energy inequalities and home retrofit (1): Equity in Practice - Advice, Access, and Retrofit Pathways
schedule_event.description:
Rapid increases in energy prices, compounded by the ongoing cost-of-living crisis, have created unprecedented challenges for households, with energy-related inequalities reaching record levels. These issues are attracting increasing academic and policy attention, given their profound consequences for health and well-being, as well as their wider implications for environmental sustainability and social justice.

Existing research has documented how energy inequalities manifest across multiple scales - within homes, between regions, and across continents, highlighting disparities between different social groups (e.g. Bouzarovski & Simcock, 2017; Petrova & Simcock, 2017). Additional work emphasises the need for place-based and relational approaches to meeting energy reduction targets (Marsden et al., 2025, Middlemiss et al. 2024).

The provision of energy advice and home improvements through retrofit are widely recognised as critical interventions for reducing energy demand, improving affordability, and supporting decarbonisation (Bouzarovski et al. 2025; Sugar et al. 2025). Yet implementation and accessibility remain uneven, raising pressing social justice questions about who benefits from energy advice and retrofit schemes and who is left behind (e.g. Charles et al., 2025).

This session invites international contributions that critically examine the role of energy advice provision and home retrofit practices, specifically for tackling energy inequalities. We aim to foster discussion and generate new insights into how these interventions can shape fairer and more equitable energy futures.

Suggested areas may include (but are not limited to):

  • Innovative and emerging approaches to delivering household energy advice and retrofit interventions aimed at reducing energy inequalities
  • Challenges and opportunities for equitable access to advice and retrofit, including best practice examples and case studies
  • Community-led initiatives and grassroots responses in the design and delivery of energy advice and retrofit
  • Policy and governance frameworks for progressing energy equity
  • The role of governing actors and cross-sectoral/collaborative partnership networks in energy advice and retrofit delivery

Due to exceptional demand, this is Session 1/3.

paper.title: Energy equality through community-led renewables and retrofit
Energy precarity no more? The importance of grounded and personalised support to accompany households through home energy retrofit programs
Building Trust into Retrofit Delivery: Design Lessons from a Scottish One-Stop-Shop
From advice to advocacy: collective assemblies and the governance of energy inequalities
Home energy retrofit advice in the UK: Towards equitable provision

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Tackling Energy Inequalities through Home Energy Advice and Retrofit (2): Examining how place-based retrofit unfolds in practice
schedule_event.description:
Decarbonising heat and buildings through energy retrofit remains essential for mitigating climate change and addressing spatially entrenched health, energy and economic inequalities. Emergent 'place-based' or 'neighbourhood' approaches, which integrate fabric, heating and smart technology interventions within spatially-defined programmes, are receiving increased attention in policy and practice in many countries. Often this is based on contentions that place-based approaches can accelerate retrofit delivery by connecting national strategy with local needs, develop local supply chains, foster community and citizen engagement, and engage with the financial, social and cultural realities of retrofit in places (Bolton et al, 2023; Macrorie et al, 2024; van Casteren et al, 2024).

Place-based retrofit represents a departure from piecemeal interventions, embedding energy improvements within broader processes of neighbourhood regeneration and transformation. As such delivery may combine energy measures with wider improvements to green space, transport and public realm and/or incorporate innovative finance mechanisms or community co-design. However, as new ‘imaginaries’ of energy efficient neighbourhoods and places arise, important questions of power, governance, justice and efficacy arise (Robinson et al, 2024), including: Who envisions, governs, funds and implements these 'future neighbourhoods'? How do residents from diverse socio-economic, cultural and demographic backgrounds experience, respond to, and engage with such transformations? How democratic and inclusive are such processes? How do place-based approaches mobilise socio-spatial relations to accelerate delivery?

This session invites papers that critically examine the theoretical foundations, practical implementation and social implications of place-based retrofit approaches, drawing on scholarship in place-making, energy transitions, governance, environmental justice and beyond. The sessions will employ a World Café approach over two sessions, each comprised of four papers and rotating discussion. We are open to a diverse range of abstracts that engage with the spatial and justice aspects of building decarbonisation and welcome submissions from all career-stages and non-academic organisations.

paper.title: Behind the dashboard: Data, prioritisation and justice in targeting place-based retrofit
The contribution of community intermediary led, bottom-up, approaches to place based retrofit in a Just Transition: a case study from Levenshulme, Manchester.
Devolved governance for scaled quality housing energy retrofit: A comparative regional evaluation.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Political ecologies of the illicit and their nonhuman reconfigurations
schedule_event.description:
Informal economies and global supply chains, inclusive of unauthorised, illegal/illicit, and criminal/ised forms of production and trade are profoundly implicated in the commodification, wellbeing, and conservation of nonhuman life. Although illegal wildlife trade is perhaps the most well-known example of how illegal or illicit economies can have devastating consequences for species futures (Collard, 2021; Duffy, 2022), the scale of global farmed animal production, for example, is overwhelmingly in informal, illegal, and even criminal economies, and is often entangled with the racial persecution of those labouring in risky animal trades (Narayanan 2019). Further, nonhuman labour-capital is also imposed upon via indirect or secondary effects of illicit economies: the multispecies slavery-environment nexus implicated in illicit sandmining with devastating ecological consequences (Narayanan 2025), while even illicit trade in ornamental plants is leveraged by law enforcement actors to deepen the criminalization and further marginalization of already racialized populations excluded from mainstream economies (Hübschle and Margulies, 2024).

Critically, state institutions are often directly or indirectly involved in scaffolding illicit economies and supply chains, (re)producing environmental harms and animal violence (McSweeney, 2023; Vasile and Iordăchescu, 2022) by exploiting racially vulnerable workforces. A growing body of scholarship on ‘political ecologies of the illicit,’ attends to both the uneven power and politics reshaping ecologies through illicit forms of trade, land use change, resource exploitation, “…and how responses to them produce and/or reify long-standing practices of exclusionary environmental protection and securitization bolstering military, state, and capitalist economic power and control” (Margulies et al., 2023: 1). But nonhuman life is not only impacted by illicit economies, it also (re)shapes and (re)configures them (Dickinson, 2022). To this end, papers will expand the remit and scholarship of political ecologies of the illicit attentive to both their entangled ecological and economic drivers and consequences and what sorts of institutions and actors benefit or lose out in these illicit configurations. Further, papers will consider the agentic role of nonhumans in (re)shaping illicit economies in thwarting, resisting, or otherwise troubling commodification and violent destruction of nonhuman life.

paper.title: Jugaad and informality as drivers of India’s cow slaughter economy
Navigating the interface between legality and illegality: actors and networks of red coral trade in China

paper.title: Where Conservation is a Trap: Venus flytraps, criminalisation, and the racialised landscapes of threatened plant management
Fraudulent Natures: How materialities shape the political ecologies of Illegal Wildlife Trade and enforcement

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Spaces of belonging and barriers: the changing geographies of higher education inequalities
schedule_event.description: Higher education (HE) has long been positioned as a pathway to social mobility (Bathmaker et al., 2016; Breen and In, 2025), yet contemporary landscapes of HE are increasingly marked by deepening inequalities that are spatial, structural, and intersectional. This session invites contributions that critically examine the uneven geographies of access, participation, experience, and outcomes, and how these are produced, reproduced, and potentially transformed within contemporary HE systems. We encourage papers that interrogate inequalities across multiple scales – from the global to the local – and contexts that consider how structural forces, policy frameworks, marketisation, and socio‑cultural dynamics shape differentiated HE including student trajectories.

paper.title: Towards Just Places:  Higher Education, Rural Inequalities, and Localised Pathways to Educational Justice
Competencies, capitals and contexts: relational geographies of graduate employability in England
Bureaucracies of (non) belonging: The extended inequalities of being ‘international’ in UK HE
Precarious Dwelling: Differentiation, Embodied Strategies, and Domestic (Dis)Connections in Vietnamese International Students’ Housing Experiences in the UK
A New Era of Studenthood? Shifting Campus Geographies during a Cost-of-Living Crisis.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Emerging voices in economic geography (1)
schedule_event.description:
This session invites postgraduate researchers (PGRs) and early career researchers (ECRs) to share emerging research, ideas, and provocations in economic geography. We aim to create a supportive and inclusive space for scholars at the early stages of their academic journeys to showcase work in progress, explore new directions, and connect with others working across diverse traditions and contexts.

We welcome contributions that engage with any aspect of economic geography, broadly defined. This may include (but is not limited to) research on:

  • Economic inequalities and uneven development
  • Digital, platform, and creative economies
  • Gender, race, identity and the economy
  • Geographies of markets, production, exchange and consumption
  • Geographies of money, finance and financialisation
  • Labour, work, livelihoods, and care
  • Housing, real estate, and the built environment
  • Regional, urban, and rural economies
  • Sustainability, climate, and green transitions
  • Industrial change, innovation, and entrepreneurship
  • Informal, alternative, and community economies
  • Intellectual histories and futures of economic geography
  • Theories and theorising in economic geography
  • Methods and methodological innovation in research on the economy

The session is intentionally open and exploratory. We particularly encourage:

  • Papers based on PhD research or postdoctoral projects
  • Works-in-progress or presentation of preliminary findings
  • Contributions that experiment with methods, theory, or interdisciplinary approaches
  • Submissions from researchers who are new to the RGS–IBG conference or academic conferencing more broadly

Our goal is to foster constructive discussion, peer support, and confidence-building in a friendly environment. Presenters will be encouraged to share not only findings, but also questions, challenges, and future directions.

paper.title: Uneven Geographies of Economic Geography: Where Paradigms are Made and How They Travel
Contextualizing AI workplace disruptions in the call center sector
Unequal Entrepreneurial Ecosystems: The Lived Experiences of Migrant Women Entrepreneurs in Switzerland
Negotiating Logics of Value in Peripheral Development: The Role of Creative Entrepreneurs

paper.title: Beyond Homogeneity: Differentiated Engagement with High- and Low-Risk Fringe Finance among People with Disabilities in Waterloo Region, Ontario, Canada
Permissibility, power and exclusion at the economic frontiers of vacant property in London
Exploring the extent of spatial mismatch between employment and housing in European cities
Performing Sustainability: Symbolic Governance and the State–Finance Nexus in Indonesia’s Green Transition

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Beyond Human Bias: Re-imagining Equitable AI for Tourism’s Multiple Stakeholders
schedule_event.description:
Session Co-Organisers: Duygu Salman, Fiona Bakas, Aysun Bozanta

AI systems are increasingly embedded in tourism platforms, shaping how travellers navigate destinations, how places and local communities are represented, and how tourism workers experience labour and well-being. The Digital Revolution is a wave of innovation, leading to the growth of some tourism and leisure jobs and the disappearance of others, but it is reproducing longstanding gendered, racialised, and accessibility-related inequalities, while also generating new forms of algorithmic exclusion. The European Institute for Gender Equality warns that whilst sharing platforms can con­tribute to more gender equality, they can also reinforce sex­ism and inequality. Despite the infiltration of digitisation within many areas of tourism, very limited research exists on the impact of the rapidly technologically advancing phenomenon.

Complementing these perspectives, this session foregrounds a technical perspective on how artificial intelligence—particularly large language models (LLMs), retrieval-augmented systems, and recommender algorithms—structures tourism’s information and decision-making landscapes. LLMs may also propagate stereotypical cultural representations. In addition, algorithmic management and automated moderation can misclassify or suppress content when linguistic or cultural contexts are not adequately captured . This session aims to integrate methods from AI auditing, fairness-aware machine learning, and dataset documentation with critical tourism geography. Thus, it highlights the need for research on transparent evaluation practices and equity-oriented governance frameworks to ensure that AI reshapes tourism in ways that reduce rather than reproduce structural inequalities related to tourism workers, customers and all related stakeholders.

We invite contributions that explore how bias is encoded, amplified, or mitigated across tourism’s diverse stakeholder groups—including travellers, hosts and guides, platform employees, customer-service workers, and small tourism businesses whose livelihoods increasingly depend on AI transparency. By foregrounding feminist, intersectional, anti-racist, and accessibility-centred approaches, the session seeks to connect critical tourism studies with emerging debates in AI ethics, digital labour, platformisation and information systems.

We particularly welcome papers that:

  • analyse how AI shapes experiences of mobility, safety, labour, and representation;
  • audit or critique LLM outputs, datasets, or platform algorithms;
  • examine how inequalities in algorithmic management and digital customer-service infrastructures influence tourism workers and tourists
  • propose design frameworks, imaginaries, or governance practices for more equitable and inclusive AI use in tourism.
  • provide feminist analyses of how gender roles impact and are impacted by the digitisation and platformisation of tourism and leisure
  • expand on how intersectional inequalities of tourism workers are amplified by the digitisation and platformisation of tourism
  • explore how Ai and platformisation impact tourism workers’ and tourists´ well-being
  • develop an understanding of who AI-mediated platforms are excluding and who they are including

Empirical, theoretical, and methodological contributions are encouraged.

Bringing together tourism geographers, digital geographers, AI scholars, and tourism technology and leisure practitioners, this session aims to open a critical space to rethink how AI could be designed and governed to reduce rather than reinforce geographies of inequality.

Sponsored by Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group (https://www.gltrg.org)

paper.title: Worker Well-being and Social Dialogue in the era of Ai-enabled digitization of Tourism and Hospitality work processes.
Algorithmic Governance and Structural Inequality in Digital Tourism: A System Dynamics Perspective
The Algorithmic Tour Guide: How LLMs Construct Safety, Danger, and Belonging across Tourist Destinations
The Digital Divide in the Tourism Industry: Generative AI, Digital Confidence, and the Risk of Exclusion

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Wither Human(ity)? (1)
schedule_event.description:
In a moment marked by militarised borders, racialised policing, authoritarian resurgence, environmental degradation, and genocidal violence, humanitarian language saturates public discourse. Appeals to “humanity” promise universality, dignity, and protection. Yet who is recognised as fully human within these appeals – and who is not? Responding directly to the conference theme Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places, this session examines how unjust places are organised through historically racialised constructions of the human itself.

Rather than treating the human as a neutral and unquestioned foundation, the papers approach it as a stratified and contested category that distributes life, comfort, responsibilities, and vulnerabilities unevenly across spaces. Inequalities, in this view, are not only economically or institutionally (re)produced, but ontologically grounded.

The session begins by revisiting more-than-human and new materialist debates that seek to destabilise Western humanism. While these approaches productively challenge anthropocentrism, they often sideline the socio-economic and racial logics that have historically determined who counts as human in the first place. By foregrounding racialisation and political economy, this intervention insists that moving “beyond” the human cannot substitute for confronting its racial foundations.

Questions of spatial belonging emerge in an analysis of the “politics of comfort”. Home, dwelling, and spatial design appear neutral or technical, yet they presuppose a normative, secure, and propertied human subject. Comfort becomes a spatial technology that renders some bodies properly “at home” while marking others as excessive, out of place, or less-than-human. Inequality is embedded not only in policy but in spatial imaginaries themselves.

The problem of responsibility then comes into focus. If poststructuralist and posthumanist critiques destabilise reason and subjecthood, on what grounds can ethical obligation be articulated? Rather than returning to human exceptionalism, this session explores whether a critical re-humanisation of geography might reconfigure responsibility in ways that remain reflexive while resisting indifference.

Finally, drawing on Black radical and decolonial thought, being human is reframed as struggle rather than stable category. Humanitarian and human rights frameworks, far from neutral, can operate as racial ordering devices that distribute life and death along enduring colonial lines. Humanity emerges as a terrain of contestation rather than a settled fact.

Together, these papers argue that working toward just places requires confronting how the category of the human has been historically produced through racial and colonial hierarchies. This conceptual interrogation forms the foundation for the linked session Wither Human(ity)? (II), which turns to the concrete sites where these hierarchies are enacted, negotiated, and resisted in practice.

paper.title: Have we ever been social? On present absences in new materialisms
The Politics of Comfort: Posthuman Being and the Making of Home
Responsibility without Vernunft? Opportunities of a re-humanisation of cultural geography
Human Being as Struggle: Toward Insurrectionist Ethics

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Wither Human(ity)? (2)
schedule_event.description:
If inequalities are embedded in the very definition of the human, how are these hierarchies lived, reproduced, and contested in everyday spaces? Building directly on the conceptual groundwork of Wither Human(ity)? (I), this session turns to urban, environmental, securitised, and healthcare contexts in which access to humanity is actively negotiated.

Engaging the conference theme Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places, the session demonstrates that struggles for justice are simultaneously struggles over who is recognised as fully human – and whose life, care, and voice are rendered expendable.

In Rio de Janeiro, ethnographic research with K-9 security trainers reveals controversies around “humanisation”. Here, the boundaries between human and non-human are not abstract philosophical concerns but practical matters shaping training regimes and security practices. Situated cosmologies complicate academic moves beyond the human, revealing how colonial legacies and necropolitical conditions structure lived understandings of humanity.

In the UK, racialised environmental injustice unfolds through exclusion from green spaces. Black and Black-mixed heritage communities describe material barriers and affective landscapes of fear and hesitation. At the same time, outdoor community groups cultivate alternative geographies of belonging and wellbeing, reclaiming nature as a space of dignity and collective presence. Nature becomes a site where humanity is denied and reasserted.

Urban vacant spaces in Brussels offer another terrain of contestation. Framed alternately as ecological sanctuaries or as potential shelters for homeless and migrant populations, these spaces expose tensions between multispecies justice and claims to housing and refuge. Vacancy becomes a lens through which competing constructions of the human – and of justice – collide.

In healthcare, analysis of NHS guidance and policy reveals how institutional discourse positions patients as passive recipients rather than epistemic partners. Migration, linguistic diversity, and socio-economic difference are flattened within standardised health literacy frameworks. Through datafication and professional authority, experiential knowledge is marginalised, producing epistemic injustice and reinforcing unequal access to care.

Across these sites, hierarchies of life, care, and voice are spatially organised and institutionally reproduced. Just places cannot be achieved without confronting how institutional, urban, and policy regimes differentially recognise, protect, and deny humanity in practice.

Together with Wither Human(ity)? (I), this session advances a relational and anti-racist rethinking of humanity as both conceptual problem and lived struggle – linking ontological critique of inequalities to everyday geographies of injustice.

paper.title: The “Human” in more-than-human research: “humanization” in Rio de Janeiro’s K-9 security
Urban Vacant Spaces: Refuges for Vulnerable Human and Non-Human Life?
Muted Voices In Health Literacy: How Discursive Power Produces Epistemic Injustice In Urban Healthcare

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Energy inequalities and home retrofit (2): Structural Inequalities and Policy Landscapes in Energy and Retrofit Advice
schedule_event.description: This session examines how structural arrangements, institutional governance, and policy frameworks reproduce or challenge energy inequalities across different geographies, particularly in terms of energy and retrofit advice.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Navigating the ordinary Anthropocene in the Global  North: local encounters and embodied knowledges
schedule_event.description:
We live in a world increasingly shaped by environmental and climate change. Events once understood as exceptional – floods, droughts, heatwaves, storms, or extreme cold – are becoming part of a new normal, subtly yet persistently reworking social relations, material environments, and everyday routines. These changes are not only registered through spectacular moments of crisis, but are sensed, felt, and embodied in the mundane practices of daily life.

This session explores the navigation of the Anthropocene ordinary, with a particular focus on contexts in the Global North. While climatic disruptions have long been central to disaster research – especially in the Global South – the Global North has often been characterized by infrastructures, welfare systems, and political-economic arrangements that buffer risk and sustain assumptions of stability, predictability, and protection. Yet these assumptions are increasingly unsettled. Environmental change is now encountered locally, negotiated in everyday encounters between humans and more-than-human worlds, and made meaningful through situated forms of knowledge, practice, and affect.

This session foregrounds the intersections of disruption and everydayness, attending to embodied experiences, emotions, and affects, as well as to the collective and individual practices through which people navigate environmental uncertainty. We are particularly interested in how (local) knowledges are continuously negotiated in mundane routines and moments of rupture, giving rise to new ways of living with, explaining, or contesting environmental change. Across contributions, we encourage critical attention to questions of power, intersectional vulnerability, agency, and social justice, including the colonial and racialized dimensions of climate change, and to more-than-human relations, interspecies care, and affective ethics.

paper.title: Living on unstable foundations? – Navigating everyday climate change-induced housing insecurity  in Bordeaux and Rotterdam
Farmers’ Local Encounters and Embodied Knowledges of Climate Adaptation in Wales
Storying weather-heritage: textures of the ordinary Anthropocene in the East Anglian  Fens
The trees are to blame – environmental meanings in the aftermath of flooding on the  Ahr River

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Uneven Coasts: Spatial Inequalities, Health, and Resilience across the Life Course
schedule_event.description: Coastal communities in England are shaped by longstanding spatial inequalities that intersect with health, ageing, mental wellbeing and resilience. Despite growing policy attention, coastal places remain marginal within geographical debates on inequality and justice, often treated as peripheral rather than as sites of distinct and enduring disadvantage. This session brings together four papers that examine how coastal residence produces and sustains unequal life-course outcomes, integrating longitudinal population data with participatory, place-based research.

paper.title: Mapping Resilience in Coastal Communities: ARISE Focus Groups
Mapping frailty across place: coastal differences in older adult health in England
"Something to do and somewhere to go": adolescent leisure participation and adult mental health
Coastal health gap in life expectancy

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - International Journal of Urban and Regional Research Lecture: Endurance, Repair, Abolition: Re-thinking the Liveable City
schedule_event.description:
International Journal of Urban and Regional Research Lecture

Endurance, Repair, Abolition: Re-thinking the Liveable City
Alex Vasudevan, University of Oxford

Discussants: Michele Lancione, Politecnico di Torino, and Tatiana Thieme, UCL

Lecture abstract
At the heart of this paper are a series of programmatic reflections on the possibilities of collective city life in our current intensifying emergency. How, the paper asks, can we assemble and sustain a more just urbanism in a time of ecological crisis, planetary-scale dispossession, rising revanchist populisms and hyper-militarised violence? Building on the author’s longstanding research on the spatial politics of urban squatting, the paper seeks to develop a more expansive framework for re-thinking the urban political across different sites and scales. More specifically, it sets out three speculative frames of reference that mark out the coordinates of a renewed approach to inhabiting the city otherwise. First, the paper revisits the modest methods of sheltering, endurance and survival adopted by squatters and the precarious political horizons that they summon into being. Second, it examines the practices of squatters as reparative acts of world-making that re-assemble the city as a space of care and experimentation. Finally, the paper explores the relationship between squatting and an abolitionist politics of resistance that seeks to challenge a global carceral order and the violent modes of banishment, dispossession, and expulsion it ultimately depends on. At stake here, the paper concludes, are important normative questions about the possibility of a liveable life and the re-making of an inhabitable world.

paper.title: Endurance, Repair, Abolition: Re-thinking the Liveable City
