schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Robots, Drones and urban (in)justice: Automation, surveillance, inclusion and new geographies of (in)justice in more than human cities (1)
schedule_event.description:
Artificial Intelligences increasingly enter already technicised urban spaces, which they can occupy and affect materially through devices such as robots and drones. Progress in AI and the highly networked nature of current urban environments make it possible for robots and drones to operate in close proximity to humans in complex, unscripted environments including public spaces, at home, at work or in urban green spaces. As they do so, they have potential to impactfully reconfigure cities, automating tasks, transforming urban flows, and extending the reach of AIs designed to maximise specific objectives, metrics or utility functions. In this session we will explore the contours of the new forms of (in)justice relevant to emerging entanglements and relations in urban spaces shared by human, non-human and artificial intelligences: How and why are Geographers thinking about and engaging with (in)justice in urban futures which now include humans but also non-human intelligences and agencies embodied in drones and robots? What new forms of injustice may make themselves felt when robots and drones relate in various ways to urban space (or fail to do so)?

Papers in this session contribute to theoretical and empirical discussion of urban drones and robots, with attention to the new forms of justice or injustice potentially introduced as embodied AIs transform, augment, optimise or automate urban life.

paper.title: Robotic Urbanism and the Question of Justice: Towards a Critical Framework for Governing Automated Cities
Robotic utopias and evil paradises: Utopia as a method to articulate the futures implied in smart machine strategies
Urban Drone Vision, Automation, BVLOS and the More Than Human juncture
Script Collision: Layered Operations and the Emergence of Urban  (In)Justice in Robotic Space

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Robots, Drones and urban (in)justice: Automation, surveillance, inclusion and new geographies of (in)justice in more than human cities (2)
schedule_event.description:
Artificial Intelligences increasingly enter already technicised urban spaces, which they can occupy and affect materially through devices such as robots and drones. Progress in AI and the highly networked nature of current urban environments make it possible for robots and drones to operate in close proximity to humans in complex, unscripted environments including public spaces, at home, at work or in urban green spaces. As they do so, they have potential to impactfully reconfigure cities, automating tasks, transforming urban flows, and extending the reach of AIs designed to maximise specific objectives, metrics or utility functions. In this panel session we will explore the contours of the new forms of (in)justice relevant to emerging entanglements and relations in urban spaces shared by human, non-human and artificial intelligences: How and why are Geographers thinking about and engaging with (in)justice in urban futures which now include humans but also non-human intelligences and agencies embodied in drones and robots? What new forms of injustice may make themselves felt when robots and drones relate in various ways to urban space (or fail to do so)?

Panellists will engage in theoretical and empirical discussion of urban drones and robots, with attention to the new forms of justice or injustice potentially introduced as embodied AIs transform, augment, optimise or automate urban life.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Big-Tech and the Governance of Labour and Bodies
schedule_event.description:
Recent scholarship highlights the growing influence of technology corporations in shaping political, social, and state practices. Big Tech platforms and AI technologies are increasingly entangled with state power, authoritarian logics, and new forms of governance, raising fundamental questions about political authority, public accountability, and democratic life (Jarret et al, 2025). The concept of “Big Tech authoritarianism” (Akbari 2025) foregrounds how tech giants not only mediate information but also align with or reproduce authoritarian logics of power, surveillance, and control. Likewise, emerging frameworks of AI authoritarianism (Dekeyser et al. 2025) argue that key features of AI-such as scalability, algorithmic incorporation of difference, and techno-determinist ideologies - actively reshape political governance and authoritarian dynamics across geopolitical contexts.

This session responds to a growing need to critically interrogate the political thrust of technological power and the growing political influence of tech capital at a time when digital platforms and algorithmic systems are increasingly central to shaping state practices, public discourse, citizen engagement, and the possibilities for resistance. Rather than treating “the digital” as a neutral domain, we invite geopolitically informed analyses that situate technology in the relational dynamics of power, space, and governance. This aims to build on existing scholarship theorizing data power (Thatcher & Dalton, 2021), technofascism (McElroy 2025; Coeckelbergh, 2026), or digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019), and aligns with recent calls to rethink governance frameworks in light of AI’s active modulation of political processes and the political synergies between Big Tech and state actors.

This session explores how Big Tech and AI are implicated in reconfiguring the practices, techniques, and imaginaries of governance, with a specific focus on the governance of labour and bodies.

paper.title: The Right to Information in the Algorithmic Workplace: Labor Law, Worker Agency, and AI Governance in Spain’s Contact Center Sector
Power, Capital Coordination, and Platform Urbanism in a Global South Financial Center
Automation and the social division of labour in fruit and veg production
Big Tech’s Imaginations on the Future of Work

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - The Geographies of Generative AI in Higher Education
schedule_event.description:
Generative artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping practices of learning, teaching, and knowledge production in higher education (Pratschke, 2024, HEPI, 2025). The widespread use of large language models by both students and faculty presents fundamental challenges to established pedagogical norms, as well as academic integrity, and institutional governance (Bertram Gallant and Rettinger, 2025). As universities struggle to respond to these disruptions, significant shifts are emerging in assessment practices, curriculum (re)design, and integrity and assurance mechanisms, as well as everyday pedagogical cultures of teaching and learning.

Taking seriously the geographies of the university, this session invites critical reflection on how generative AI is reconfiguring the geographies of higher education; spatially, institutionally, ethically and relationally. Rather than focusing narrowly on technical or instrumental approaches such as prompt engineering, the session seeks to centre broader conceptual and empirical insights on how universities are being reshaped by cultures of artificial generativity, including changes in pedagogical spaces and cultures, shifts in students’ digital practices, as well as how forms of academic labour are being reimagined in response.

We welcome contributions in the broadest sense, including conceptual interventions, pedagogical reflections, and empirical case studies. Topics may include, but are not limited to:

  • experiences embedding critical AI literacy within geography degree programmes
  • analysis of institutional AI strategies and responses
  • critical AI politics and resistance amongst students and faculty
  • approaches to governance, assurance, and academic integrity
  • assessment redesign and curriculum innovation
  • AI and student experience enhancement and student-voice projects
  • the implications of generative AI for student wellbeing, pastoral care, and academic support.

paper.title: How can I help you? GenAI as a Partner in Academic Integrity
Marketisation, Managerialism and the AI “Crisis” in Higher Education
Reflections from Across the AI Assessment Faultline: Diverging Practices of Re Design or Resistance in Geography Assessment
Cultural and Political Biases in Generative AI: A Critical Classroom-Based Study in Geography of Climate Change Education
AI in Action: Knowledge, ideology and practice of AI amongst university students
Ethical use of GenAI in Teaching and Learning: Insights from a Community of Practice Approach

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Atmospheres and Young People’s Lives: Sensory Worlds, Socio-Technical Environments, and Speculative Fiction
schedule_event.description:
This panel consolidates and extends atmospheric research within youth geographies by engaging atmospheres as conceptual, methodological, and political problematics rather than as discrete empirical objects. Although affective atmospheres have been extensively theorised in human geography (e.g. Ben Anderson; Derek McCormack; Kathleen Stewart), their analytic uptake in youth studies has been comparatively cautious. Youth geographies have tended to privilege voice, representation, and agency, often mobilising “youth” as a stable socio-political category shaped through governance and generational power. While this orientation has been vital for foregrounding young people as situated actors, it has constrained engagement with the diffuse, pre-personal, and more-than-human forces that condition their spatial experience (Kraftl and Horton, 2017).

Bringing together work on sensory attunement, socio-technical milieus, and geo-social imaginaries, the panel repositions youth not as the origin of agency but as participants within distributed socio-material-technical assemblages. Young people’s lives are understood as emerging through atmospheres composed of rhythms, infrastructures, architectures, digital systems, environmental conditions, and embodied practices. Inquiry, in this framing, becomes an immersive and collaborative attunement to the intensities that shape how young people sense, inhabit, and imagine their worlds. Rather than capturing youth experience as already formed, atmospheric research attends to how young people are implicated in the ongoing composition of relational fields that exceed them yet remain intimate in their effects.

Conceptually, this orientation shifts from fixed social categories toward an atmospheric politics aligned with affect theory (e.g. Nigel Thrift; Brian Massumi). Here, youth are neither sovereign agents nor passive subjects, but are caught up in circulations of feeling, density, and sensibility through which power operates. Inequality and possibility take shape in the modulation of the environments young people inhabit and in the anticipatory disabling urban environments (Leyman),  geological life and climate inheritance (Truman), and socio-technical infrastructures (Trafí-Prats) that press upon their present. By centring youth within these atmospheric dynamics, the panel advances a more-than-human analytics attentive to how young lives are composed, constrained, and opened within shifting environmental and affective conditions.

Session Format

We propose a live-only in person paper session. It will begin with a 15-minute introduction by the chair, Samantha Wilkinson (Manchester Metropolitan University) framing the conceptual and methodological stakes of the panel. Each of the three papers will be allocated 15 minutes for presentation, followed by 5 minutes of focused discussion. The session will conclude with a 15-minute response from the discussant, EJ Renold (Manchester Metropolitan University), leaving an additional 5 minutes at the end for a brief collective exchange.

paper.title: Youth, Technicity, and Urban Atmospheres: Reframing the Smartphone Debate
Un/Common Sensory Worlds: Cosplay, Neurodiversity, and the Re Atmosphering of Urban Life
Geo-Atmospheres: Youth Speculative Writing about Place and the Future in Post Industrial Wales and Meridianed London

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of inequalities and public policy for urban vulnerable populations in Latin America (2)
schedule_event.description:
The lives of vulnerable populations in Latin American cities are shaped by deeply rooted social, economic and political inequalities. This session seeks to advance a collaborative and policy-oriented conversation about how public policy can enable more just urban environments for those experiencing vulnerability and living with disadvantages. Building on scholarship about urban informality, everyday resilience and (creative) responses to marginalisation (Fundación IDEA, 2017; Fernández et al., 2023), we welcome empirical and theoretical contributions that examine how public policies actively shape, intensify or mitigate urban vulnerability, and how vulnerable populations respond to, negotiate, comply with or contest these policy interventions in their everyday lives. We also welcome work that critically examines the limits of existing policy frameworks (e.g., social protection, housing, climate resilience, informal economy) and explores the institutional, financial, and bureaucratic barriers and enablers to effective and actionable policy change.

This session builds on the earlier “Creative Vulnerabilities in Latin America” panel and workshop (RGS-IBG 2025) and seeks to move the debate further into public-policy grounds, bringing together theory and practice to reflect on policy design, implementation and lived outcomes.

paper.title: Urban Wellbeing at the Margins: Structural Barriers to Wellbeing-Oriented Urban Policy in Lima, Peru
Building infrastructure without infrastructure: exploring walking for transport in the disadvantaged Santiago de Chile
Knowledge Economies and Inequality: Rethinking Innovation-Led Growth in Medellín
Humanitarian Housing In Bogotá

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Heat-stress in and Beyond Humans: Critical Planetary Health Perspectives (2)
schedule_event.description:
Over 2.4 billion workers worldwide are exposed to excessive heat each year, resulting in nearly 19,000 deaths and tens of millions of occupational injuries and illnesses (ILO, 2025). Heat also affects more-than-human life, including the deaths of over 650,000 farm animals during the 2021 Pacific Northwest heatwave and thousands of chickens in India’s 2015 extreme heat events (Huber, 2022). These patterns position heat not simply as environmental exposure but as a lived health condition produced through intertwined climatic, ecological, and socio-economic transformations that unevenly distribute vulnerability across human and other-than-human life.

This panel foregrounds heat as an embodied and relational health experience. Rather than treating climate change as a distant environmental driver, we approach heat as something encountered through working bodies, homes, infrastructures, care practices, and everyday survival strategies. Heat reshapes hydration, fatigue, sleep, cognition, pregnancy outcomes, and chronic illness, while also altering patterns of disease transmission, microbial activity, and multispecies cohabitation. Health, in this sense, emerges not from isolated bodies but from entangled environments.

We place these dynamics in conversation with EcoHealth, One Health, and Planetary Health frameworks to examine how well-being and care are (re)produced across human and more-than-human relations (Cole & Deshpande, 2019; Hoogeveen et al., 2023). While these frameworks emphasize interconnected systems, lived experiences of heat reveal uneven burdens shaped by labour precarity, housing conditions, access to care, and social marginalisation. Occupational exposure often restricts possibilities for rest and recovery, making heat a workplace health and safety issue as well as a broader well-being concern (Kjellstrom et al., 2019).

At the same time, responses to the climate–health nexus frequently remain technocratic and depoliticised, privileging metrics and thresholds over lived experience (Sim & Parsons, 2025). We instead consider heat harms as ethical and relational conditions shaping care, responsibility, and vulnerability across species (Celermajer et al., 2025). This perspective foregrounds everyday coping practices, cultural understandings of heat, and community-based adaptations as central to health.

This panel invites papers that ask questions such as (but not limited to):

  • How is heat experienced as an embodied condition across human and more-than-human bodies in specific places and environments?
  • In what ways does heat and its related indicators reorganise labouring conditions and
    occupational health for working bodies, including vulnerable human labour groups and more-than-human workers such as livestock and working animals, particularly where exposure cannot easily be avoided?
  • What critical, methodological, or interdisciplinary interventions are needed to move beyond technocratic or Eurocentric framings of heat and health, thus moving towards multispecies just futures?

This session extended and exploratory conversation, with an aims to bring together scholars working across environmental, health, labour, and more-than-human geographies to think collectively about heat and its related forms as an embodied, relational, and multispecies condition, and to explore how more just planetary futures might be imagined under conditions of climate change.

This is an extension to Part 1 of Heat-stress in and Beyond Human/

paper.title: Heat, Labour, and the Politics of Habitability
Heatwaves and Multiple Precarities: Lessons from the Weathered Experiences of the Contracted, Outdoor Laborers Working at Calcutta Electric Supply Corporation in Kolkata

paper.title: Bodies and bricks in tandem: Weathering the more-than-human rhythms of brick moulding labour in Delhi

schedule_event.title: Population Geography Research Group Evening Social
schedule_event.description:
Join the committee of the Population Geography Research Group for an evening social, to meet others working in this area. 

PopGRG members will meet outside the Royal College of Music entrance on Prince Consort Road (opposite the steps leading to the Albert Hall).

We’ll head for something to eat and then for a drink (locations dependent on numbers/availability on the evening). A great, informal way to meet other population geographers.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Area Panel: The afterlives of geographical research
schedule_event.description:
What does it mean for research to ‘end’? Beyond the closing of a funding account, the archiving of a website and storage of data, and the move to some ‘new’ project, topic, or paper, what endures from research after it has ‘ended’? Whilst research training has long recognised, and sought to account for, the messy and often unpredictable nature of research, this has tended to foreground questions of research design, ethical conduct, and careful planning. The closing stages of the lifecycle of research have received less critical reflection, often being framed in terms of dissemination pathways, public engagement, and the longue durée of impact work and relationships. Yet the lingering after-effects and influences of geographical research in the world cannot be easily reduced to these pathways alone. This panel seeks to explore how research may end and how it may endure, what remains in the messy afterlives of projects that take unexpected turns after they have officially ‘ended’, and what ethical questions are raised when we take seriously the afterlives of research.

Drawing on Area’s work to advance discussions of ethics, methodological innovation, and the life cycle of research, this panel addresses these topics through discussion of the ‘afterlives’ of research. By ‘afterlives’ we point to not only how research is met, represented, and responded to in the world beyond academia, often in unanticipated and sometimes uncomfortable ways, but also to the question of whether, and how, research ‘ends’. Afterlives might encompass the unforeseen weaponisation of research for a range of interests contrary to expectations, the unanticipated return of past work, concepts, or findings to influence current issues, or of research taking on new directions and connections with civil society, business, or social groups unforeseen at the point of ‘doing’ the research. How do researchers account for and manage these relations, and how do the afterlives of research come to influence and effect research practice? Equally, the requirement of funding agencies and institutions to store research data, materials, and outputs in a range of repositories opens up questions over the future use of research materials. In a context of rapidly expanding AI engagement across the University sector, the question of how such repositories and materials can, and will, be used in the future is a pressing one.

The panel will explore the following questions:

  • How do geographers attend to the ‘afterlives’ of their research?
  • What are the ethical challenges that the ‘afterlives’ of research pose for geographers?
  • How are the ‘afterlives’ of research envisioned, planned for, and managed in the process of developing research? Is it possible, or even desirable, to plan for the ‘afterlife’ of research?
  • In a context of expanding AI integration, how can geographers navigate demands for data to be available for diverse and often unknown future uses?
  • What are the effects of the lives that our research takes on beyond academia on our experiences as geographers? What emotional and other investments are made in shaping the afterlives of research and how can we account for these?

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Language and the Languages of Geography Revisited
schedule_event.description:
It has been 25 years since the themed section ‘Geographies of Language/ Languages of Geography’ was published in Social and Cultural Geography (Desforges and Jones, 2001). Featuring papers from the RGS-IBG conference in 2000, it brought attention to the relationships between language, geography and identity and “the potential that a more sustained focus on languages may bring to geography” (Desforges and Jones, 2001). Since then, a wealth of scholarship on the Franco, Germanic, and, predominantly, Anglo, dominance of geographical knowledge has emerged (e.g. Desbiens and Ruddick, 2006; Kitchin, 2005; Mattissek and Glasze, 2016; Müller, 2021), considering the effects of the languages in which geographical knowledge is written, and how / where geographical knowledge is taught and circulated. However, geographical scholarship on the spatialities and social practices of language use and their relationships with identity, inclusion and power, have been more limited.

Yet, the spatial implications of languages include “the possibility [to] change the way that spaces are ordered” (Valentine, Sporton and Bang Nielsen, 2008), and thinking about language geographically can open new ways to “analyse the processes affecting language ability, use and transmission, as well as the association between such languages and various identities, representations, materialities and practices” (Jones and Lewis, 2019). This research area is only growing in importance due to globalisation and migration increasingly creating multilingual societies.

This session aims to bring together scholars working on the geographies of language and/or the languages of geography to take stock and revitalize discussion on the area, consider more recent and emerging scholarship on language within geography, and consider future avenues for geographical research on language.

References:

Desbiens, C., and Ruddick, S. (2006) Speaking of Geography: Language, Power, and the Spaces of Anglo-Saxon ‘Hegemony’, Environment & Planning D: Society & Space, 24(1): 1-8.

Desforges, L., and Jones, R. (2001) Geographies of Languages/Languages of Geography, Social & Cultural Geography, 2(3): 261-4.

Jones, R. and Lewis, H. (2019) New Geographies of Language: Language, Culture and Politics in Wales, London: Palgrave Macmillan.

Kitchin, R. (2005) Commentary: Disrupting and Destabilizing Anglo-American and English-Language Hegemony in Geography, Social & Cultural Geography, 6(1): 1-15

Mattissek, A., and Glasze, G. (2016) Discourse Analysis in German-language Human Geography: Integrating Theory and Method, Social & Cultural Geography, 17(1): 39-51.

Müller, M. (2021) Worlding Geography: From Linguistic Privilege to Decolonial Anywheres, Progress in Human Geography, 45(6): 1440-66.

Valentine, G., Sporton, D., and Bang Nielsen, K. (2008) Language Use on the Move: Sites of Encounter, Identities and Belonging, Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, 33(3): 376-87.

paper.title: Italian-Language Geographies of Polycrisis
Long Live the Bad Esch: The Role of Degrading Language in the  Formation of Urban Pathology and Spatial Governance in Esch-sur-Alzette, Luxembourg
A Language in Crisis, a Language for Crisis: Econarratives of Healing and Revitalisation of Gallo-Romance of Northern Italy
Immigrants, Language, and the Creation of Multicultural Spaces in the City
Foreword from the session organiser

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Geographies of Intensity (3)
schedule_event.description:
Intensity appears to be a problem today. The crisis prone present seems to be characterised by too much of the wrong sort of intensity. Claims are made that subjects are too outraged or too disaffected. Whilst some are baited to outrage amid today’s digital economy, others lie flat as they feel a pernicious combination of expectation and economic downturn. Extended periods of stretched out waiting for fragile promises to materialise (Pettit 2023), coexist with hustle cultures of constant activity (Thieme 2025). Calls to foster passionate attachment to work coexist with quiet quitting (Hong 2022). Politics amid the extended end of neoliberalism and as mediated by digital capitalism is characterised as being full of uneasy mixtures of outrage and boredom. Maladies of intensity – from burnout (Han 2015) to overstimulation – appear to serve as the signature affects for a present characterised as at once too much and too little, both too intense and not intense enough.

But in addition to being key to diagnosing the present conjuncture in all its overdetermined complexity, intensity can be found across multiple geographies. Value is created through generating intensity, whether in new digital worlds, through branding and hype, or via entertainment forms (e.g. the Blockbuster film). Capitalism has frequently been understood to involve a logic of intensification as value is extracted from life, labour and land. Political forms offer and promise intensity, whether the quietness and disaffection of liberalism or the rage infused enjoyment of right-wing populism (Anderson & Secor 2025). Forms of power work through intensity. Foucault (1977), for example, writes of how sovereignty works through ‘visible scenes of intensity’ as he details the spectacle of the scaffold. Events that are apprehended as crises are mediated through intensities that can change how they become present in everyday life. Mediated in terms of the catastrophic, climate change might be met with boredom and other disaffected feelings, for some subjects. The ‘immigration crisis’ gathers intensities around it, as authoritarian intensities are performed through mediated spectacle of violent raids targeting racialised subjects. At the same time, intensity is central to the lived experience of place. Whether the buzz of a city, the density of a crowd (McFarlane 2016) or stillness of a landscape, particular kinds of good intensity are governed, desired, valued, and experienced. This set of sessions explores the cultures, politics and economies of intensity.

paper.title: When care runs flat: the intensities of emotional labouring in service work
Imperfect Affective Infrastructures: Radical Housing, Inconvenience, and Political Intensity
Atmospheres and Intensities of Genocidal Weathering: Hypervigilance and Exhaustion in Gaza
From regime to machine: Intensive eliminatory velocities in Palestine
Intensity, pathology, burnout

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Chair's event: A conversation with Professor Jason Arday
schedule_event.description:
Join this 'fireside chat' between the Chair of Conference Professor Peter Hopkins and Professor Jason Arday, the 2002 Professorial Chair in the Sociology of Education at the University of Cambridge.

The youngest Black person ever appointed to a professorship at Cambridge, join this session to hear from, and ask questions about, Jason’s experiences of - and research about - race, inequality and education.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Chair's event: Queer and feminist perspectives toward just geographies
schedule_event.description:
Join this panel session to learn about important feminist and queer research that is exposing and confronting inequalities.

Focusing on how such inequalities are evident in our classrooms and lecture theatres, within our discipline, and within public spaces, this session draws attention to the challenges of addressing patriarchy and confronting the marginalisation and exclusion of queer, trans and non-binary people and knowledges in everyday institutional and public spaces.

Featuring Dr Rae Rosenberg (University of Edinburgh), Tilen Kolar (Leeds)
Dr Rachael Squires (Royal Holloway, University of London), Dr Kate Boyer (Cardiff University) and chaired by Dr Alessandro Boussalem (University of St Andrews).

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Geo Plenary Panel: AI and Climate
schedule_event.description:
The next few decades could be the most disruptive and consequential in human history, as environmental uncertainty and technological change intensify and interact. The existential threat posed by global heating is well documented; as UN Secretary General Antonio Gueterres put it in April 2022, we are 'firmly on track toward an unliveable world', making it 'now or never' for the planet. At the same time, governments and their populations are starting to wake up to the potential existential risks threatened by rapid and largely unregulated developments in AI technology, as well as the myriad challenges presented by the socio-economic transformations already occurring at significant environmental cost.

The interaction of these two threats is an issue of major importance in both scholarship and policy, yet the outcome remains an open question. Whilst more pessimistic analysts have warned that water and energy-intensive AI will further entrench existing inequalities and erode the social and environmental foundations of resilience, others have posited a brighter scenario. Could AI enable greater social mobility? Could it become more crisis-responsive, channeled towards innovations to address large-scale hazards and climate change-induced risk? In the eyes of some, each crisis may be the solution to the other, with AI enhancing human capacity to understand and handle the many challenges associated with a changing climate.

This keynote will explore the threats, challenges, and opportunities of AI in the context of environmental sustainability and climate breakdown. Is its environmental cost insurmountable, or is there hope of a sustainable future with AI as an active partner? Our keynote speakers will consider these questions and more.

schedule_event.title: IN-PERSON - Nuclear Latitudes: Positions on Landscape, Memory and the Invisible
schedule_event.description:
Nuclear landscapes are shaped as much by imaginaries—of progress, sacrifice, secrecy, and catastrophe—as by the material infrastructures they host. Yet these imaginaries often operate invisibly, abstracting nuclear processes from the places and communities that bear their consequences. This performance-led panel gathers artist-researchers whose work across sound, film, photography, text, curatorial practice, and site-specific intervention makes these invisible forces knowable. The panel foregrounds artistic practice as geographical method—equipped to render visible and audible what dominant modes of knowledge production leave unseen: the slow, embodied, and materially entangled.

Convened by Jay Needham, Louise K Wilson, and Frederike Lange, the group of artist-researchers share a commitment to situated knowledge and nuclear placemaking as  subject and method. Their practices treat nuclear sites as constitutive space- places where memory, power, and ecology are produced. Collectively, they consider how issues of contested geographies and justice influenced their practices as well as the sites they have researched and inhabited.

Through the affective capacity of the arts, the panel will engage with how nuclear imaginaries produce spatiotemporalities—zones, and places, and how these are interpreted, sustained, and challenged through artistic practice.

Louise K. Wilson and Jay Needham create video and sound-based media art that reflects on nuclear imaginaries through post-memory and auto-ethnographic site visits. Each has dedicated their careers to the creation of performances and exhibitions that harness the capacity of art to express the complex aesthetic and cultural relations of toxic legacies both former and present.

Frederike Lange’s curatorial and research practice centres on visualizing the legacies of extractive industries. Her recent project on uranium mining heritage in the German-Czech borderlands combined exhibition-making, oral history, and artistic positions to trace nuclear pasts into the present.

Gair Dunlop works in text, photography, and video, documentary forms through an artist’s eye to surface remnants of a fantasy military-scientific-pastoral nation. His practice features collaboration and critical-cultural interrogations of Britain’s nuclear imaginary.

Etsuko Ichikawa’s contemplative film Echo at Satsop, filmed inside a nuclear cooling tower, creates a space where memory and place  can be experienced. Shaped by the histories of Hiroshima and Fukushima across generations, her work resists explicit declaration in favour of open encounter.

Harry Willis Fleming owns Bervie Brow Research Station, a former RAF ROTOR station and Cold War bunker in Scotland, secret and off-limits for seventy years. Now converted into a space for artists, it serves as an arena for working with nuclear environments and imagined futures.

Ken Hollings presents Terminal Desert, a lecture tracing how atomic tests in the American Southwest transformed physical terrain into symbolic wastelands. From 1950s creature features to irradiated nightmares, Hollings reveals how horror thrives in relentless, unforgiving sunlight.

Together, these contributions position artistic research as a mode of inquiry capable of surfacing what dominant nuclear narratives render invisible: the slow violence of contamination, the persistence of Cold War spatial logics, and the embodied experiences of communities living with and against nuclear infrastructure. The panel argues that art doesn't merely represent nuclear places but actively intervenes in how they are known, remembered, and imagined.

paper.title: Ken Hollings
Etsuko Ichikawa
Dr. Louise K. Wilson
Jay Needham
Dr. Harry Willis Fleming
Gair Dunlop
Frederike Lange

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - From Displacement to Dispossession: Carceral Urbanisms and the Politics of Banishment in Contemporary Europe
schedule_event.description:
This panel invites critical geographers to interrogate contemporary regimes of banishment proliferating across European cities. From the intensification and normalisation of border controls to the routinised violation of human rights; from the emergence of new forms of right-wing violence to the recrudescence of authoritarian populisms, European cities have become central infrastructures through which racialised exclusion and abjection are produced and governed. Urban space functions not merely as a backdrop but as a key technology in the manufacture and management of intersecting emergencies, carceral logics, and necropolitics.

The concept of racial banishment, as foregrounded in the seminal work of Ananya Roy (2019) and others, provides a critical entry point into these dynamics. Roy’s theorisation highlights the central role of the state and the constitutive place of race in the shift from displacement to dispossession. Banishment names not only the racial-colonial histories and dispossessive political economies of dwelling, but also Eurocentric and white supremacist regimes of the human articulated through the urban itself—a dispossession of personhood that sustains racial capitalism, mediated by anticipatory urban logics of punishment, peripheralisation, and premature death. At the same time, cities remain contested terrains in which practices of refusal, survival, and insurgent inhabitation continue to emerge against and within these violent conditions.

As this panel aims to explore, the analytic of banishment foregrounds the historical and material relations that exceed its governing logics, raising as Roy argues, urgent epistemological and ontological questions for radical geography. How might we trace the genealogies, spatialities, and materialities through which banishment is actually (dis)assembled in everyday life? Setting in conversation European and North American cities at this moment, the panel poses an urgent political question concerning the position of cities vis-à-vis national politics: what might an affirmative urban political project look like amid national (and supranational) regimes of banishment?

These are questions that not only speak directly to the Conference’s focus on spatial inequalities and injustices but also foreground the development of spatial grammars that point to the making of more just and sustainable worlds.

paper.title: Carceral continuums in the urban space: The racialized exclusion of undocumented people in Turin, Italy.
Resisting Racial Banishment at the Northern French Border
After Decampment/Démantèlement: Racial banishment, dispossession and habitation at city’s end
Affirmative Geographies and Communities of Method: Narrating a Counter-Athens Beyond Banishment

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Micro-Geographies of Spatial Injustice: (Im)mobility and Tourist Practices between Hyper-Full and Hyper-Empty Urban Landscapes
schedule_event.description:
Urban public space is increasingly shaped by sharp micro-geographies of inequality. In many cities both of the Global North and the Global South, public squares, streets, sidewalks, and crossroads are poised between hyper-full and hyper-empty spatial regimes.

On the one hand, with the growing role of private actors in shaping forms and functions of public spaces, some central urban areas become increasingly saturated by tourism, consumption-related activities, and neoliberal strategies that foster aestheticisation and homogenization, as well as generating dense atmospheres in which visibility, access, and mobility are shaped by commercial and tourism-driven logics. Although not always conceived for tourism-related activities, big-scale urban regeneration programs or small-scale projects such as street pedestrianization often end up with fostering new flows of people, causing touristification or overtourism.

On the other hand, peripheral districts are increasingly marked by infrastructural scarcity and spatial degrade, with wide “empty” spaces provided with insufficient street furnishings, weak lighting, lacking services, and minimal social infrastructures. Paradoxically, these voids act as barriers in themselves, generating forms of exclusion that are as material as those produced by overcrowding. In these districts, tourism practices play an ambivalent role, alternatively seen both as a tool of local development, especially within off-the-beaten-track itineraries, and a exclusionary instrument of further segregation that selectively make visible marginal areas trhough exactrivist approaches of slum tourism.

Thus, this dialectics between void and empty, over and under used, fonctionally hyper-connoted and deprived spaces, hyperturistified and undertouristified can exacerbate micro-geographies of inequality at the urban scale, producing an increasingly fragmented urban patchwork where disparities are intensified and spatially entrenched.

This session investigates how these contrasting spatial conditions shape everyday experiences of spatial injustice, uneven urban citizenship, and tourist practices as well as what are the emerging practices of adaptation or resistance. The focus is especially on bodies navigating margins (women, children/the elderly, gender-nonconforming people, people with disabilities, racialised subjects etc.) whose mobility patterns and visibility in public spaces are differently constrained and shaped by overcrowded touristified cores and infrastructural voids.

We invite contributions that analyse how such micro-geographies emerge, how they are negotiated, and how they reproduce (or challenge) contemporary urban power structures.

Possible themes include (but are not limited to):

•⁠ ⁠Inclusive/exclusionary Walk-ability and socio-spatial inequalities: spaces, patterns and methods of analysis

•⁠ ⁠Overtourism/undertourism

•⁠ ⁠Dialectics of hyper-full and hyper-empty spaces in the contemporary city

•⁠ ⁠Dialectics Global North/Global South; Centre/Periphery and their differences in terms of access and people mobility

•⁠ ⁠Dialectics between formal and informal urbanism in shaping new forms of mobility and emerging everyday practices

•⁠ ⁠Spatial injustice and everyday practices of resistance

•⁠ ⁠Gendered and embodied experiences of fear and safety

•⁠ ⁠Barriers and limits: from infrastructural scarcity to sensory atmospheres

•⁠ ⁠Neoliberal place-making and the aesthetic governance of central districts

•⁠ ⁠Peripheral emptiness as a form of exclusion or “slow violence”

•⁠ ⁠Methodological approaches to studying micro-geographies of mobilities (walking methods, sensory ethnographies, visual analysis, participatory mapping, etc.)

•⁠ ⁠Comparative perspectives across different national/urban contexts

•⁠ ⁠Creative, performative, or activist practices of resistance

paper.title: Visible in tourism, invisible in emergencies: communicative inequalities and hydrogeological risk in Sicily (Italy)

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Towards just temporalities
schedule_event.description:
This asynchronous panel emerges from our shared reflection that spatial justice cannot be achieved without our sustained attention to time. Unequal temporal regimes that prioritise productivity and efficiency in both land and bodies function as central mechanisms through which spatial inequities are produced, normalised, and maintained (Sharma, 2015). These temporal structures actively produce and organise space, shaping whose bodies can dwell, work, and participate. Building on this reflection, this panel asks how geographers might think with embodied queer, crip, and feminist methodologies to organise spaces for participation and knowledge exchange that move beyond extractive and resource-based temporalities.

Drawing on recent geographical engagements with crip temporalities (Evans et al., 2025; Boyd, 2025; Budworth, 2025; Senanayake, 2022), we examine how productivity and efficiency (Gregg, 2018; Chapman, 2023) structure institutional spaces (e.g. conferences) while also underpinning broader landscapes of public space, mobility, and access. Conceptually, we engage with notions such as “un-time” (Han, 2018); “crip time” (Kafer, 2013); “slow scholarship” (Mountz et al, 2015); “rest as resistance” (Hersey, 2022), ‘unwellness” (Khuc, 2024), “stop-time as crip time” (Dokumaci, 2018), and “strange-ing time” (Bradley, 2025) to understand how alternative temporalities can inform debates and practices of academic participation and inclusion. Methodologically, we follow feminist and queer scholarship that treats method as performative to explore how to enact just temporalities (Bjork, C., & Buhre, 2021) within the conference itself, experimenting with layered and differently paced forms of knowledge exchange. As such, we position the asynchronous format of the panel not only as an accessibility accommodation but as a methodological intervention to trouble dominant academic temporal expectations of immediacy, speed, and presence-as-value.

Bringing together perspectives from queer and crip ecologies, critical feminist geographies, disability studies, and creative and design-based approaches, we open up a series of questions:

  • How do extractive temporalities shape who can participate in spaces of knowledge production, academic life, and community organising?
  • Whose labour sustains dominant spatio-temporal arrangements of spaces of knowledge production and community organising?
  • When and why did productivity and efficiency become dominant organising logics of modern life, and how have they been used to shape notions of disability and illness?
  • What are non-normative temporalities alternatives, and who has access to them? Who has not? Who can “slow down” (Fung, 2025)?
  • How might crip time and just temporalities extend across research, teaching, and other forms of academic labour?
  • How might creative practices make these temporal norms visible and open them to revision?

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Everyday Practices and Public Life in Urban China (1)
schedule_event.description:
Cities in China are in the midst of a profound, ongoing transition. Intertwined with macroeconomic and demographic shifts, this transition is grounded in changing urban landscapes and deeply felt in the rhythms of urban life. It is reshaping people's aspirations—regarding work, family, gender and ageing—also their everyday practices and embodied experiences, from evolving cultures of consumption and leisure to new ways of spending time, seeking connection, and building relationships in the city. In this sense, public space provides a compelling site for examining everyday urban life under these conditions, where multi-layered changes, challenges, encounters, and coexistence are juxtaposed, negotiated, reconfigured and lived.

This session seeks to open a conversation about how to study, think, and write about Urban China through the lens of everyday practices and public life. It brings together interdisciplinary and empirically grounded research that foregrounds ordinary, mundane, and often overlooked dimensions of everyday life through which individuals navigate and negotiate urban change. By centring these lived experiences, the session demonstrates how close attention to everyday life generates conceptual insights and opens up methodological possibilities for urban geography.

paper.title: Negotiating the Streets: Exploring Cyclists' Embodied Social Interactions in Shenzhen, China
Platform-Constituted Belonging: Foreign Residents, Digital Public Space and Urban Membership in Guangzhou
Friendship, family, and women in Zhuhai: thinking beyond guanxi in a Chinese city
Keeping Things Going: Public Life Beyond Co-presence in Community Gardens

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - What cities does the Belt and Road lead to? Future directions and critical questions at the nexus of Global China, infrastructure and urbanism
schedule_event.description:
Twenty-first century urbanism is being fundamentally reshaped by the reconfiguration of global capital flows, geopolitical realignments, and new infrastructural networks that challenge Western-led development paradigms. China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) has emerged as perhaps the most consequential infrastructure-led development strategy of our time, producing what scholars have termed a "global force of urban transformation" (Apostolopoulou et al., 2024). Yet as the BRI enters a new phase—marked by intensifying US-China rivalry, shifting European approaches to Chinese capital, and starkly differentiated local responses ranging from enthusiastic embrace in Serbia and Hungary to recent reversals in Argentina—the theoretical and empirical tools for understanding BRI urbanism demand critical reassessment. How China "arrives" differs dramatically across places, yet scholarship has only begun to unpack the mechanisms, actors, and infrastructures through which these differentiated geographies are produced.

This session engages with what we term the "urbanisation, localisation, and infrastructurisation" of the BRI and Global China agenda. Building on recent calls to understand the BRI as fundamentally an urban project (Shin et al., 2022; Andujar et al., 2024) and infrastructure as a key site for theorising contemporary urbanisation (Kanai and Schindler, 2022; Schindler et al., 2022), the session seeks to advance both theoretical frameworks and empirical research at the nexus of BRI/Global China, infrastructure, and urbanism. The session embraces contributions that move beyond geopolitical and macro-economic analyses to examine how the BRI materialises locally—through what infrastructures, involving what actors, producing what forms of urbanism, and with what consequences for governance, labour, and everyday life. It welcomes disciplinary perspectives from across geography's broad field with the aim of generating new vocabularies and comparative insights for understanding infrastructure-led urbanisation in an era of shifting global power.

The session will engage with the following themes: 

Infrastructure and Urbanisation — What infrastructures are driving BRI-inspired urbanism? How do digital, financial, transport, logistics, and cultural infrastructures interact to reshape urban spaces? 

Geopolitics, Capital, and Local Responses — How are changing geopolitical conditions reshaping the landscape of Chinese investments? What is the relationship between openness or closure to Chinese capital and local political formations? How do cities across respond to Chinese infrastructure investment?

Actors, Governance, and Agency —  What actors are involved in BRI urbanism, and what is at stake for different stakeholders? What coalitions emerge, and what forms of local agency are exercised?

Financial Mechanisms and Development Trajectories — What financial mechanisms drive BRI urbanism? How do investment strategies, public-private partnerships, and capital flows shape urban development trajectories? 

Global Geographies and Comparative Perspectives — What is the global geography of BRI urbanism? How can comparative research help us understand the particularities of how Chinese infrastructure investment impacts different regions and localities? What patterns emerge across cases, and what context-specific dynamics demand attention?

Theoretical Concepts and Methodological Advances — What new theoretical frameworks are needed to conceptualise BRI urbanism and its relationship to infrastructure-led development? How might the urbanisation, localisation, and infrastructurisation of the Global China agenda be better theorised? What methodological approaches can advance our understanding of these processes?

schedule_event.title: NO SESSION - workshop set up
schedule_event.description: The Sensory Lives project aims to better understand how homeless families with neurodivergent children experience living in Temporary Accommodation. In this workshop, learn about and experience a multi-sensory playhouse tent designed to communicate children’s lived experiences of B&Bs, hotels, and other forms of Temporary Accommodation. The playhouse tent hopes to re-centre children’s perspectives in discussions of homelessness and communicate neurological difference more inclusively in research and policy. Please attend our session in timeslot 4!

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Marginality and question of (in)equality and justice.
schedule_event.description:
The mission of the IGU Commission is to research marginality and the processes of marginalisation from different perspectives and with a geographical basis. The main focus is to better understand multi-scalar relations between the globalisation process and how geographical marginality evolves at the local and regional levels. Moreover, we seek to improve our understanding of local and regional responses to different forms of marginality and marginalisation processes.

This aligns very closely with the Conference’s 2026 theme, namely: Geographies of inequalities: toward just places, in that our Commission’s work focusses on spatial, social and economic differences and inequalities across geographical space and potential interventions to address such disparities in a just manner.

To this end, we have compiled a set of papers which we propose to present at the conference in 2026, centred on the shared topic of inequalities and marginalisation/de-marginalisation. The papers demonstrate the existing variety of marginality and marginalisation facets, whether social and/or spatial, encompassing rural, urban, and in-between places, as well as environmental (in)justice leading to marginalisation of groups. The selection also highlights different, and perhaps the most dominant, encounters with marginality in various places and regions across the world. We have organised the proposed papers and topics according to their main focus: social inequalities and marginalisation and environmental (in)justice, which mostly concern mixed social and cultural groups and the resulting marginalisation, and the underlying processes of marginalisation and environmental (in)justice and (attempts at) de-marginalisation in urban and rural places.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Geographies of inequalities and public policy for urban vulnerable populations in Latin America (1)
schedule_event.description:
The lives of vulnerable populations in Latin American cities are shaped by deeply rooted social, economic and political inequalities. This session seeks to advance a collaborative and policy-oriented conversation about how public policy can enable more just urban environments for those experiencing vulnerability and living with disadvantages. Building on scholarship about urban informality, everyday resilience and (creative) responses to marginalisation (Fundación IDEA, 2017; Fernández et al., 2023), we welcome empirical and theoretical contributions that examine how public policies actively shape, intensify or mitigate urban vulnerability, and how vulnerable populations respond to, negotiate, comply with or contest these policy interventions in their everyday lives. We also welcome work that critically examines the limits of existing policy frameworks (e.g., social protection, housing, climate resilience, informal economy) and explores the institutional, financial, and bureaucratic barriers and enablers to effective and actionable policy change.

This session builds on the earlier “Creative Vulnerabilities in Latin America” panel and workshop (RGS-IBG 2025) and seeks to move the debate further into public-policy grounds, bringing together theory and practice to reflect on policy design, implementation and lived outcomes.

schedule_event.description:
Delegates participating to the conference are welcome to join and hear about GLTRG's initiatives and collaboration opportunities.

During this event, the Committee will present the outcome of the activities from August 2025 to August 2026 as well as run votes for the appointment of Committee Members (information to the registered members of the GLTRG will be circulated in due course).

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Disability and Cycling: critical reflections, lived experiences, policy and practice
schedule_event.description:
This session will launch, share ideas from, and respond to, a forthcoming 2026 Special Issue of the Open Access journal Active Travel Studies on Cycling and Disability. This contains over twenty contributions ranging from more traditional long form academic articles to lived experience pieces and policy-focused case studies, broadly sharing a critical approach to active travel rooted in disability studies. Perspectives in the Special Issue come from authors inside and outside academic and across the world (e.g. Indonesia, Japan, Ireland, Australia), with topics including, for example, reflections on racialised exclusions of Disabled cyclists, quantifying and mapping barriers to Disabled cyclists’ use of the cycle network, and analysis of experiences of cycling as a Disabled family group.

To make this session more accessible, it will be hybrid will incorporate short pre-recorded contributions, for instance from lived experience authors unable to attend the conference, who have expressed interest in their voices being heard through such a contribution. The panel will include in-person contributors to the special issue and session co-organisers, who will speak to the themes of the special issue, and bring different perspectives (academics, Disabled cyclists, practitioners and policy-makers, etc.) into conversation. Those attending the session will gain insight into the latest work in this under-researched but rapidly developing field, including crucially the perspectives of Disabled people who cycle or who would like to cycle.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Engaging inequalities and injustice issues towards developing just educational spaces within and outside institutions
schedule_event.description:
This session foregrounds education as a spatial and social practice, and geography education as a potential vehicle to achieve social justice. Formal and informal educational spaces, such as classrooms, universities, digital platforms, museums, streets, and community organisations, are inherently geographically significant. These spaces are shaped by uneven power relations, institutional structures, cultural traditions and material conditions, constituting critical sites where inequalities are reproduced, negotiated, or resisted.

Geographical research has extensively explored inequalities related to migration, climate change, development, health and geopolitics. However, the spatial politics of teaching and learning remain comparatively under-theorised within mainstream geographical debates. In other words, the role of geography in shaping public sayings and doings of equality and justice remains marginalised.

We argue that conventional linear education models are increasingly insufficient to enable learners to critically engage with complex and intertwined knowledges of equality and justice (see: Kim, 2019; Sundaresan, 2020; Simm, Marvell & Mellor, 2021; Lambert, Béneker, Bladh & Mitchell, 2025). At the same time, the lack of communication and transformation between academic geography, school geography and public geographical imaginaries has raised urgent questions about who are able to access to geographical knowledge, whose voices are heard, and what role geography can play to make a difference beyond institutions.

This session invites contributions that (re-)conceptualise educational spaces as contested geographies and explore how geography (as disciplines, pedagogy, and public practice) can advocate for developing just spaces and places. It especially seeks the works that bridge research, teaching, academic activism, and public engagement, amplifying emerging or marginalised voices.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Critical Muslim Geographies: Trajectories and Research Pathways (2)
schedule_event.description:
Disquiet with the dominant framing of integration and difference in the existing body of work on the geographies of Muslims has, in recent years, led to emerging scholarly debates on Critical Muslim Geographies and related studies (Allouache, 2023; Fernandez, 2025, Sidaway, 2022). This call for a panel session invites pithy contributions and reflections that consider the prospects for advancing Critical Muslim Geographies and engage with the insights, challenges, thresholds, and intellectual solidarities to shape manifestos for this developing field (Sidaway, 2023). While there is a breadth of literature on Muslims in geography, we call to expand the depth and range of theoretical work (not least from within Islamic scholarship), as well empirical sites and experiences that inform Muslim geographies, thereby transcending thematic areas of focus that too often simply render “Muslim” as an adjective of diversity across space (Allouache, 2023). We share the ambition that being able to think of the world from “perspectives of the mosque from the inside looking out” (Ahmed, 2018, p. 206), rather than from the outside looking in, has potential to change the treatment of Islam and Muslims in geography.

Panelists will recharge and move beyond established decolonial geographies by forging connections with adjacent literatures and other intellectual traditions. The panel discussion may be theoretical, historically oriented, methodological, or empirically grounded, and will contribute to advancing Critical Muslim geographies by engaging the following vectors:

  • Critical geography and Critical Muslim geography and geographers (capital, class, coloniality, difference, discourse, power, and racialisation, and the uneven and oft-contested production of landscapes, places, and spaces)
  • Securityscapes, surveillance, control, and biopolitics
  • Geographies of AI, STS, technopolitics, and Islam
  • Geographies of Islamophobia; wider geographical analysis of “everyday” (Najib, 2021), historical and political Islamophobias
  • Labour; Islam and the city, the politics of homes, work, workplaces, sites of worship, umrah and haj
  • Muslim environmentalisms and political ecologies
  • Histories and development of geographical thought minding and thinking with traditions of Islamic knowledge and learning
  • Decolonial pathways in the interface between geography, Islam and area studies
  • Research methodologies, ethics and ways of fully realising Critical Muslim geographies’ potential (Intellectual and research community-building)

The session is co-sponsored by the History and Philosophy of Geography Research Group (HPGRG), the Race, Culture and Equality Working Group (RACE), and the Geographies of the Middle East and North Africa Research Group (MENA-GEOG).

***

REFERENCES

Ahmed, A.-A. (2018). The Other Ethical Approval: The Importance of Being “Islamic”. Fieldwork in Religion, 12(2), 204-222.

Allouache, Y. A. (2023). Muslim geographies, positionality, and ways of knowing migration. Area, 55(3), 381-389.

Allouache, Y. A. (2024). Makkah's hinterworlds: Jeddah, the Indian Ocean Hajj, and Southeast Asia. Singapore Journal of Tropical Geography, 45(2). 403-412.

Fernandez, S. (2025) Networks of securitisation in the academy: The role of friendship, solidarity and radical geographies. Area, 00, e70051.

Najib, K. (2021). Spatialized Islamophobia. Routledge.

Sidaway, J. D. (2022). Beyond the decolonial: Critical Muslim geographies. Dialogues in Human Geography, 13(3), 337-358.

Sidaway, J. D. (2023). A manifesto for critical Muslim geographies. Dialogues in Human Geography, 13(3), 387-391.

schedule_event.title: Energy Geographies Research Group Lunchtime Social
HYBRID - Violent Transitions: Environmental Crime, Energy Justice, and Resistance in Latin America
schedule_event.description:
Latin America's pivotal role in the global energy transition—hosting the "Lithium Triangle", vast wind corridors, and extensive hydropower networks—has generated a disturbing parallel phenomenon: the proliferation of environmental crimes committed in the name of sustainability. As multinational corporations and state actors accelerate renewable energy development across the continent, they are perpetrating systematic violations against Indigenous and rural communities that expose the violent underbelly of the "green economy".

This panel examines how environmental crimes are embedded within energy transition projects in Latin America, engaging with green criminology, political ecology, and decolonial perspectives. We focus on the criminal dimensions of renewable energy development: from the de facto theft of water and land through lithium brine extraction in Chile, Argentina, and Bolivia, to the violent repression of anti-dam movements in Colombia and Mexico, to the criminalisation of Indigenous leaders resisting wind farms in Oaxaca and La Guajira.

Drawing on emerging scholarship in environmental crime and violence (Goyes et al., 2017; Scheidel et al., 2020), this session interrogates how state-corporate alliances use legal and extra-legal mechanisms to secure access to territories and resources for "green" extraction. We examine how paramilitary groups have emerged around hydroelectric dam construction in Colombia (Martínez & Castillo, 2016); how lithium mining generates "green grabbing" that replicates colonial extractivism (Mejía-Muñoz & Babidge, 2023); and how wind energy projects trigger police repression and death threats against community defenders (Business & Human Rights Resource Centre, 2022).

The panel asks: How do energy transition projects function as sites of environmental criminality? What mechanisms enable state violence, corporate impunity, and the criminalisation of dissent? What actors—such as police forces—should be incorporated in the analysis of energy transitions? How do communities resist these green crimes, and what alternative frameworks of energy justice are emerging?

We invite papers engaging with: (1) Lithium and copper extraction, including water theft in the Atacama, Salta/Jujuy, and Uyuni; corporate regulatory capture; and criminalisation of Indigenous water defenders. (2) Hydropower violence, including paramilitary activities, extrajudicial killings of activists, and forced displacement. (3) Wind and solar projects, examining police repression, death threats against leaders, and "green grabbing" violating FPIC. (4) Theoretical frameworks conceptualising "energy crimes" and "green violence", exploring intersections of environmental crime, coloniality, and extractivism, or comparing renewable energy crimes with palm oil, mining, and forestry. (5) Resistance and justice, including community-led documentation, the Escazú Agreement, Indigenous energy sovereignty, and gendered environmental violence.

paper.title: Environmental protection and sustainable development: An Analysis of the Lithium Exploration Project in the Vale do Jequitinhonha and Mucuri
Critical Materials and Green Crimes: Slow Violence and the Making of Sacrifice Zones in  Chile’s Copper–Lithium Frontier
A critical criminology of green extractivism
Violent Transitions: Naming Structural Violence in Latin America's Renewable Energy Projects
Feminised ecologies and Hyper-masculine Infrastructure - Extractivism and Nation building in Kashmir.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Urban Creative Clusters and Upscaling of Socio-Spatial Exclusion in the Global South (1)
schedule_event.description:
The Global South is undergoing a cultural resurgence in a contradictory frame of both decolonisation and recolonisation. Start-up hubs, heritage precincts, film cities, and design parks based on regional and national cultural themes are promoted as creative clusters for economic growth, innovation and global competitiveness, and as symbolic representations of cultural pride. Modernist projects like Dashanzi Art District of Beijing (Yin, 2014), Porto Maravilha (Rio de Janeiro) (Broudehoux,, 2017), Seongsu-dong and Seoul's creative neighbourhoods of South Korea (Chang, 2023), Medellín’s social urbanism and cultural infrastructure of Colombia (Guerra, 2014), and Marina Bay of Singapore (Micheli, 2023) are also known to have led to adverse and exclusionary development. Such initiatives are linked to national urban policy frameworks (Roy, 2009). In many countries, in addition to focused programmes for heritage conservation, redevelopment and place-based regeneration, a ‘retrofitting’ framework under the Smart City infrastructure modernisation mission is used to this end. However, critical scholarship indicates that such an attempt to reconfigure culture and sites through the imagination of the past for reproducing historical social memories can also yield uneven socio-economic outcomes (Molho, 2020). The demolition and displacement of informal settlements and shops, the sanitisation of places from the poor and ‘cultural others’, and ‘selective’ heritage conservation and promotion are frequently observed across Africa, Eastern and South Asia, and Latin America. Smith (2006) calls this ‘authorised heritage discourse’, implying the promotion of official narratives on what should count as heritage (Appadurai, 1996). Creative clusters, as such, may reinforce inequalities based on class, caste, gender, and ethnicity rather than address them (El-Husseiny, 2020; Peck, 2005; Pratt, 2011).

This session invites papers based on empirical case studies, comparative studies (South and North, or South-South) and conceptual contributions on how creative cluster projects operate within countries of the Global South. In other words, the papers should foreground the urban realities of the Global South and should address, though not limited to, any of the questions of (a) How do urban planning and policy frameworks (like smart city) shape the heritage and creative clusters and urban spaces? (b) How does the development of creative clusters lead to demolitions, displacements, gentrification, and socio-spatial exclusions? (C) How do social and cultural belongings, and informality, negotiate their access to the creative clusters and their economies? (d) What forms of alternative policies and planning can help in socio-economically just and inclusive creative urban development?

Papers focusing on metropolitan cities, heritage towns/cities, and small and secondary cities are especially encouraged.

Keywords: Creative cluster, heritage, urban policies, exclusion, Global South

Instructions for Contributors/Authors

If you would like to contribute a paper, please submit an abstract (400 words maximum) with 5-6 keywords to shaban@tiss.ac.in or submit online using the links given above for the type of sessions you want to participate in.

At the end of the abstract, please include a short biography (including the full name of the author(s), position, institutional affiliations, and email address). In case you have any questions, please do write to shaban@tiss.ac.in
