schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Research with, not on: A Workshop on Engaged Scholarship on the Geographies of Labour and Technology
schedule_event.description:
Research at the intersection of labour and technology has flourished in recent years, drawing attention to the exponential growth in precarious labour in the digital economy. Simultaneously, labour movements have erupted across geographies, sectors, and class positions to shape, contest, and even reverse its growth trajectories. Yet, despite these parallel developments, these movements and the scholarship around them remain disconnected, rarely intersecting in sustained or generative ways. Intervening in this disconnect, this session proposes an interactive workshop for critical geographers to collectively reflect on our roles within contemporary labour struggles in the digital economy. Specifically, this session will create a space for critical dialogue about engaged and non-extractive methodological practice at the intersections of technology, labour, and the geographies of work.

Central to our discussion is the question of how scholars, working within structures of the neoliberal university, can extend radical solidarity to labour movements within the digital economy. We will examine the complexities of privilege and precarity across different geographical contexts, exploring how positionality shapes solidarity politics and the possibilities for meaningful praxis. We will explore principles of non-extractive research practice and strategies for building long-term relationships that challenge institutional complicity while supporting movement work.

Facilitated by two PhD researchers drawing on their experiences of engaged research with platform worker unions in India, this interactive co-production workshop invites scholar-activists undertaking engaged projects to reflect collectively on our practice. Using hands-on materials such as maps, videos, photos, pamphlets, social media content, the workshop will prompt collaborative reflection on participants' own research trajectories. Participants will work in small groups, selecting from pre-identified topics and guiding questions that foreground the practical, political, and ethical dilemmas of scholar-activism.

Rather than producing prescriptive "best practices" or toolkits that risk flattening complexities or reproducing epistemic violence, the workshop will work towards two outcomes. First, participants will collaboratively draft a research manifesto or statement of principles articulating shared commitments for politically engaged scholarship. Second, we will initiate a post-conference writing and dialogue network to support participants in conducting engaged research, and to develop the workshop’s reflections into collaborative outputs, extending this work beyond the conference setting.

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - What does Gender Equality, Disability and Social Inclusion mean for a just energy transition? An exploration from feminist and decolonial perspectives
schedule_event.description:
Current policy thinking acknowledges that any efforts to advance a just energy transition must incorporate Gender Equality, Disability, and Social Inclusion (GEDSI) concerns. Empirical evidence, however, shows a persistent gender and inclusion gap in the energy transition. This gap manifests in the limited participation of women and gender non-conforming people in the sustainable energy labour force, as well as in the limited knowledge of how gender relations and intersecting forms of social discrimination (such as racism or ableism) reproduce energy injustices in the energy transition.

Feminist research provides practical insights to deliver a just, sustainable, and inclusive energy transition, yet it also calls to challenge the very principles that inform policy efforts in the energy sector. Many energy projects that incorporate GEDSI concerns, for example through gender-disaggregated data collection or holding single-sex learning sessions, rarely tackle the root causes of discrimination and social inequality. Many projects focus on differences between men and women without questioning the homogeneous, universal categories used to characterise diverse groups and complex experiences of power relations and exclusion. Over-simplifying the relationships between gender relations, discrimination, and access to energy resources leads to decontextualised, inappropriate actions (such as when cookstove improvement programmes make inaccurate assumptions about cooking practices and fuel choices). Such generalisations portray women as passive victims or virtuous stewards in ways that increase their responsibility for delivering collective action without the corresponding rewards (such as when biogas-cooking programmes seek to ‘empower’ women but inadvertently result in additional domestic labour).

In this session we interrogate the components of a GEDSI-transformative approach for a just energy transition, with reference to experiences in Ethiopia, Malawi, Mozambique and Tanzania. The project identifies three areas of action: implementation actions that examine GEDSI barriers in practice, processes of institutional change, and capacity building programmes that tackle directly the gender gap through affirmative action.

paper.title: Mapping Power: An Institutional Ethnography Framework for Advancing Gender Equality, Disability, and Social Inclusion in Energy Transitions
Whose Experiences Matter? Gender, Equality, Disability and Social Inclusion in Energy Curricula
Whose energy transition? Mapping electric cooking through a GEDSI lens in Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania and Ethiopia
Understanding GEDSI barriers in accessing energy and engineering education for delivering a Just Energy Transition: Insights from Ethiopia, Malawi, Mozambique, and Tanzania
Just energy transitions and the need for inclusive energy access innovation ecosystems

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Contested Inclusions: Platform Power, (In)Visibility and Digital Inequalities
schedule_event.description:
Digital inclusion is frequently presented as a matter of connectivity, skills, or technological access (van Dijk, 2020) enabled by the democratisation promised by digital technologies. Such framings obscure the extent to which inclusion is increasingly organised through platform infrastructures and algorithmic (in)visibility regimes (Petre et al., 2019; Glatt, 2022) that actively structure who is able to participate, whose labour is rendered valuable, and which forms of engagement remain marginal or extractable. Across sectors ranging from governance (Boulianne, 2018) and environmental action (Feroz & Chiravuri, 2021) to gambling and digital play (Macey & Hamari, 2024), migration (Zou & Deng, 2022), health (Ahmed et al., 2025), creative economies (Pererva & Mazorenko, 2025) and cultural production (Poell et. al., 2022), digital platforms have become central mechanisms through which longstanding global and local inequalities are re-worked.

This session invites contributions that examine digital inclusion as a contested process shaped by political economy, design, and institutional power. We are particularly interested in research tracing how platform business models, interface standards, behavioural incentives, and governance arrangements embed presumptions about inclusion while distributing risks, responsibilities, and rewards unevenly across regions and populations. These dynamics structure access to citizenship, care, cultural participation, mobility, and economic opportunity along intersecting lines of gender, class, race, dis/ability, citizenship, and geographies, often reinforcing asymmetries between sites of data extraction, sites of platform ownership, and sites of digital labour.

By bringing interdisciplinary perspectives into dialogue, the session aims to move debates beyond celebratory narratives of digital democratisation and toward a more grounded understanding of how digital inclusion and exclusion are actively negotiated in practice. Contributions may engage empirical, theoretical, or methodological approaches that highlight both the structural conditions producing digital inequalities and the everyday strategies (Burgess, 2006) through which individuals and communities navigate, resist, or reconfigure them in diverse contexts.

paper.title: Asymmetries and inequalities in a labor market with increased digital remote working
Just urban logistics: Tracing the spaces for resisting platform capitalism
Unequal Exchange: Platform Power, Serious Games, and Digital Inequalities in Ghana
Digital emancipation?: Examining the politics of datafied inclusion and the constitutive authority of networked governance

schedule_event.title: Food Geographies Research Group Annual General Meeting (AGM)
HYBRID - Hormonal Geographies: Tracing the Endocrine Through the Anthropocene (2)
schedule_event.description:
Session Aim: This session seeks to bring together research on hormones and endocrine-disrupting chemicals to explore how understanding the endocrine system as co-modulated by both internal and environmental factors can inform responses to contemporary forms of hormonal governance and (dis)regulation.

In recent years, hormones have emerged as an important theme in the social sciences and humanities for their role in shaping health, reproduction, and gender, with particular attention to the ways hormones are sensed and negotiated in everyday life. Work on contraceptives, fertility, menopause, pregnancy, the uterus, polycystic ovary syndrome, and low testosterone in cismen, alongside critiques of hormonal gatekeeping in trans* communities and non-consensual hormonal interventions on people with intersex conditions, has shown how hormones function as tools of biopolitical governance, enforcing heterosexual reproductive futurity while pathologizing deviations from binary sex and gender norms (Appleton, 2022; Berg and Linander, 2024; Erikainen et al., 2025; Joanes, 2023; Wang, 2024).

Meanwhile, attention across multiple research strands has been growing to the consequences of exposure to 'endocrine-disrupting chemicals' - substances largely originating from industrial sources that interfere with the body's hormonal system. As many of these compounds are xenoestrogens (synthetic estrogens), queer and trans* scholars have explored how such exposures might 'queer' sexed and gendered embodiments (Ah-King and Hayward, 2014; Di Chiro, 2010; Zabalegui, 2024), while environmental justice perspectives have highlighted the unequal health burdens of toxicity among those communities living near industrial sites (Shadaan and Murphy, 2020).

This session seeks to bring research on hormones and endocrine disruption into conversation to highlight how they co-modulate the endocrine system, both through biochemical processes and as biopolitical regimes that manage exposure, regulate populations, and produce uneven conditions of harm and resistance. This convergence underscores urgent political stakes: in the Anthropocene, industrial, ecological, and socio-political transformations have created conditions where bodies are increasingly disciplined through biomedical intervention, access to gender-affirming care is restricted, while atmospheric exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals remains largely mitigated. At this critical moment, this session brings together diverse perspectives on hormones and endocrine disrupting chemicals to explore pathways towards more just and sustainable endocrine futures.

We invite theoretical, empirical, methodological, and ethical contributions focusing on hormones, endocrine disruption, and the endocrine system from feminist, queer, and trans perspectives, as well as from health geography and environmental justice approaches.

Potential topics may include, but are not limited to:

  • The medicalisation and regulation of hormones
  • Gendered and life-course experiences of hormones
  • Hormonal health inequalities and endocrine health
  • Queer and Trans* hormonal experiences and practices
  • Fertility, reproduction, and contraception
  • Embodied and sensory experiences of hormones
  • Sexuality and sexual practices
  • Endocrine-disrupting chemicals
  • Environmental justice approaches to hormonally active pollution

paper.title: Hormones as a Biopolitical Governance Tool: A lived mid-2020s trans experience
Depletion, deficit and hormonal possibilities: A feminist geography of menopause
Endocrine landscapes: Chemical mattering of body imaginaries in trans and queer artistic work

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Social Infrastructure and the Making of Just Places (3)
schedule_event.description:
This proposed session marks the third consecutive year in which we have advanced a conference theme focused on social infrastructure, reflecting a sustained and growing commitment within geographical scholarship to understanding its central role in addressing social and spatial inequalities. Responding directly to the RGS 2026 conference theme, Geographies of inequalities: towards just places, the session examines how social infrastructure shapes, sustains, and challenges spatial inequalities, and how it contributes to the making of more just places. Social infrastructure, traditionally understood as the spaces, services, institutions, and networks that support social life, plays a crucial role in structuring access to care, education, health, participation, safety, and belonging (Klinenberg, 2018; Latham & Layton, 2019). Its distribution, governance, and everyday use are increasingly uneven, reflecting and reinforcing broader inequalities related to class, race, gender, age, disability, migration status, and place (Marmot, 2015; Landman, 2019; Sezer & Van Melik, 2023). These inequalities are often intensified in contexts of crisis, including pandemics, climate change, austerity, and displacement, where social infrastructure can act both as a buffer against harm and as a site where exclusions are reproduced (Renner et al., 2024).

At the same time, social infrastructure can function as a critical arena through which claims for social justice are articulated, negotiated, and contested, and through which alternative, more inclusive futures are imagined (Aelbrecht & Stevens, 2019; Klinenberg, 2018). This session invites papers that critically explore how social infrastructure operates across different spatial contexts, including urban, rural, and digital settings and at multiple scales, from the everyday to the institutional and policy level.

Aligned with the Participatory Geographies Research Group, the session welcomes theoretical, empirical, and methodological contributions that engage reflexively with questions of power, ethics, representation, and responsibility in researching social infrastructure and justice (Pain, 2004; Kindon, Pain & Kesby, 2007). We encourage interdisciplinary perspectives and global case studies that illuminate both the potentials and limits of social infrastructure in advancing social change within and beyond the academy.

We invite contributions that explore (but are not limited to) the following themes:

  • Social infrastructure as a foundation of spatial justice
  • Inequalities in provision, access, and use of social infrastructure
  • Social infrastructure and lived experience
  • Power relations, governance, and institutional arrangements
  • Social infrastructure in times of crisis and transition
  • urban, suburban, rural, coastal, and peripheral settings, and at neighbourhood, city-regional, national, and transnational scales.
  • Digital and hybrid social infrastructures
  • Measuring, mapping and representing inequality
  • Ethics, reflexivity, and responsibility in research and practice

This session encourages global perspectives and welcomes interdisciplinary contributions combining theoretical insights, empirical research, and case studies. Papers engaging at the intersection of social and cultural geographies and built environment disciplines, including architecture, landscape architecture, planning, and urban design, are particularly encouraged.

References

Not included here.

paper.title: Whose City, Whose Innovation? Social Infrastructure and Spatial Justice in Rotterdam’s M4H district
From Elite Practice to Public Service? Ballet and public Dance Conservatories as Social Infrastructure in the Parisian Suburbs
Hybrid Social Infrastructure and Spatial Justice: Managing Seasonal Religious Urbanization in Historic Pilgrimage Cities
Narratives of deprivation and aspiration: discursive place-making in the political economies of social infrastructure

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Are the State and Market Still Relevant for the Urban Poor? Rethinking Housing in Contemporary Cities
schedule_event.description:
A sizeable share of the population in many cities in both the Global South and the North lives without proper shelter. The casual and market-oriented housing policies in many cities/countries have failed to ensure affordable housing for low-income groups forcing them to live in a sub-human environment. The enormous flow of capital into cities' real estate in recent years has not produced housing for all. In fact, market reforms and rising real estate prices have further increased the vulnerability of marginalised sections to housing. In many cities, prices have appreciated several times in recent years, while workers' real wages have grown slowly during the same period. Many cities have also experienced rising costs for other essential commodities and services. This indicates that income and affordability are not keeping pace with the rise in real estate prices. It can now be argued whether the State and the Market remain relevant for poorer sections of society in cities with respect to housing.

This session invites papers based on empirical case studies from both the Global South and the Global North, as well as conceptual contributions on how housing provisions and markets operate within cities and how marginalised groups negotiate with the state and the market regarding their shelter. In other words, the papers should foreground the urban realities and should address the following issues though not limited to, (i) How are the urban housing questions addressed by State and Market and what are chasm that are created between income of marginalised sections and market prices in the housing sector, (ii) How have been the growth of houseless population in cities and what are their coping mechanism, (iii) How do urban planning and policy frameworks (like smart city) shape the urban housing question and the location of the housing in the cities? (iv) How does the aestheticisation and infringement of citizenship rights/legalities lead to demolitions, displacements, gentrification, and socio-spatial exclusions of the marginalised sections? (v) What forms of alternative policies and planning can help in socio-economically just and inclusive urban housing in cities?

Papers focusing on metropolitan cities are especially encouraged.

paper.title: Housing Ownership in India: Patterns and Regional Variation
Housing in Mumbai: Situation of the Poor between the State Apathy and Irrelevance of the Market

paper_authors.identity_string: Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Mumbai

paper.title: Performative Compliance and Human Buffers: Negotiating the State and Market Chasm in Majuqiao, Beijing
Beyond the State and the Market: Social Enterprise Responses to the Urban Unhoused in Mumbai and the Global South

schedule_event.title: HYBRID - Geography in the World: Toward Latin American Dialogues
schedule_event.description:
This roundtable engages with Geography in the World Part 4, a themed intervention from Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers (with IBG) that seeks to deepen dialogue with geographical knowledge produced beyond the Anglophone core—specifically in Latin America—while being frank about the practical and institutional obstacles that shape such encounters. The session is designed as a collective conversation about what it takes to move beyond “citational inclusion” toward sustained practices of reading, translation (linguistic and epistemic), and mutual recognition.

We begin from a shared diagnosis: Latin American geographical scholarship remains structurally under-visible in many “international” publishing circuits, even as evaluation regimes within and beyond the region increasingly reward publication in English-language, commercial-indexed venues. These pressures intensify inequalities, distort research agendas, and reproduce the coloniality of knowledge production. At the same time, Latin America offers distinctive lessons—through its cooperative open-access infrastructures, its debates on scientific sovereignty, and its long traditions of politically engaged geography—about how disciplinary worlds might be reshaped.

The roundtable will discuss practical strategies and collective responsibilities: editorial policies, peer review practices, translation infrastructures, mentoring and writing workshops, cross-journal collaborations, and the limits imposed by publisher logics and national assessment systems. The aim is to generate actionable ideas for building more plural, dialogical, and ethically grounded circuits of geographical knowledge—within and beyond Latin America—under the pressures of the current conjuncture.

Core questions to frame the discussion

  • What would it mean to reshape (not just “internationalise”) the spaces where geography is validated and circulated?
  • Which interventions are institutional (journals, societies, universities) vs individual (authors, reviewers), and how do we rebalance the burden?
  • How can we build translation practices that are ethical, funded, and durable, rather than ad hoc and extractive?
  • What can Anglophone geographers learn from Latin American infrastructures (SciELO/Redalyc/Latindex; open access traditions) without romanticising them?

schedule_event.title: IN PERSON - Meet the Editors of the Journal of Transport Geography
schedule_event.description: This roundtable offers researchers insight into publishing in the Journal of Transport Geography. Members of the journal’s editorial team will outline the journal’s aims and scope, articulate its vision and discuss the types and formats of papers it publishes. Editors will also explain how the review process works and how authors can position their work for successful evaluation. The roundtable aims to support scholars in strengthening their publishing strategies. Open to researchers at all career stages and across all areas of geography.

schedule_event.title: ONLINE - Counting and Tracing Inequality
schedule_event.description:
Inequalities are not only social and economic realities but distinctly spatial phenomena embedded in territories, infrastructures, and lived environments. Quantitative methods including spatial statistics, GIS-based modeling, inequality indices, accessibility analysis, regression techniques, and geospatial big data analytics enable researchers to move beyond descriptive narratives toward measurable, comparable, and policy-relevant insights. By quantifying disparities in access to services, environmental exposure, mobility, health outcomes, and socio-economic opportunity, these approaches help reveal hidden patterns of exclusion and concentration of advantage across scales. This session critically engage with the core question how innovative quantitative spatial analysis can support justice-oriented planning and decision-making process. It emphasizes methodological rigor alongside ethical and inclusive interpretation, ensuring that numerical models are socially grounded and context-sensitive. Contributors are invited to present empirical case studies, methodological innovations, and applied research that use quantitative tools to diagnose Spatial Inequality, evaluate interventions, and guide equitable resource allocation across urban, rural, and regional contexts. Work integrating GIS with statistical application, network analysis, composite index construction, and spatial decision-support systems is particularly welcome.

The session welcomes contributions from the post graduate students particularly and also researchers addressing Urban–Rural Divide, Uneven Development Patterns, Participatory Mapping and Community Spatial Knowledge towards equitable planning, Hazard and risk mapping. The expected outcome is to build a stronger bridge for integrating Qualitative Knowledge also includes GIS approach with Spatial Modeling and measurement and justice where quantitative evidence does not merely map disparities but actively informs strategies for creating more inclusive, balanced, and just places.

paper.title: Constraints on Uniform Access to Groundwater Resources: Insights from a Newly Emerged Industrial Site in Chhattisgarh, India
Transport Corridors and Uneven Access: Roadside Markets, Economic Disparity and Gendered Exclusion in Rural Bihar, India
Spatial Inequality in Human Development Status: A Block Level Study of Purulia District, West Bengal

schedule_event.title: ONLINE - Independent Geographers:  Between the Centre and the Periphery of Geographical Knowledge
schedule_event.description:
This session is an effort to establish a network for independent geographers, aiming to provide support and highlight issues such as recognition by the broader academic community and access to financial and social support mechanisms. It aligns with ongoing initiatives to foster inclusivity within academia and at the RGS, particularly through efforts focused on equality, diversity, and inclusion, as well as the advancement of decolonial, multi-, and interdisciplinary knowledge production.Despite these efforts, independent scholarship remains frequently overlooked, in part due to ambiguities around its definition and the absence of both formal and informal support structures for independent scholars. “Independent scholar” has become an umbrella term encompassing a wide range of meanings, referring to those working under flexible or precarious conditions. The term “independent scholar” usually refers to a scholar without a permanent job, which also implies a lack of a regular wage and institutional affiliation, and as a result, a lack of recognition for their work.Currently, academia experiences an unprecedented crisis due to various challenges, such as a declining number of permanent jobs, increasing redundancies among staff, departmental closures or mergers, increasing socio-political pressure, and the rise of new technologies, which particularly affect younger academics who work under precarious conditions.Independent scholarship is also expected to receive more attention due to changes in how academic knowledge is produced and disseminated through more flexible ways, both in academic and non-academic, and institutional and non-institutional settings. This involves using more inclusive research designs and methods to actively incorporate communities and people in research projects. There has also been a recent interest in academia to open it up to broader society through initiatives such as open-access or alternative forms of publication.In this context, the number of independent scholars and geographers who meet this broad definition is expected to increase. This session is open to all geographers who believe they fit within this framework and aims to bring them together to discuss issues, including but not limited to:

  1. A lack of recognition and the inability to get credentials from the wider academic community
  2. A lack of access to databases, journal archives, or information only accessible through institutional email and membership
  3. Difficulty of having access to funding sources for travel or research
  4. The independence of academic knowledge and whether independent academic knowledge can be produced outside academia.

If you are interested in presenting your papers/ideas in the session, please send your abstract of no more than 250 words and your information (name, surname, email address, and institutional affiliation -if any-) by 20 February 2026 to Basak Tanulku at tanulkub@gmail.com. The session is planned to be online, and if there is sufficient interest, the organiser will apply for a hybrid format.

Non-representational Scholarship on the Periphery
